Backbone for diffusion-style image generators and any text-conditioned vision system
Foundation for understanding modern multimodal architectures
not_ideal_for: Fine-grained visual reasoning (modern VLMs like Molmo, Pixtral, Qwen-VL outperform)
Generation tasks — CLIP is encoder-only by design

public_view.plain_english:
A model trained on 400 million image-caption pairs from the web that
learned to match images to their descriptions. The breakthrough: it
could classify images it had never been trained to classify, just by
being told the candidate labels in plain English ("a photo of a
cat" vs "a photo of a dog").
public_view.analogy:
Earlier image classifiers were like multiple-choice tests where you
had to pre-define every possible answer. CLIP is open-ended — you
can describe new categories at inference time and it slots images
into them.

applications.product: Image search across all major catalogs

public_view.investment_angle:
The cornerstone technique that made text-to-image generation possible
at quality. Every text-to-image system in 2022–2024 (DALL·E,
Midjourney, Stable Diffusion, Imagen) uses CLIP or a CLIP-derivative
for text-image alignment. Spawned the multimodal AI investment wave
(~$10B+ in 2023 alone).
public_view.why_it_matters:
Before CLIP, "AI" was a separate model per modality. After CLIP, the
field accepted that vision and language could share a single
representation space — which is what makes Claude, GPT-4o, and
Gemini "see" images natively today.

citations.title: Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision

nodes.body_md:
## How it changed downstream architectures

| Year | Model | CLIP role |
|---|---|---|
| 2021 | DALL·E 1 | not used (discrete VAE + transformer) |
| 2022-04 | DALL·E 2 | CLIP text & image embeddings drive diffusion prior |
| 2022-08 | Stable Diffusion | CLIP-L text encoder feeds U-Net cross-attention |
| 2023-07 | SDXL | OpenCLIP-G + CLIP-L dual text encoders |
| 2024-05 | GPT-4o | natively multimodal (CLIP-style pretraining at much larger scale) |

CLIP's scale (400M pairs) was the ceiling-breaker; the architecture was conceptually simple. The data pipeline was the moat.

nodes.title: AlphaFold 2 — Protein Structure Prediction Solved

model_spec.architecture: Evoformer (transformer-style attention on MSA + pair representations) + structure module + iterative refinement

feels_like: Solved a 50-year grand challenge in biology — protein structure prediction at experimental accuracy
CASP14 median GDT score 92.4 vs prior best ~62 — the kind of step-change that ends a research subfield
Predicted 200M+ protein structures in AlphaFold DB by 2022 — 1000x more than all experimental data combined
Demis Hassabis and John Jumper won the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry — first AI work to do so
Open-source code + structure database used by 2M+ researchers worldwide
AlphaFold 3 (2024) extended this to protein-ligand and protein-DNA complexes
best_for: Drug discovery and target validation pipelines
Structural biology research where experimental crystallography is too slow / expensive
Vaccine and antibody design starting from sequence
Teaching how transformer-style attention generalises beyond language
not_ideal_for: Membrane proteins and intrinsically disordered regions (still imperfect)
Direct use as a chemistry / docking model (AlphaFold 3 is more relevant for that)

public_view.plain_english:
DeepMind's protein structure prediction system that, in late 2020,
solved a 50-year-old grand challenge in biology: given a protein's
amino acid sequence, predict its 3D folded shape. The 2021 paper
+ open-source release made these predictions free for any
researcher worldwide. Within 3 years, the AlphaFold Database
contained predicted structures for nearly all 200 million known
proteins.
public_view.analogy:
Imagine being given a string of beads and having to predict the
shape of the origami it would fold into — a problem that had
taken biologists weeks-to-years per protein with X-ray
crystallography. AlphaFold did it in seconds, at human-experimental
accuracy, for any protein.
public_view.investment_angle:
AlphaFold is the canonical example of "AI as scientific
instrument" — comparable in impact to the electron microscope
or DNA sequencing. The downstream economic impact is hard to
quantify but vast: faster drug discovery (every major pharma
now uses AlphaFold predictions in early-stage research),
cheaper academic biology, new biotech company formation
(~$5B+ in AI-biotech funding 2021-2024 with explicit AlphaFold
foundations). DeepMind's biotech spinout Isomorphic Labs
raised >$600M by 2024 on the AlphaFold platform. Demis
Hassabis (DeepMind CEO) and John Jumper (AlphaFold lead)
shared the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for the work.
public_view.why_it_matters:
AlphaFold proved that AI could not just match but transform
a foundational scientific discipline. The Nobel Prize award
in 2024 made the recognition official: this is a Nobel-class
contribution to science, not just to AI.

citations.title: Highly accurate protein structure prediction with AlphaFold

nodes.body_md:
## CASP14 — the moment biology accepted AI

The Critical Assessment of Structure Prediction (CASP) is a biennial blind benchmark where computational methods predict structures of proteins whose true structures are known but unpublished. In 2020 (CASP14), AlphaFold 2 scored a median GDT (Global Distance Test) of 92.4 — comparable to experimental methods. The next-best entry scored ~62. The structural biology community publicly accepted the problem as "essentially solved."

| CASP year | AlphaFold | Next-best | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2018 (CASP13) | AlphaFold 1: 58 | 52 | early signal |
| 2020 (CASP14) | AlphaFold 2: 92.4 | 62 | step change; problem declared solved |
| 2022 (CASP15) | AlphaFold 2 still leading | various near | refinements |
| 2024 | (AlphaFold 3 era) | various | broader scope: ligand binding |

## The AlphaFold Database

After publishing the model weights and code, DeepMind partnered with EMBL-EBI to compute structures for the entire UniProt database. By 2024, ~200M predicted structures were public — vs the ~200k experimentally-determined structures accumulated over 60 years of structural biology.

This database alone is now cited in tens of thousands of biology papers per year. The comparison to "before" is not "slower experimental methods" — most of these proteins simply had no known structure at all before AlphaFold. AI didn't speed up an existing process; it created a new dataset that didn't previously exist.

## AlphaFold 3 (2024)

Extends to predicting structures of proteins + DNA + RNA + ligands together — i.e., complexes relevant for drug binding. Released as Isomorphic Labs' AlphaFold Server (closed access) rather than open-source, which generated significant scientific community discussion about precedents for openness in AI-biology collaborations.

nodes.title: InstructGPT — Training LMs to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback

model_spec.architecture: GPT-3 + supervised fine-tuning + reward model + PPO RL fine-tuning

benchmarks.name: Human-rater preference vs GPT-3 175B
benchmarks.vs_baseline: smaller, more aligned model > larger, raw model

feels_like: Introduced RLHF (reinforcement learning from human feedback) at scale — recipe behind ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini
1.3B InstructGPT preferred over 175B GPT-3 on user prompts — alignment beat scale
Three-step training: SFT on demos, reward model from comparisons, PPO against reward — still the standard template
Direct precursor of ChatGPT (Nov 2022): same recipe with more conversational tuning
TruthfulQA improvement of +10 to +25 points over base GPT-3 — measurable hallucination reduction
Most cited alignment paper of the GPT era; sparked Constitutional AI and DPO follow-ups
best_for: Foundational reading on RLHF and modern instruction-tuning
Citation in any work on alignment, preference modelling, or reward modelling
Understanding why ChatGPT 'feels different' from raw GPT-3
not_ideal_for: Direct production use — long superseded by ChatGPT and successors
Self-hosted / open-weights workflows (closed API only)

sources.name: OpenAI — Aligning language models to follow instructions

public_view.plain_english:
The training recipe — supervised fine-tuning followed by reinforcement
learning from human feedback — that turned GPT-3 from a raw text
completer into a model that actually follows instructions. Human
raters preferred the 1.3B InstructGPT model over the 175B raw GPT-3
model, suggesting that alignment can matter more than scale for
user-facing tasks.
public_view.analogy:
Take a brilliant generalist who blurts out everything they know in
every direction, and put them through a year of customer-service
training. Same brain, vastly more useful.
public_view.investment_angle:
The most operationally important post-training paper of the LLM era.
Established RLHF as the default alignment recipe, which every major
model company (Anthropic, Google, Meta, Mistral) then adopted in
some form. Created a new technical specialty (data annotation +
preference modeling) and the labor market for it (Scale AI's revenue
growth from ~$60M to ~$700M between 2021-2024).
public_view.why_it_matters:
InstructGPT, not GPT-3, is what people interact with when they use a
"language model" today. The recipe in this paper is why the chat
box on chat.openai.com is helpful instead of a chaotic autocomplete.

citations.title: Training Language Models to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback

nodes.body_md:
## The "1.3B beats 175B" headline

The most-cited result: human raters preferred outputs from the 1.3B InstructGPT model over the 175B GPT-3 base 85% of the time on the API prompt distribution. Two implications:

1. Alignment ≠ scale. Smaller, well-aligned models can dominate larger raw models on user-facing tasks.
2. RLHF is not free — it modestly hurts some benchmark scores while massively improving practical helpfulness. This trade was worth making.

## What this paper birthed

| Successor | Date | Inheritance |
|---|---|---|
| ChatGPT | 2022-11 | Same recipe + chat UI |
| Anthropic Constitutional AI | 2022-12 | RLAIF variant — replaces human raters with AI critique |
| Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) | 2023-05 | Skips the explicit reward model; same data, simpler training |
| Llama 2 Chat | 2023-07 | Open-source replication of the recipe |
| RLHF "as a service" companies | 2022-23 | Scale AI, Surge AI, Toloka — annotation as moat |

nodes.title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting (CoT)

feels_like: Language model from Google — see the linked sources below for benchmark and review coverage
best_for: General-purpose tasks within Google's deployment footprint
See the model spec and sources block for benchmarked use cases
not_ideal_for: Tasks far outside the modalities listed in this model's spec
Workflows where a more recent successor in the same family scores higher

public_view.plain_english:
A prompting technique that simply asks the model to "show its
work" — generate intermediate reasoning steps before the final
answer — and produces large accuracy gains on math and reasoning
problems. Free, requires no model retraining, and works only on
sufficiently large models (~60B+ parameters).
public_view.analogy:
The difference between asking a student "what's 27 × 49?" and
asking them to write out the multiplication. Same brain, same
knowledge — vastly better answer when given room to reason.

applications.product: Default behavior in every modern chat LLM
Foundational technique behind o1, R1, Claude reasoning

public_view.investment_angle:
CoT is the simplest, most-cited prompting technique in AI history
and the conceptual root of inference-time scaling. The o1/R1
"reasoning model" wave starting late 2024 — which created an
entire new monetization tier ($200/mo ChatGPT Pro, $100/mo Claude
Max) — is essentially "what if we trained the model to do CoT
automatically and let it think for longer." Without this 2022
paper, the 2024-26 reasoning-model market doesn't exist.
public_view.why_it_matters:
CoT is the discovery that LLM capability isn't just a function of
parameters and data — it's also a function of how you elicit the
model's computation at inference time. This three-axis view
(params × data × inference compute) is now the standard frame for
LLM capability planning.

citations.title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
Large Language Models are Zero-Shot Reasoners

nodes.body_md:
## Why this matters mechanically

The transformer's forward pass has bounded compute per token. Multi-step reasoning that doesn't fit in one forward pass simply can't be done in one shot. CoT works because it lets the model spread reasoning across many tokens, each of which gets its own forward pass.

Implication: CoT is a way of buying *more compute per query* by emitting more tokens. This insight — which the Wei et al. paper noted but didn't dwell on — became the foundation for o1 / R1 reasoning models two and a half years later.

## What CoT enables (and doesn't)

- **Helps**: math, code, multi-step planning, common-sense reasoning chains, tasks where intermediate steps are checkable.
- **Doesn't help (much)**: factual recall, simple translation, single-step classification, anything where the answer is "in the model" without computation.
- **Backfires**: high-stakes single-token answers where the chain introduces errors, or trivial tasks where the chain adds noise.

## CoT's three children

1. **Self-consistency** (Wang et al., 2022) — sample multiple CoT chains, take majority vote of final answers. Free accuracy gain at the cost of N× inference compute.
2. **Tree-of-Thought** (Yao et al., 2023) — explore CoT branches as a search tree with pruning.
3. **Reasoning models** (o1, 2024-09; R1, 2025-01) — train the model to do internal CoT automatically, optionally invisibly. The descendants where this technique becomes a product category.

nodes.title: Chinchilla — Compute-Optimal LLM Training

model_spec.architecture: Decoder-only transformer, same architecture as Gopher
model_spec.training_compute: ~same FLOPs as Gopher — reallocated from params to data

feels_like: Language model from DeepMind — see the linked sources below for benchmark and review coverage
best_for: General-purpose tasks within DeepMind's deployment footprint
See the model spec and sources block for benchmarked use cases
not_ideal_for: Tasks far outside the modalities listed in this model's spec
Workflows where a more recent successor in the same family scores higher

public_view.plain_english:
A DeepMind study that showed — contrary to two years of received
wisdom — that most existing large language models were under-trained.
A 70B model trained on 4× more data outperformed a 280B model
trained on the same compute. Everyone had been making models too
large relative to their data budget.
public_view.analogy:
Imagine assuming that the way to bake better bread is just a bigger
oven. Chinchilla showed the flour-to-oven ratio had been off all
along — more flour in the same oven bakes better bread.

applications.product: Frontier training runs at every major lab post-2022

public_view.investment_angle:
Chinchilla shifted tens of billions of dollars in training spend
from "buy more GPUs for bigger models" toward "buy more GPUs for
longer training runs on more data." This revalued proprietary
training data (which became the new bottleneck) and triggered the
2023-25 surge in data-licensing deals — Reddit/Google, AP/OpenAI,
Shutterstock/OpenAI, newspaper lawsuits, and synthetic-data
startups. Also structurally favored open-weight models (Llama)
which could follow Chinchilla-optimal sizing while closed models
had sunk cost in under-trained giants.
public_view.why_it_matters:
If the model on your phone is smaller and yet just as capable as
GPT-3 was five years ago, you are benefiting from Chinchilla. Every
efficient small model (phi, Mistral 7B, Gemma) is a Chinchilla
descendant.

nodes.body_md:
## The under-training table

| Model | Params | Tokens | Tokens / Param | Chinchilla-optimal? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-3 | 175B | 300B | 1.7× | drastically under-trained |
| Gopher | 280B | 300B | 1.1× | drastically under-trained |
| Megatron-Turing NLG | 530B | 270B | 0.5× | extremely under-trained |
| **Chinchilla** | **70B** | **1.4T** | **20×** | **optimal** |
| Llama 1 65B | 65B | 1.4T | 22× | ~optimal |
| Llama 2 70B | 70B | 2.0T | 29× | over-trained (deliberately, for inference efficiency) |
| Llama 3 70B | 70B | 15T | 215× | vastly over-trained (inference efficiency dominates) |

Modern models deliberately over-train beyond Chinchilla-optimal because training cost is a one-time expense while inference cost compounds forever.

## Why the paper lands in the tree, not as a footnote

Chinchilla didn't just correct a scaling exponent. It reshaped how the industry allocates AI capital. The observation "data is the bottleneck, not parameters" directly triggered the 2023-24 push toward proprietary data licensing, synthetic data generation, and the legal fights (NYT v OpenAI, Getty v Stability) that are still unresolved as of 2026.

nodes.title: PaLM — Pathways Language Model (540B)

feels_like: Language model from Google — see the linked sources below for benchmark and review coverage
best_for: General-purpose tasks within Google's deployment footprint
See the model spec and sources block for benchmarked use cases
not_ideal_for: Tasks far outside the modalities listed in this model's spec
Workflows where a more recent successor in the same family scores higher

public_view.plain_english:
Google's 540-billion-parameter language model, three times larger
than GPT-3 and trained on Google's TPU v4 supercomputer. It set
state-of-the-art on dozens of benchmarks but was never released as
a product, becoming the largest AI model ever to live and die
entirely as a research artifact.
public_view.analogy:
A concept car at an auto show — built to prove what was possible,
never sold. Google had the technical capability before OpenAI's
consumer launch but missed the product moment by 7 months.

applications.product: PaLM API (deprecated 2024 in favor of Gemini)

public_view.investment_angle:
PaLM is Exhibit A in the case that "having the technology" and
"having the product" are different problems. Google demonstrably
had a more capable model than ChatGPT seven months before ChatGPT
launched — and lost the consumer narrative anyway. That gap led
directly to Google declaring "code red" in December 2022 and the
aggressive Gemini launch the following year. Microsoft + Anthropic
market caps appreciated by hundreds of billions during the gap.
public_view.why_it_matters:
PaLM is the cautionary tale every Big Tech AI strategy now studies.
Capability without distribution is not a competitive moat. The
industry's current obsession with shipping cadence over technical
novelty traces directly to this miss.

citations.title: PaLM: Scaling Language Modeling with Pathways

nodes.body_md:
## How it compares to peers in 2022

| Model | Date | Org | Params | Train tokens | MMLU | Released? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-3 | 2020-05 | OpenAI | 175B | 300B | ~44% | API |
| Gopher | 2021-12 | DeepMind | 280B | 300B | 60.0% | no |
| Megatron-Turing | 2022-01 | MS+Nvidia | 530B | 270B | ~50% | no |
| Chinchilla | 2022-03 | DeepMind | 70B | 1.4T | 67.5% | no |
| **PaLM** | **2022-04** | **Google** | **540B** | **780B** | **69.3%** | **API only, late 2023** |
| GPT-4 | 2023-03 | OpenAI | undisclosed | undisclosed | 86.4% | API + product |

PaLM was state of the art for ~6 months. Then the world stopped caring about benchmarks because ChatGPT shipped to consumers.

## What PaLM contributed technically

- **Multi-query attention** — share K and V projections across attention heads. Reduces inference KV-cache memory by ~10× with minimal quality loss. Universally adopted (GQA in Llama 2, GPT-4, Claude).
- **SwiGLU** — gated linear unit activation. Mild quality lift; standard in modern LLMs.
- **Pathways infrastructure** — the cross-pod orchestration layer that made 6000-chip jobs tractable. Internal Google asset, never open-sourced.

The architectural lessons survive in Gemini and beyond; the PaLM brand was retired in late 2024.

nodes.title: Stable Diffusion — Open-Weight Latent Diffusion for Images

model_spec.architecture: Latent diffusion (denoise in 64×64 latent space, not 512×512 pixels) + CLIP conditioning

benchmarks.name: Inference VRAM
benchmarks.vs_baseline: first open model runnable on consumer GPU

feels_like: First text-to-image model with DALL·E 2-class quality and permissive open weights
Latent diffusion innovation — denoising in compressed latent space, not pixel space — made consumer-GPU inference viable
Ran on <8GB VRAM at release — first generative model regular people could use locally
Spawned an enormous ecosystem (LoRAs, ControlNet, AUTOMATIC1111, ComfyUI) within months
Triggered the 'AI art' cultural moment of late 2022 along with Midjourney
Lower default photorealism than DALL·E 2 / Midjourney v3 — but customisability completely flipped the trade-off
best_for: Self-hosted image generation pipelines (privacy / volume / customisation)
Custom-style fine-tuning via LoRA / textual inversion / Dreambooth
ControlNet-style guided generation requiring weight access
Research and academic experimentation on diffusion models
not_ideal_for: Out-of-the-box photorealistic aesthetics — Midjourney still the default for that
Reliable in-image text rendering (FLUX.2 and later models leapfrogged here)
Production deployments without a community-finetuned LoRA / model card

sources.name: Stability AI — Stable Diffusion public release
BentoML — Best open-source image generation models

public_view.plain_english:
An image generator released with open weights and a permissive
license that, within weeks, ran on consumer graphics cards in
apartments around the world. It was the first generative AI model
with quality comparable to closed competitors (DALL·E 2, Midjourney)
that anyone could download, modify, and commercialize without
permission.
public_view.analogy:
The moment Linux became good enough to challenge Windows — but
compressed into three weeks. Within a month of release the community
had built dozens of variants, UIs, and fine-tuned models; within a
year an entire open creative-AI ecosystem had formed.
public_view.investment_angle:
Stable Diffusion created an entire open-source creative-AI economy
(Civitai hosts >50k fine-tunes). Stability AI's own commercial
trajectory was rocky (founder ouster, funding crises through 2024),
but the ecosystem around SD now generates billions in derivative
product revenue. The fundamental economics lesson: open-weight base
models can build large downstream markets even when the releasing
company struggles to monetize. Related investment theses: Adobe's
pivot to Firefly (~$3B new revenue 2023-24), the copyright lawsuits
(Getty v Stability) that shape the synthetic-media legal landscape,
and the data-moat thesis now driving all frontier labs.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Stable Diffusion is the image-gen equivalent of what Llama became
for text generation a year later. It proved that open-weight models
could ship at frontier quality — a precedent that now shapes every
conversation about LLM regulation, China competitiveness, and the
viability of closed-model moats.

citations.title: High-Resolution Image Synthesis with Latent Diffusion Models

nodes.body_md:
## How it compares to concurrent image generators

| Model | Date | Org | Approach | Weights | Quality (FID COCO) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| DALL·E 1 | 2021-01 | OpenAI | discrete VAE + autoregressive transformer | closed | — |
| GLIDE | 2021-12 | OpenAI | pixel-space diffusion | partial | 12.24 |
| DALL·E 2 | 2022-04 | OpenAI | CLIP + diffusion prior + upsampler | closed | 10.39 |
| Imagen | 2022-05 | Google | T5-XXL + cascaded pixel diffusion | closed | 7.27 |
| Midjourney v3 | 2022-07 | Midjourney | diffusion (details undisclosed) | closed | — |
| **Stable Diffusion 1.4** | **2022-08** | **Stability AI** | **latent diffusion + CLIP** | **open** | **12.63** |
| Stable Diffusion XL | 2023-07 | Stability AI | 2.6B params, better text encoder | open | ~8 |
| FLUX.1 | 2024-08 | Black Forest Labs | 12B rectified flow transformer | open (dev) | ~6 |

SD 1.4's quality was behind the closed competitors at launch — but the open weights + consumer-GPU tractability mattered more than the quality gap. By SDXL (2023-07) the gap had closed.

## What changed after release

- **Civitai** launched within 2 months — today hosts >100k community fine-tunes
- **Midjourney v4** quality leap (late 2022) was partly a response
- **ControlNet** (Feb 2023) added structured conditioning (pose, depth, edge maps) that closed models lacked
- **LoRA fine-tuning** popularized by SD — now standard across all generative AI
- **Getty Images v Stability AI** (2023) — still-unresolved copyright case that will shape generative AI law

nodes.title: Whisper — Open Multilingual Speech Recognition

model_spec.architecture: Encoder-decoder transformer; 80-channel mel-spectrogram input → token output

benchmarks.name: Word Error Rate (English, LibriSpeech test-clean)
benchmarks.vs_baseline: near-human; matches commercial systems trained on much more curated data

benchmarks.name: Multilingual coverage
benchmarks.vs_baseline: Google Speech-to-Text ~125 languages but commercial closed

benchmarks.name: Robustness (background noise, accents)
benchmarks.score: outperforms 14/15 prior systems on cross-domain test sets

feels_like: Trained on 680,000 hours of weakly-supervised audio — among the largest supervised speech datasets ever
55% fewer errors than fine-tuned ASR models on broad/diverse data — best zero-shot ASR at release
Robust to background noise: maintains performance below 10dB SNR where competitors fail
99 languages officially supported, but only 50 of 82 evaluated have <20% WER — multilingual quality varies wildly
Best on Romance languages, German, Japanese; weak on under-resourced languages
Hallucinations and proper-noun mistakes are the main failure modes — not perfect, but free
best_for: Self-hosted transcription replacing Google / AWS / Rev / Otter for many workloads
Podcast / video subtitle generation in 50+ languages
Multilingual call-center analytics where API privacy matters
Research baselines for ASR — open weights + paper made it the canonical citation
not_ideal_for: Real-time transcription at scale without aggressive optimisation (latency)
Under-resourced languages where WER is still >20%
Use cases needing speaker diarization out of the box (Whisper does ASR, not diarization)

public_view.plain_english:
OpenAI's open-source speech recognition model, released
September 2022 (two months before ChatGPT). Trained on 680,000
hours of web audio in 99 languages, it dramatically lowered
the bar for "transcribe audio in any language" applications.
The open release displaced commercial speech-to-text APIs
(Google, AWS Transcribe, Rev, Otter) for many use cases
overnight.
public_view.analogy:
Whisper did for speech recognition what Stable Diffusion did
for image generation a month earlier — released a
near-state-of-the-art model with open weights that anyone
could run on their own laptop, undercutting commercial APIs
in the process.

applications.product: Subtitle generation across podcast / YouTube tools

public_view.investment_angle:
Whisper killed the standalone speech-to-text API as a viable
business — Google, AWS, IBM, and others saw their speech
revenue lines compress as customers self-hosted Whisper or
used OpenAI's $0.006/minute API. Created entire new
categories of products: AI meeting assistants (~$2B funding
wave 2023-24), automated podcast / video transcription,
real-time multilingual conversation. Also became the input
layer for voice-first AI products (Apple Intelligence,
GPT-4o voice mode, Pi from Inflection).
public_view.why_it_matters:
Whisper is the speech-recognition layer underneath most
"AI listens to audio" products since 2023. Without it,
the meeting-AI category, the voice-mode-AI category, and
much of consumer-facing AI audio infrastructure would have
been gated by commercial-API economics rather than commodity
open-weights.

citations.title: Robust Speech Recognition via Large-Scale Weak Supervision

nodes.body_md:
## How Whisper compares to peers

| System | Year | Languages | Open? | LibriSpeech WER (clean) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Google Speech-to-Text | 2017+ | 125+ | API | ~3% |
| AWS Transcribe | 2017 | ~30 | API | ~5% |
| DeepSpeech 2 | 2015 | 2 (EN, ZH) | open | ~5% |
| wav2vec 2.0 | 2020 | EN+ | open | ~2.5% |
| **Whisper large** | **2022** | **99** | **open** | **2.5%** |
| Whisper large-v3 | 2023 | 99 | open | ~2% |

Whisper's combination of language coverage + open weights + reasonable on-laptop inference (medium variant runs faster than realtime on a Mac) was unique at release.

## Lineage

| Date | Variant | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 2022-09 | Whisper original | 5 sizes, 99 languages |
| 2023-12 | Whisper large-v3 | improved Mel-bins, +5 languages |
| 2024+ | community variants | Distil-Whisper (smaller, faster); Whisper-Streaming; etc. |
| 2025 | OpenAI gpt-4o-transcribe | newer multimodal speech successor |

nodes.title: ReAct — Reasoning + Acting in Language Models

benchmarks.name: HotpotQA (multi-hop QA)
benchmarks.vs_baseline: first prompting framework to combine reasoning + retrieval

feels_like: Language model from Google — see the linked sources below for benchmark and review coverage
Tool-use and agent loops are the typical fit per the published model card
best_for: Agent / tool-use workflows that match the model's published benchmarks
See the model spec and sources block for benchmarked use cases
not_ideal_for: Tasks far outside the modalities listed in this model's spec
Workflows where a more recent successor in the same family scores higher

public_view.plain_english:
A simple prompting framework that interleaves "thought" steps
with "action" steps (like calling a search engine or calculator)
in a single chain. Made it possible to build "agentic" LLM
workflows — give the model tools, let it decide which to use,
when, and based on what reasoning. Conceptual root of every
modern agent framework.
public_view.analogy:
Earlier LLMs were closed-book exam takers. ReAct turned them into
open-book exam takers — same brain, but with the option to
look something up, do a calculation, or run code, mid-thought.
public_view.investment_angle:
ReAct is the conceptual root of the entire agentic-AI investment
wave 2023-26. Every dollar invested in agent frameworks
(LangChain $200M+ raised, CrewAI, AutoGen) and agent products
(Devin $4B valuation, Cursor $9B, Replit Agent) traces methodologically
to this paper. The "tool-using LLM" architecture — model + system
prompt + tool schema + execution loop — is now standard plumbing
in every LLM API (OpenAI Responses, Anthropic Tool Use, Gemini
Function Calling).
public_view.why_it_matters:
The conceptual jump from "LLM as text generator" to "LLM as
decision-maker that uses tools" started here. Without this
framing, the 2024-26 transition from chatbots to agents
happens later or differently.

citations.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models

nodes.body_md:
## What ReAct combined that wasn't combined before

| Capability | Pre-ReAct | ReAct |
|---|---|---|
| LM reasoning | CoT (Wei 2022) | ✓ inherited |
| LM tool use | restricted to fine-tuned single-tool pipelines | ✓ general |
| Reasoning + tool use in one trajectory | ✗ | ✓ |
| Trace-based debugging | ✗ | ✓ (each thought visible) |

The contribution wasn't any single technical innovation — it was the integration into a single coherent trajectory that the model itself orchestrated.

## What ReAct birthed

| Successor | Date | Inheritance |
|---|---|---|
| LangChain agents | 2023-Q1 | productized the ReAct loop as a Python framework |
| OpenAI function calling | 2023-06 | API-native tool schema (formalizes the action format) |
| AutoGPT / BabyAGI | 2023-04 | autonomous multi-step ReAct with goal decomposition |
| ToolFormer (Meta) | 2023-02 | trains the model to insert API calls during pretraining |
| Anthropic tool use | 2024-04 | first-class tool-use API with structured streaming |
| MCP (Model Context Protocol) | 2024-11 | open protocol for tool / context server integration |
| Devin / Cursor / Claude Code | 2024-25 | productized agentic IDE / dev workflows |

Every modern agentic system inherits the core ReAct loop, even when not explicitly cited.

nodes.title: ChatGPT — RLHF-Aligned Conversational LLM

model_spec.architecture: Decoder-only transformer + RLHF post-training (PPO against reward model)

feels_like: Reached 1 million users in 5 days — fastest consumer tech adoption recorded at the time
First chat interface that made LLMs useful to non-technical users — the productisation moment
Built on InstructGPT (GPT-3.5 + RLHF), wrapped in dialogue tuning + system-prompt scaffolding
Triggered the entire AI-product wave — every major lab released a competing chatbot within 6 months
Free at launch (Nov 2022); ChatGPT Plus ($20/mo) followed Feb 2023; by 2024 ~200M WAU
Today's ChatGPT product surface is GPT-5 / GPT-4o under the hood — the brand outlived the original model
best_for: Casual chat, writing assistance, brainstorming, and general-purpose Q&A
Onboarding non-technical users to AI
Studying the productisation of AI as a tech-history case study
not_ideal_for: Discussions of the November 2022 model itself — the production model has changed many times
Self-hosted / regulated workloads (it's a hosted consumer / enterprise product)

sources.name: MIT Press — How is ChatGPT's behavior changing

public_view.plain_english:
A version of GPT-3.5 fine-tuned with human feedback so that it
follows instructions, holds a multi-turn conversation, and refuses
obviously harmful requests. Free public access through a chat
interface made it the fastest-growing consumer product in history,
crossing 100 million users in two months.
public_view.analogy:
GPT-3 was a brilliant but socially clueless savant who would
autocomplete anything. ChatGPT was the same savant after a year of
intense customer-service training — same raw capability, vastly more
usable.
public_view.investment_angle:
The product launch that converted "AI is impressive in demos" into
"AI is a category every public-market analyst now models." Microsoft's
$10B investment in OpenAI (Jan 2023), Nvidia's $1T → $3T market cap
expansion (2023–24), Anthropic's accelerated funding rounds, and the
creation of a ~$100B/yr commercial LLM market — all proximally
triggered by ChatGPT's launch traction. Moat shape: distribution +
brand + post-training know-how. Pricing model: $20/mo consumer,
seat-based enterprise, per-token API.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Even readers with no technical interest in AI now interact with
GPT-class systems weekly — through ChatGPT, Copilot, or one of
dozens of derivative products. ChatGPT is the moment AI stopped
being a research field and became a consumer category. Every
subsequent regulatory, geopolitical, labor-market, and education
debate about AI is downstream of this launch.

citations.title: Training Language Models to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback
Deep Reinforcement Learning from Human Preferences

nodes.body_md:
## How it works (training pipeline)

```
1. Pretraining          → next-token prediction on internet text (GPT-3.5 base)
2. Supervised fine-tune → human-written demonstrations of "correct" responses
3. Reward modeling      → train a separate model to predict human preference rankings
4. RL fine-tuning       → optimize the LM against the reward model using PPO
```

Step 4 is what makes the model "aligned" — its outputs are pushed toward what human raters preferred, away from what they downvoted. This is why ChatGPT sounds helpful and avoids most obvious harms, even though the underlying base model could equally well produce the opposite.

```python
# Conceptual sketch of the RLHF inner loop
for prompt in prompts:
    response = policy_model.generate(prompt)
    reward = reward_model.score(prompt, response)
    kl_penalty = kl_divergence(policy_model, reference_model)
    loss = -(reward - beta * kl_penalty)
    loss.backward(); ppo_step()
```

The KL penalty against a frozen reference model prevents the policy from drifting into incoherent text that happens to score well with the reward model — a critical detail.

## How to use it

End users: chat at `chat.openai.com` or via mobile apps. Pro tier ($20/mo) for capability; Enterprise for org features.

Developers: the same techniques are available across providers:

```python
import openai
r = openai.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o",
    messages=[{"role":"user","content":"Summarize the SEC 10-K of MSFT in 5 bullets."}]
)
```

Or, via Anthropic's Claude (a peer model trained with a related but distinct approach, Constitutional AI):

```python
import anthropic
r = anthropic.Anthropic().messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-7",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[{"role":"user","content":"Summarize the SEC 10-K of MSFT in 5 bullets."}]
)
```

## SWOT

- **Strengths** — Conversational UX is universally accessible (no API knowledge required). Instruction-following is reliable enough for production use. RLHF reduces (but doesn't eliminate) overtly harmful outputs. Created a defensible brand.
- **Weaknesses** — Hallucinations persist. Refusals are sometimes overcalibrated (false positives) or under-calibrated (jailbreakable). Reward-model exploitation: models learn to game raters' preferences (sycophancy, verbose answers).
- **Opportunities** — Tool use, multi-modal inputs (vision, audio), long-context retrieval, agentic workflows have all been added on top of the base RLHF recipe without redesigning the alignment pipeline.
- **Threats** — Open-weight models (Llama 3.1+, Mistral, Qwen) reach near-parity on many benchmarks at much lower cost. Constitutional AI and DPO-family methods may displace RLHF as the standard alignment recipe.

## Limitations

- **Sycophancy** — RLHF rewards what raters preferred, which often correlates with agreement, not truth.
- **Refusal calibration** — both over-refusal (refusing benign requests) and under-refusal (jailbreaks) remain hard.
- **Verbosity** — RLHF-trained models tend to over-explain because raters prefer thorough-looking answers.
- **No persistent memory by default** (added later as a feature, with privacy tradeoffs).
- **Reward hacking** — at sufficient scale, models find solutions that score well on the reward model but don't reflect actual preference.

## Why this is a paradigm node, not just a product

Three reasons ChatGPT belongs in the tree alongside Backprop and Transformer:

1. **It established RLHF as the production alignment recipe.** Every major LLM since (Claude, Gemini, Llama-Chat, Qwen-Chat) ships with some RLHF or RLHF-derivative post-training step.
2. **It proved consumer demand for LLM access at scale.** Pre-ChatGPT, "AI" was a B2B SaaS feature. Post-ChatGPT, it's a consumer category with ~$200/yr ARPU at the high end.
3. **It triggered the regulatory turn.** Every major AI policy initiative since 2023 — EU AI Act final form, US Executive Orders, UK AI Safety Institute, China's interim measures — was substantially accelerated by the political salience that followed ChatGPT's launch.

A research result that would have been a footnote without distribution became, with distribution, the inflection point of a decade.

nodes.title: Constitutional AI — Harmlessness from AI Feedback

benchmarks.name: Helpful + Harmless trade-off
benchmarks.vs_baseline: less harmful at no cost to helpfulness

feels_like: Language model from Anthropic — see the linked sources below for benchmark and review coverage

See the model spec and sources block for benchmarked use cases
not_ideal_for: Tasks far outside the modalities listed in this model's spec
Workflows where a more recent successor in the same family scores higher

public_view.plain_english:
Anthropic's alternative to RLHF: instead of using human raters to
rank responses, the model critiques and revises its own outputs
according to a written list of principles (a "constitution"). This
reduces the need for expensive human labeling and makes the
alignment process more transparent — anyone can read the
constitution.
public_view.analogy:
RLHF is hiring thousands of editors to rate every draft. Constitutional
AI is giving the writer a clear style guide and trusting them to
self-edit. Both produce cleaner output; the constitution is cheaper
to scale and easier to audit.

applications.product: Influenced Llama 3, Gemini, OpenAI safety stacks

public_view.investment_angle:
Constitutional AI is Anthropic's core technical differentiator and
a key piece of its $13.8B Amazon deal narrative. The "alignment
research lab" positioning — distinct from "model lab" — was bought
on the strength of this paper and the deployment of its principles
in Claude. Also influenced regulatory framing: the EU AI Act and US
executive orders on AI both incorporate language about "stated
principles" and "auditable safety processes" that maps onto the
constitutional approach.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Whether you trust Anthropic's safety story or not, this paper
reshaped the alignment vocabulary. Every frontier lab now publishes
some version of "model principles," "spec," or "policy" that traces
back to the constitutional framing introduced here.

citations.title: Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback

nodes.body_md:
## How it differs from InstructGPT/RLHF

| Dimension | InstructGPT (2022-01) | Constitutional AI (2022-12) |
|---|---|---|
| Preference source | human raters | model critique against text rules |
| Cost per data point | $0.20–$2 | ~free (compute only) |
| Auditability | preference data | preference data + constitution |
| Bottleneck | annotator hiring | reward-model + policy quality |
| Scalability | linear in $$$ | linear in compute |
| Adopter | OpenAI | Anthropic |

Both are now used together at all major labs — RLHF for some objectives, RLAIF / CAI for others.

## What's in Anthropic's actual constitution

Public-facing principles (excerpts):
- "Please choose the response that is most supportive and encouraging of life, liberty, and personal security."
- "Please choose the response that has the least objectionable, offensive, unlawful, deceptive, inaccurate, or harmful content."
- "Please choose the response that's least intended to build a relationship with the user."

The full constitution has been iteratively refined and is partially public. The opacity is itself a critique vector: "auditable" only goes so far if the operative version evolves with each model release.

nodes.title: LLaMA — Open Foundation Language Models from Meta

model_spec.architecture: Decoder-only transformer, RMSNorm, SwiGLU, RoPE, no biases

feels_like: Original Llama — historical; superseded by Llama 2+.
Major leap over Llama 2 — 70B operates in the same 'weight class' as GPT-4 and Claude 3 Sonnet at release
Climbed to 5th place on the LMSYS leaderboard within weeks — first open-weights model to genuinely compete with GPT-4
128K-token vocabulary tokenizer noticeably improved encoding efficiency vs Llama 2
best_for: Open-weights production deployments where Llama 2 was the previous baseline
Self-hosted RAG, code-completion, and chat applications under Llama 3 license
Fine-tuning on private corpora (the open license makes vertical specialisation safe)
not_ideal_for: Frontier reasoning leaderboards by late 2025 — superseded by Llama 3.1/3.3/4 and competitors
Multimodal tasks — text-only at this generation (Llama 3.2 Vision came later)

public_view.plain_english:
Meta's first publicly-released foundation language model family.
Originally distributed under a research-only license, the weights
leaked within a week and circulated freely. The 13B and 65B
variants matched or exceeded much larger closed competitors, kicking
off the open-source LLM ecosystem.
public_view.analogy:
The "leak" of Llama in March 2023 played the same role for LLMs
that Stable Diffusion's open release played for image generation
six months earlier — once frontier weights were public, the
community rebuilt the entire stack around them in weeks.
public_view.investment_angle:
Llama 1's leak was the single event that broke the closed-model
moat narrative. Within weeks, sub-$1k developer hardware could run
a model competitive with what OpenAI charged enterprise rates for.
Meta's strategic shift from "private model" to "open weights as
commodity layer" began here and culminated with Llama 3.1 405B
(2024). Triggered a wave of inference-optimization startups
(TogetherAI, Fireworks, Groq) and reshaped enterprise AI buyers'
expectations: open alternatives became table-stakes for vendor
leverage in OpenAI/Anthropic negotiations.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Llama is why the LLM market in 2026 has more than three serious
players. Without an open-weight alternative at frontier-comparable
quality, the LLM economy would be a duopoly with closed APIs and
closed pricing. Llama created the third option.

citations.title: LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models

nodes.body_md:
## How Llama 1 65B compares to peers

| Model | Date | Org | Params | MMLU | Released | License |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-3 | 2020-05 | OpenAI | 175B | 43.9% | API only | proprietary |
| Gopher | 2021-12 | DeepMind | 280B | 60.0% | no | proprietary |
| Chinchilla | 2022-03 | DeepMind | 70B | 67.5% | no | proprietary |
| PaLM | 2022-04 | Google | 540B | 69.3% | API only | proprietary |
| **Llama 1 65B** | **2023-02** | **Meta** | **65B** | **63.4%** | **weights (research)** | **research-only → leaked** |
| Llama 2 70B | 2023-07 | Meta | 70B | 68.9% | weights | commercial OK |
| Llama 3 70B | 2024-04 | Meta | 70B | 82.0% | weights | commercial OK |

Llama 1 hit a quality similar to Chinchilla at a smaller (and open) parameter count. The leak made it the first frontier-comparable model anyone could download and run locally.

## The leak and its consequences

Meta initially distributed Llama via a request form gated to vetted researchers. Within seven days, a torrent containing all four model sizes appeared on 4chan; within a month, it was on Hugging Face. Meta's response was telling: rather than legal action, they spent the next four months preparing Llama 2 — released with a permissive commercial license. The pivot from "leak crisis" to "open is the strategy" defined Meta's AI positioning for the next 3 years.

public_view.plain_english:
A 6-billion-parameter open-weight chat model from Tsinghua University,
released the same week as GPT-4. The first major open model
specifically tuned for Chinese — its release on Hugging Face within
days drew >1M downloads and seeded the Chinese open-source LLM
ecosystem (later joined by Qwen, Yi, DeepSeek, Baichuan).
public_view.analogy:
The "Chinese Llama 1" — same role of catalyzing a national
open-source ecosystem, with the same kind of week-one viral spread.
Released before Llama 1 itself in some respects.
public_view.investment_angle:
ChatGLM seeded Zhipu AI, which by 2024 had raised >$2B at >$3B
valuation as one of China's "AI tigers" (the four startups Beijing
backed: Zhipu, Baichuan, Moonshot, MiniMax). The release also
established the operating template Chinese AI labs would follow:
open-weight base model for ecosystem traction + closed flagship
API for revenue. By 2026, every major Chinese AI lab uses some
variant of this dual-track strategy.
public_view.why_it_matters:
ChatGLM is the moment Chinese AI labs stopped just translating Western
models and started shipping models tuned natively for Chinese language
and culture. The current Chinese AI ecosystem — Qwen, DeepSeek, Yi,
Kimi — exists because ChatGLM proved a market existed.

citations.title: GLM-130B: An Open Bilingual Pre-trained Model

nodes.body_md:
## How ChatGLM compared to peers (March 2023)

| Model | Date | Org | Params | Chinese | License |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-4 | 2023-03-14 | OpenAI | undisclosed | strong | API only |
| Claude 1 | 2023-03-14 | Anthropic | undisclosed | weaker | API only |
| **ChatGLM-6B** | **2023-03-14** | **Tsinghua** | **6.2B** | **native** | **research-only** |
| Llama 1 | 2023-02-24 | Meta | 7B-65B | weak | research-only |
| Bard | 2023-03-21 | Google | undisclosed | weak | free chat |

ChatGLM was the only open release in this group with native Chinese capability. The strategic positioning — bilingual + small + open — defined the Chinese open-LLM playbook.

## Lineage to GLM-4

The ChatGLM line evolved into:
- **ChatGLM2-6B** (Jun 2023) — 32k context, 12% better quality
- **ChatGLM3-6B** (Oct 2023) — function calling, code interpreter
- **GLM-4** (Jan 2024) — closed flagship, multimodal, claims GPT-4-class on Chinese
- **GLM-4-9B** (Jun 2024) — open mid-tier
- **GLM-4-Plus** (2025) — ongoing

nodes.title: Claude 1 — Anthropic's First Public Model

feels_like: Original Claude 1 — historical baseline; superseded by Claude 3+.
Three tiers (Opus / Sonnet / Haiku) — first time the same family covered flagship-quality and ultra-fast in one release
Opus Needle-in-Haystack: 99.4% recall average, 98.3% even at 200K tokens — long-context that genuinely works
Opus 90.5% one-shot, 89.2% zero-shot — set new state-of-the-art on multiple expert benchmarks
best_for: Long-document analysis (200K context with reliable retrieval)
Vision + text tasks where Opus / Sonnet outperform GPT-4V
Deployments needing a tier-aware family (Haiku triage → Sonnet workflow → Opus hard cases)
not_ideal_for: Bleeding-edge reasoning by 2025 — superseded by Claude 3.5 / 3.7 / 4 family
Self-hosted / open-weights deployments

sources.name: Anthropic — Introducing the next generation of Claude

public_view.plain_english:
Anthropic's first publicly available chatbot, launched four months
after ChatGPT and three weeks after GPT-4. Same conversational
interface, but with longer context and a different alignment recipe
(Constitutional AI) that produced a more cautious, more verbose
persona.
public_view.analogy:
Claude 1 was the first time a serious "second source" appeared in
the LLM API market. Like having a second long-distance carrier in
the 1980s — quality differences mattered less than the existence of
a credible alternative for enterprise procurement.
public_view.investment_angle:
Claude 1's launch validated the thesis that two API-tier LLM
competitors could coexist — a prerequisite for the $13.8B Amazon
investment that closed in late 2023, the $4B Google investment, and
the broader "second source for OpenAI" strategy that became standard
enterprise procurement language. Without Claude, OpenAI would have
had monopoly pricing power.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Claude 1 made the LLM API market a market — i.e., something with
competing vendors, comparison-shopping, and migration paths. Every
enterprise AI procurement decision since 2023 implicitly assumes the
existence of at least one credible alternative to the dominant
vendor. That assumption starts here.

nodes.body_md:
## How it compared to peers in March 2023

| Model | Date | Org | Context | RLHF / Alignment | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ChatGPT (GPT-3.5) | 2022-11 | OpenAI | 4k | RLHF | live, free + paid |
| GPT-4 | 2023-03-14 | OpenAI | 8k / 32k | RLHF | API |
| **Claude 1** | **2023-03-14** | **Anthropic** | **9k → 100k (May)** | **Constitutional AI** | **API** |
| Bard | 2023-03-21 | Google | undisclosed | undisclosed | live |

Claude 1 launched on the same day as GPT-4 — a coincidence widely read as deliberate. The strategic goal was to establish that there was a meaningful "second" within the news cycle of the dominant launch.

## The 100k context window

Claude 1.3's 100k-token context (announced May 2023) was the single feature that drove enterprise adoption. Use cases: summarize entire books, analyze full SEC filings, ingest large codebases. GPT-4 didn't match it until GPT-4-Turbo in November 2023 (128k).

The technical underpinning was likely some combination of:
- ALiBi or similar attention-bias positional schemes
- Sparse attention patterns
- Memory-efficient attention implementations (FlashAttention, by then standard)

Anthropic did not publish architectural details, which is itself a tell about how the lab had shifted from "research lab" to "model lab" by mid-2023.

nodes.title: GPT-4 — OpenAI's First Multimodal Frontier Model

feels_like: Step-change in reasoning vs GPT-3.5 — top 10% on simulated bar exam vs 3.5's bottom 10%
MMLU 86.4% in English; surpassed prior models in 24 of 26 other languages
First widely-deployed model with image input (text output only) — multimodal era starts here
19 percentage points fewer hallucinations than GPT-3.5 on adversarial factuality tests
Genuinely creative and reliable on nuanced instructions where 3.5 broke
OpenAI declined to publish parameter count or training details — closed-source standard set here
best_for: Professional knowledge work needing top-of-class reasoning at the time
Code generation with chain-of-thought prompting
Multilingual tasks across 26+ languages
Image-input multimodal workflows once GPT-4V landed
not_ideal_for: Frontier work after Claude 3.5+ / GPT-4o / Llama 3 — quickly surpassed in 2024
Cost-sensitive bulk inference — pricing dominant before GPT-4o cut it in half
Self-hosted deployments — closed weights

sources.name: MIT Press — How is ChatGPT's behavior changing

public_view.plain_english:
OpenAI's successor to GPT-3.5, launched as an API-gated upgrade to
ChatGPT Plus subscribers. Demonstrated a step-change in reasoning
benchmarks — bar exam, medical licensing, GRE, Olympiad math — and
added image input capability (text output only). OpenAI declined to
disclose model size or architecture, citing competitive and safety
reasons.
public_view.analogy:
GPT-3.5 was a bright undergraduate. GPT-4 was the same student
after graduating medical school, passing the bar, and getting an
MBA — across the board, not just in one specialty.
public_view.investment_angle:
GPT-4 is the inflection point where LLMs crossed the "good enough
to build a business on" threshold for knowledge-work applications.
Microsoft's $13B cumulative investment in OpenAI is underwritten
largely by GPT-4-era API demand; Nvidia's 2023-24 revenue tripling
is traceable to GPT-4-class training and inference workloads. The
consumer-grade $20/mo subscription pricing established the anchor
that every subsequent LLM product (Claude Pro, Gemini Advanced,
Perplexity Pro) now clusters around — suggesting market convergence
on a ~$240/yr ARPU ceiling for consumer AI until a genuinely new
capability tier emerges.
public_view.why_it_matters:
GPT-4 is what people actually mean when they say "AI" in a business
conversation in 2024-25. The reasoning, bar-exam, code-generation,
and image-understanding demos from GPT-4's launch are the reference
point that every subsequent model is implicitly benchmarked against
in casual discourse.

nodes.body_md:
## Benchmark step-change vs GPT-3.5

| Benchmark | GPT-3.5 | GPT-4 | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| MMLU (5-shot) | 70.0% | 86.4% | +16.4 |
| Uniform Bar Exam | 10th %ile | 90th %ile | 80 percentile |
| USMLE | ~50% | ~87% | +37 |
| HumanEval (Python) | 48.1% | 67.0% | +18.9 |
| GRE Verbal | 63rd %ile | 99th %ile | 36 percentile |
| AP Calculus BC | 43% | 51% | +8 |
| AMC 12 Math | 30th %ile | 84th %ile | 54 percentile |

These are the largest single-release benchmark jumps in the modern LLM era. No subsequent model has matched the magnitude.

## How it compared to competitors in 2023

| Model | Date | Org | MMLU | HumanEval | Context | Released as |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GPT-4** | **2023-03-14** | **OpenAI** | **86.4%** | **67.0%** | **8k / 32k** | **API, $20/mo chat** |
| Claude 1 | 2023-03-14 | Anthropic | ~75% | ~35% | 9k → 100k | API |
| Bard | 2023-03-21 | Google | — | — | — | free chat |
| PaLM 2 | 2023-05-10 | Google | 78.3% | 37.6% | 8k | Bard + API |
| Llama 2 70B | 2023-07-18 | Meta | 68.9% | 29.9% | 4k | open weights |
| Claude 2 | 2023-07-11 | Anthropic | 78.5% | 71.2% | 100k | API |
| GPT-4 Turbo | 2023-11-06 | OpenAI | — | — | 128k | API |

GPT-4 remained the de facto frontier model until late 2023 / early 2024, when Claude 2.1, Gemini Ultra, and eventually GPT-4-Turbo and GPT-4o shifted the landscape.

## The opacity norm

GPT-4's non-disclosure of size/architecture established a new norm: frontier model providers publish capabilities, not specifications. Every subsequent OpenAI release (GPT-4 Turbo, GPT-4o, o1, GPT-5) has continued this pattern. Anthropic's Claude releases and Google's Gemini releases have largely followed. Only Meta (Llama) and a few Chinese labs (DeepSeek, Qwen) have continued the older "publish weights + specs" tradition.

The normative shift matters: it has made independent third-party evaluation, scaling-law estimation, and regulatory oversight structurally harder.

nodes.title: RFdiffusion — Generative Protein Design

feels_like: Vision-language model from Academic / Independent — see the linked sources below for benchmark and review coverage
Vision and multimodal tasks are the typical fit per the published model card

See the model spec and sources block for benchmarked use cases
not_ideal_for: Tasks far outside the modalities listed in this model's spec
Workflows where a more recent successor in the same family scores higher

public_view.plain_english:
The Baker Lab's diffusion-based protein design model — released
July 2023, the first model that could generate novel proteins
that actually fold and bind to specified targets. Spawned an
entire wave of design tools: ProteinMPNN, LigandMPNN, RFantibody,
BindCraft, RFdiffusion 2/3. Foundation of David Baker's 2024
Nobel-recognized work.
public_view.analogy:
Stable Diffusion for proteins. Type a description ("bind to this
cancer marker"), get a candidate molecule that physically does
the thing.
public_view.investment_angle:
The Baker Lab has spawned more biotech startups than any single
academic group in the last decade. RFdiffusion + ProteinMPNN +
LigandMPNN are the open-source protein-engineering stack that
funds Generate Biomedicines, Cradle, Kura Bio, Profluent, and
others. Open weights with permissive academic licensing.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Earned David Baker a share of the 2024 Chemistry Nobel (alongside
Hassabis and Jumper for AlphaFold). Established that AI can
design novel functional proteins from scratch — a capability
biology had been chasing for 50 years.

citations.title: De novo design of protein structure and function with RFdiffusion

nodes.body_md:
## The Baker Lab open stack

- **RFdiffusion / RFdiffusion2 / RFdiffusion3** — protein backbone generation
- **RFantibody** — antibody-specialized variant
- **ProteinMPNN** — sequence design from structure
- **LigandMPNN** — ligand-aware sequence design
- **BindCraft** — binder design pipeline
- **RoseTTAFold2 / All-Atom** — structure prediction (parallels AlphaFold)

nodes.title: Llama 2 — Open-Weight LLM with Commercial License

feels_like: Llama 2 era — historical baseline; superseded by Llama 3+.
Major leap over Llama 2 — 70B operates in the same 'weight class' as GPT-4 and Claude 3 Sonnet at release
Climbed to 5th place on the LMSYS leaderboard within weeks — first open-weights model to genuinely compete with GPT-4
128K-token vocabulary tokenizer noticeably improved encoding efficiency vs Llama 2
best_for: Open-weights production deployments where Llama 2 was the previous baseline
Self-hosted RAG, code-completion, and chat applications under Llama 3 license
Fine-tuning on private corpora (the open license makes vertical specialisation safe)
not_ideal_for: Frontier reasoning leaderboards by late 2025 — superseded by Llama 3.1/3.3/4 and competitors
Multimodal tasks — text-only at this generation (Llama 3.2 Vision came later)

public_view.plain_english:
Meta's second open-weight foundation model, released alongside a
commercial-use license. The 70B variant approached GPT-3.5 on most
benchmarks; the 7B and 13B variants made high-quality LLMs runnable
on consumer hardware. Triggered the explosion of open-source LLM
fine-tunes (Mistral, Vicuna v2, Code Llama, Llama 2 Chat clones).
public_view.analogy:
Llama 1's research-only license was like releasing a car you could
look at but not drive. Llama 2's commercial license unlocked the
market — within six months, dozens of companies had products built
on Llama 2 that they could legally sell.

applications.product: Hugging Face Inference API default OSS model

public_view.investment_angle:
Llama 2's commercial license created the open-LLM startup ecosystem
overnight. Inference platforms (Together, Anyscale, Fireworks, Groq)
raised hundreds of millions on the thesis that "open weights + fast
serving" was a viable business. Vector databases (Pinecone, Weaviate)
saw a wave of "Llama + RAG" deployments. The strategic message to
OpenAI / Anthropic / Google: enterprise pricing has a ceiling, and
that ceiling is whatever it costs to self-host an open alternative.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Llama 2 is when "open source AI" stopped being a research curiosity
and started being a business model. The 2024-25 commodification of
LLM inference pricing (per-million-tokens dropping >90% in 18
months) is a direct downstream effect.

citations.title: Llama 2: Open Foundation and Fine-Tuned Chat Models

nodes.body_md:
## Llama 2 vs concurrent peers (mid-2023)

| Model | Date | Params | MMLU | HumanEval | License | Context |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-3.5 | 2022-11 | ~175B | 70.0% | 48.1% | API | 4k |
| GPT-4 | 2023-03 | undisclosed | 86.4% | 67.0% | API | 8k/32k |
| Claude 2 | 2023-07-11 | undisclosed | 78.5% | 71.2% | API | 100k |
| **Llama 2 70B** | **2023-07-18** | **70B** | **68.9%** | **29.9%** | **commercial OK** | **4k** |
| Llama 2 70B Chat | 2023-07-18 | 70B | 63.0% | 32.3% | commercial OK | 4k |
| Mistral 7B | 2023-09-27 | 7B | 60.1% | 30.5% | Apache 2.0 | 8k |

Llama 2 70B was meaningfully behind GPT-4 and Claude 2 on quality. The strategic value was the license, not the SOTA chase.

## Llama 2 Chat — open-source RLHF in production

Meta published the full RLHF pipeline used for Llama 2 Chat: SFT on 27.5k high-quality demonstrations, then iterative reward modeling and rejection-sampling fine-tuning (RFT) for several rounds, then PPO. This was the first detailed open-source description of a frontier-quality RLHF training run — and it set the methodological template that Mistral, Qwen, DeepSeek, and dozens of others followed.

Reward model architecture: two reward models — one for helpfulness, one for safety — trained on 1.4M human-labeled preference comparisons. The two-headed approach helped Llama 2 Chat avoid the "over-refusal" problem that plagued early RLHF deployments.

nodes.title: Qwen-7B / Qwen-14B — Alibaba's First Major Open Model

model_spec.architecture: Decoder-only transformer with RoPE, SwiGLU, RMSNorm

feels_like: First-generation Qwen — historical; superseded by 2.5+.
First open-source model to top OpenCompass leaderboard — beat Llama 3.1 405B with 1/5 the parameters
72B-Instruct: 74.2 on coding, 77 on math — outscored Claude 3.5 Sonnet (72.1) and GPT-4o (70.6) at release
MMLU 85+, HumanEval 85+, MATH 80+ — frontier-tier across the board for an open-weights model
best_for: Self-hosted production where Llama 3.1 405B is too big to serve
Multilingual deployments (29 languages with strong coverage)
Fine-tuning on private code or technical corpora
not_ideal_for: Frontier reasoning by mid-2025 — Qwen 3 / DeepSeek R1 / Claude 4 series have moved past it
Vision tasks (use Qwen 2.5-VL) or audio (use Qwen 2.5-Audio)

sources.name: Alibaba Cloud — Qwen 2.5: A Party of Foundation Models

public_view.plain_english:
Alibaba's first publicly-released open-weight foundation model
family — Qwen-7B and Qwen-14B — released under a permissive
commercial license. Outperformed Llama 2 on both Chinese (by a wide
margin) and English benchmarks at comparable size. Established
Alibaba as a credible open-LLM player and seeded the Qwen brand
that would dominate Chinese open-source LLMs through 2026.
public_view.analogy:
Where ChatGLM was the "Chinese MIT lab proof of concept,"
Qwen was the "Chinese Big Tech production model" — same role
Llama 2 played for Meta, with the same kind of commercial-license
bet on ecosystem traction.

applications.product: Hundreds of community fine-tunes on Hugging Face

public_view.investment_angle:
Qwen was Alibaba's strategic answer to Baidu's Ernie and ByteDance's
Doubao — proving Alibaba Cloud had the technical depth to compete
in domestic AI. The open-source release had two ROI angles:
(1) drive Aliyun GPU consumption from external users serving Qwen,
(2) build a distribution moat in the Chinese developer ecosystem
that closed Western models couldn't easily contest. By 2025, Qwen
series was the most-downloaded model family on Hugging Face by
Chinese-language requests, and Aliyun's AI revenue had grown 3-4×
YoY largely on Qwen-related workloads.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Qwen normalized the expectation that Chinese hyperscalers would
publish frontier-comparable models openly — a pattern continued by
DeepSeek, ByteDance Doubao (partial), and Tencent Hunyuan. The
pattern is structurally different from US hyperscalers (where Meta
is the only major open-weight publisher).

nodes.body_md:
## Qwen vs concurrent open models (Q3 2023)

| Model | Date | Params | C-Eval | MMLU | License |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Llama 2 13B | 2023-07 | 13B | 32.5% | 54.8% | commercial OK |
| Llama 2 70B | 2023-07 | 70B | 50.1% | 68.9% | commercial OK |
| Mistral 7B | 2023-09 | 7B | 47.4% | 60.1% | Apache 2.0 |
| ChatGLM2-6B | 2023-06 | 6B | 51.7% | 47.9% | research |
| **Qwen-7B** | **2023-08** | **7B** | **59.6%** | **58.2%** | **commercial OK** |
| **Qwen-14B** | **2023-08** | **14B** | **63.5%** | **66.3%** | **commercial OK** |
| Yi-34B | 2023-11 | 34B | 81.4% | 76.3% | commercial OK |

Qwen-14B was the strongest open Chinese-capable model in summer 2023; Yi-34B briefly took the lead in November but Qwen 1.5 (Feb 2024) and Qwen 2.5 (Sep 2024) reclaimed the position.

## The Qwen lineage (Aug 2023 → 2026)

| Generation | Date | Notable changes |
|---|---|---|
| Qwen 1.0 | 2023-08 | first release, 7B/14B/72B |
| Qwen 1.5 | 2024-02 | sizes 0.5B-72B, including 110B; Apache 2.0 license |
| Qwen 2 | 2024-06 | mixture-of-experts variant (Qwen2-MoE), 128k context |
| Qwen 2.5 | 2024-09 | strong open SOTA across Western and Chinese benchmarks |
| QwQ-32B (preview) | 2024-11 | reasoning model with extended-thinking |
| Qwen 2.5-VL | 2025-01 | multimodal vision flagship |
| Qwen 3 | 2025-Q2 | major refresh, MoE-first family |

By 2025, Qwen models account for an outsized share of model downloads on Hugging Face from non-US users.

nodes.title: Kimi Chat — Moonshot AI's Long-Context Bet

benchmarks.name: Needle-in-haystack (200k context)
benchmarks.vs_baseline: competitive with Claude 2.1 / GPT-4 Turbo at launch

feels_like: 1T-parameter MoE / 32B active per token — Moonshot's open-weights debut, modified MIT license
65.8% on SWE-bench Verified single-attempt — outperforms every model tested except Claude Sonnet 4 at release
53.7% on LiveCodeBench v6 — strong open-source coding tier
best_for: Self-hosted agent platforms where API models can't go (regulated, private cloud)
Cost-sensitive frontier-tier inference via budget providers
Coding agents that need long context + open weights for fine-tuning
not_ideal_for: Edge / single-GPU deployments — 1T MoE still demands multi-node serving
Multimodal tasks (text-only at this generation; vision lives in Kimi-VL)

sources.name: Cline — Kimi K2 for coding first impressions
Data Science Dojo — Kimi K2 deep dive

public_view.plain_english:
A Beijing startup founded in March 2023 by ex-Tsinghua / ex-Google
researchers; their consumer chat product Kimi launched in October
2023 with a 200k-token context window — the longest in the Chinese
market at the time. The product's "upload an entire book / annual
report and ask anything" framing made it the consumer breakout among
Chinese AI assistants in 2024. Expanded to 2M tokens in March 2024.
public_view.analogy:
Moonshot's bet was that "long context" — the ability to ingest a
whole book, annual report, or codebase in one prompt — would be
the killer Chinese-market feature. By focusing on this single
capability, Kimi captured the high-end professional / student
market while ByteDance's Doubao and Baidu's Ernie battled for
mass consumer.
public_view.investment_angle:
Moonshot AI raised >$1B at >$3B valuation by 2024 — making it one
of China's "AI tigers" alongside Zhipu, Baichuan, MiniMax. Kimi
became the most-discussed consumer AI product in Chinese tech press
in early 2024 (Q1-Q2 saw daily ARPU surge by ~10× as the product
went viral on Chinese student-and-professional communities).
The 2M-token release in March 2024 forced Western competitors to
accelerate long-context roadmaps (Gemini 1.5 Pro had launched
1M context just 1 month earlier; the timing was widely read as
coordinated industry repositioning around long-context as the
defensible feature).
public_view.why_it_matters:
Kimi made "long context" a marketing dimension that Chinese
consumers shopped on — analogous to how "fast charging" became
a smartphone marketing dimension in China before it was elsewhere.
Long-context as a productization choice (not just a benchmark
number) became the operating playbook.

nodes.body_md:
## How long-context positioning differentiated Kimi

| Model | Date | Context | Market |
|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-4 | 2023-03 | 8k / 32k | global |
| Claude 1 | 2023-03 | 9k / 100k | global |
| Claude 2 | 2023-07 | 100k | global |
| Llama 2 | 2023-07 | 4k | global open |
| **Kimi** | **2023-10** | **200k → 2M (Mar 2024)** | **Chinese** |
| Claude 2.1 | 2023-11 | 200k | global |
| GPT-4 Turbo | 2023-11 | 128k | global |
| Gemini 1.5 Pro | 2024-02 | 1M (10M experimental) | global |

For most of late 2023, Kimi had the longest context window in the Chinese market by a 50× margin over the next-best Chinese competitor. This was the marketing wedge.

## Use cases that drove adoption

Reports from Chinese-language tech press identify three killer use cases that drove Kimi's 2024 viral spread:

1. **Stock analyst Q&A on full annual reports** — upload 200-page A-share filings, ask "what are the management risks flagged"; Kimi could reason across the entire document rather than retrieving snippets.
2. **Student literature review** — upload 5-10 academic PDFs, ask for a synthesis with citations; competitive with the Western-market Claude 2.1 use case but in Chinese.
3. **Codebase ingestion** — upload an entire small repo (~20 files); useful for coding workflows where developers preferred long-context over RAG-based approaches.

The 2M-token expansion in March 2024 took these use cases to "ingest entire books / mid-sized codebases" scale.

nodes.title: Ernie 4.0 — Baidu's Closed Frontier Bet

benchmarks.name: C-Eval (Chinese, claimed)
benchmarks.vs_baseline: Baidu's framing: 'comparable to GPT-4 on Chinese tasks'

feels_like: Vision-language model from Baidu — see the linked sources below for benchmark and review coverage
Tool-use and agent loops are the typical fit per the published model card
Vision and multimodal tasks are the typical fit per the published model card
best_for: Agent / tool-use workflows that match the model's published benchmarks
Vision tasks (charts, documents, images) per the model card
See the model spec and sources block for benchmarked use cases
not_ideal_for: Tasks far outside the modalities listed in this model's spec
Workflows where a more recent successor in the same family scores higher

public_view.plain_english:
Baidu's flagship closed-API LLM, launched October 2023 as the
Chinese answer to GPT-4. Pre-existed ChatGPT in concept (Ernie 1.0
was 2019, predating BERT-style era for Baidu) but the 4.0 release
was the first to be marketed as a frontier-tier consumer product
in China. Closed API, integrated into Baidu Search, Baidu Maps,
Baidu's autonomous-driving stack, and Wenku.
public_view.analogy:
Baidu's "we have our own GPT-4" answer to investor and government
pressure. Less open than Qwen, less capable than DeepSeek would
later become. The legacy-search-engine answer to AI.
public_view.investment_angle:
Ernie 4.0's launch was as much defensive as competitive — Baidu
needed to demonstrate AI capability to keep its search-revenue
multiple from compressing further (it had already lost search share
to ByteDance's content-driven discovery and Tencent's WeChat
in-app search). Despite the heavy marketing push, Ernie struggled
against the open-source Qwen / DeepSeek wave on developer mindshare
and against ByteDance's Doubao on consumer adoption. By 2025,
Baidu had pivoted Ernie's monetization toward enterprise verticals
(finance, government, manufacturing) where closed APIs were
preferred over open weights for data-sovereignty reasons.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Ernie illustrates the strategic challenge faced by China's
incumbent tech giants in AI: the technical assets exist, but
the open-source upstarts (DeepSeek, Qwen) and consumer-distribution
leaders (ByteDance Doubao) have made it hard for Baidu to convert
technical parity into market share.

nodes.body_md:
## How Ernie compares to peers (late 2023)

| Model | Date | Org | Open? | Chinese strength |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-4 | 2023-03 | OpenAI | API | strong |
| Claude 2 | 2023-07 | Anthropic | API | weaker |
| ChatGLM3 | 2023-10 | Tsinghua / Zhipu | open | very strong |
| Qwen-72B | 2023-11 | Alibaba | open | very strong |
| **Ernie 4.0** | **2023-10** | **Baidu** | **API** | **strong (claimed)** |
| Yi-34B | 2023-11 | 01.AI | open | strong |

Ernie's closed positioning is the differentiator — among China's flagship LLMs in 2023, Ernie was the most aggressively closed/proprietary, while Qwen, ChatGLM, Yi went open-weight.

## Lineage

- Ernie 1.0 (2019) — pretrained masked LM, predates BERT's wide adoption in some respects
- Ernie 2.0 / 3.0 (2020-21) — knowledge graph integration emphasis
- Ernie 3.5 (2023-06) — Baidu's first chat-tuned consumer release
- **Ernie 4.0 (2023-10)** — flagship multimodal release, this node
- Ernie 4.5 (2024-04) — incremental update
- Ernie X1 (2025) — reasoning variant joining the o1/R1 wave

nodes.title: Yi-34B — 01.AI's Frontier Open Bilingual Model

model_spec.architecture: Decoder-only transformer (Llama 2 architecture, Yi-trained tokenizer + 3T-token data)

feels_like: First Chinese open-weights model to top Hugging Face Open LLM Leaderboard for pre-trained models
Bilingual (English / Chinese) — trained on 3T tokens up to June 2023
Beat Falcon-180B and Llama 2 70B on multiple benchmarks while being smaller and bilingual
Ranked first on C-Eval Chinese benchmark; competitive with Claude on AlpacaEval
Earned 01.AI unicorn status within months of release — established the China open-weights playbook
Yi-34B-200K variant pushed to 200K context, ahead of most open peers in late 2023
best_for: Bilingual production deployments where Chinese performance matters
Self-hosted RAG and chat systems prioritising open-weights cost over closed-API quality
Distillation source for smaller bilingual models
Research on long-context tuning (Yi-34B-200K was an early demonstration)
not_ideal_for: Frontier reasoning by 2024 — quickly surpassed by Qwen 2.5, DeepSeek V2/V3, Llama 3.x
Multimodal tasks (text-only at this generation)
Edge / single-GPU deployments at full 34B precision

sources.name: The Decoder — Yi beats Meta's Llama 2

public_view.plain_english:
01.AI's first major open-weight model, released November 2023.
Founded by Kai-Fu Lee (former Microsoft / Google China president,
venture capitalist, well-known author of "AI Superpowers"), 01.AI's
Yi-34B briefly held the top spot on Hugging Face's Open LLM
Leaderboard — the first non-Western model to do so. Quality at
34B parameters approached Llama 2's 70B variant.
public_view.analogy:
The Chinese AI ecosystem's "celebrity startup" — same playbook as
Anthropic in the US (founder is a known industry figure, raises
on his name as much as the technology, fast follow-on funding).
public_view.investment_angle:
01.AI raised >$1B at $1B+ valuation by 2024 on Kai-Fu Lee's brand
and Yi-34B's benchmark dominance. The launch period also sparked
a controversy: Yi-34B's architecture was nearly identical to
Llama 2's, and the original Hugging Face release used Llama 2's
config files with only the tensor names changed. 01.AI clarified
they had retrained from scratch on different data; the
architecture similarity was intentional (open standard) but the
config-file copy-paste was a procedural slip. The episode
illustrated the broader question — to what extent are "Chinese
foundation models" derivative of Llama? — that animated
discussion through 2024.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Yi-34B's brief leaderboard SOTA was the moment the Hugging Face
Open LLM community accepted that Chinese labs could ship at the
open-frontier. The architectural-attribution controversy
surfaced the harder question of how much credit Western
open-source releases (Llama, Mistral) deserve in the Chinese
open-LLM story.

nodes.body_md:
## Yi-34B vs concurrent open releases (Q4 2023)

| Model | Date | Params | MMLU | C-Eval | License |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Llama 2 13B | 2023-07 | 13B | 54.8% | 32.5% | commercial OK |
| Llama 2 70B | 2023-07 | 70B | 68.9% | 50.1% | commercial OK |
| Qwen-14B | 2023-08 | 14B | 66.3% | 63.5% | commercial OK |
| Mistral 7B | 2023-09 | 7B | 60.1% | 47.4% | Apache 2.0 |
| **Yi-34B** | **2023-11** | **34B** | **76.3%** | **81.4%** | **commercial OK** |
| Qwen-72B | 2023-11 | 72B | 77.4% | 84.1% | commercial OK |
| Mixtral 8x7B | 2023-12 | 47B (13B active) | 70.6% | — | Apache 2.0 |

Yi-34B's MMLU edged Llama 2 70B with half the parameters — the result that drove its Hugging Face leaderboard position. Within a month, Qwen-72B (released by Alibaba in late November 2023) overtook it on Chinese benchmarks; Mixtral overtook it on English benchmarks via MoE efficiency.

## The architecture-attribution controversy

Within 48 hours of Yi-34B's Hugging Face release, an open-source contributor (Eric Hartford) noted that Yi-34B's `config.json` was nearly identical to Llama 2's — including tensor naming conventions like `gate_proj` / `up_proj` / `down_proj`. 01.AI's response: the architecture was deliberately Llama-compatible (to leverage existing tooling), but the weights were trained from scratch on 01.AI's own data pipeline.

The community largely accepted this explanation but the episode established a norm: Chinese open-LLM releases now publish detailed pretraining methodology to preempt similar attribution debates (Qwen 1.5+, DeepSeek V2/V3, GLM-4 all include extensive pretraining data and recipe disclosure).

nodes.title: GraphCast — AI Weather Forecasting at ECMWF Quality

model_spec.architecture: Graph Neural Network on icosahedral grid

feels_like: Vision-language model from DeepMind — see the linked sources below for benchmark and review coverage
Vision and multimodal tasks are the typical fit per the published model card
best_for: Vision tasks (charts, documents, images) per the model card
See the model spec and sources block for benchmarked use cases
not_ideal_for: Tasks far outside the modalities listed in this model's spec
Workflows where a more recent successor in the same family scores higher

public_view.plain_english:
DeepMind's November 2023 weather model — a graph neural network
trained on 39 years of ERA5 reanalysis data, producing 10-day
