global weather forecasts in ~1 minute on a single TPU. Beats
ECMWF's gold-standard physics-based forecast on most metrics.
Published in Science. Inspired the entire AI-for-weather field.
public_view.analogy:
Numerical weather prediction has been the supercomputing crown
jewel for 70 years. GraphCast does it on a TPU faster and more
accurately than ECMWF's billion-dollar HPC pipeline — a kind of
"AlphaFold moment" for weather.
public_view.investment_angle:
AI weather is now a procurement category — energy traders,
agriculture, insurance, and shipping all need better forecasts.
GraphCast + Pangu-Weather + Aurora + FuXi compete for hosted
forecast services; ECMWF's adoption signals the market for
AI-physics-hybrid systems.
public_view.why_it_matters:
First credible "AI replaces an HPC pipeline" demonstration in
weather. Spawned NVIDIA FourCastNet, Microsoft Aurora, Huawei
Pangu-Weather, Shanghai AI Lab FengWu, Fudan FuXi, IBM+NASA
Prithvi-WxC, Google MetNet, and ECMWF's operational AIFS —
a complete AI-weather industry in 2 years.

citations.title: Learning skillful medium-range global weather forecasting

nodes.body_md:
## AI weather field (2024-2026)

| Model | Org | Notable |
|---|---|---|
| **GraphCast / GenCast / NeuralGCM** | DeepMind | Pioneer; GenCast is ensemble |
| Pangu-Weather | Huawei | Original transformer-on-weather |
| FourCastNet / Earth-2 | NVIDIA | Hosted commercial forecast |
| Aurora / ClimaX | Microsoft | Foundation model framing |
| FengWu | Shanghai AI Lab | China's leading weather AI |
| FuXi | Fudan | Long-range forecasting |
| Prithvi-WxC | IBM + NASA | Open-source; climate focus |
| **AIFS** | ECMWF | First operational AI-only forecast |

nodes.title: Gemini 1 — Google's Native Multimodal Frontier

model_spec.architecture: Multimodal transformer (text + image + audio + video natively)

feels_like: Original Gemini 1 — historical; superseded by 1.5+.
First model where 1M-token context was a real product feature, not a benchmark headline
99% needle-in-haystack accuracy at 1M tokens; 99.2% even at 10M in research configurations
Multi-needle recall drops to ~60% — single fact retrieval is solid, multi-fact is harder
best_for: Whole-codebase analysis (30K+ lines) without tedious chunking pipelines
Document QA over 1,500-page PDFs / batches of 100 emails
Hour-long video summarisation and audio transcription QA
not_ideal_for: Tasks requiring multi-fact retrieval across long contexts (recall drops sharply)
Pure short-context chat — Flash variants are cheaper and faster

sources.name: Google Cloud — Needle in Haystack test

public_view.plain_english:
Google's first model family launched under the Gemini brand,
designed natively for text + images + audio + video as a single
integrated system. Released in three sizes: Ultra (flagship),
Pro (balanced, on Bard), and Nano (1.8B and 3.25B, on Pixel
devices). Marketed as the first model to surpass GPT-4 on MMLU,
though under contested benchmarking conditions.
public_view.analogy:
Google's "code red" response to ChatGPT, finally shipping ~12
months after the initial 2022 panic. The product equivalent of a
big-budget studio sequel that arrived after the indie original
had already defined the genre.

applications.product: Google Workspace (Duet AI → Gemini for Workspace)

public_view.investment_angle:
Gemini 1's launch closed the symbolic gap from ChatGPT's
Nov-2022 surprise — Google now had a publicly comparable
flagship. Operationally, Gemini Pro on the free Bard interface
seeded a consumer-AI distribution channel inside Google's
existing 1B+ MAU Search and Workspace surfaces. The Gemini Nano
on-device variant is the underrated component: it's the first
flagship-lab LLM running entirely on consumer phone hardware,
setting the architectural template for Apple Intelligence and
Samsung Galaxy AI a year later.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Gemini 1 was less a technical breakthrough than a strategic
reset — Google reorganized its AI efforts under DeepMind's
Demis Hassabis (post the May 2023 Brain + DeepMind merger) and
Gemini was the first product output of that consolidation.

citations.title: Gemini: A Family of Highly Capable Multimodal Models

nodes.body_md:
## The contested launch benchmarks

Google's launch announcement claimed Gemini Ultra surpassed GPT-4 on MMLU (90.0% vs GPT-4's 86.4%). Within hours, observers noted:

- Google reported MMLU with a "CoT@32" ensemble (32 independent CoT samples + majority vote) vs GPT-4's standard 5-shot.
- On apples-to-apples 5-shot, Gemini Ultra scored 83.7% — slightly *behind* GPT-4.
- GPT-4 with CoT@32 scored ~87% (per OpenAI subsequent disclosure).

The benchmarking controversy distracted from the actual story: Gemini Ultra was roughly competitive with GPT-4 across most evaluations, with a meaningful native-multimodal advantage and a clear deficit in distribution / product polish.

## Gemini lineage 2023-2026

| Model | Date | Headline change |
|---|---|---|
| Gemini 1.0 (Ultra/Pro/Nano) | 2023-12 | tiered family launch |
| Gemini 1.5 Pro | 2024-02 | 1M-token context (10M experimental) |
| Gemini 1.5 Flash | 2024-05 | fast/cheap variant |
| Gemini 2.0 Flash | 2024-12 | reasoning + native image gen + agentic upgrades |
| Gemini 2.5 Pro | 2025-03 | "thinking" mode integrated, top SWE-bench in early 2025 |
| Gemini 3 | 2025-Q4 | next major frontier reset |

The 1M-token context (Gemini 1.5 Pro, Feb 2024) was the most consequential single capability addition in the line — it enabled "video understanding" and "entire-codebase analysis" use cases that competitors couldn't match for ~12 months.

nodes.title: Mixtral 8x7B — Open Sparse Mixture-of-Experts

model_spec.architecture: 8 experts × 7B; top-2 routing per token; MoE in FFN layers only

feels_like: First major MoE open-weights release that actually worked for production — 46.7B total / 13B active per token
6x faster inference than Llama 2 70B at equal-or-better quality on most benchmarks
Outperformed GPT-3.5 on the LMSYS arena while being fully open-weight under Apache 2.0
MT-Bench score earned 'best open-weights model as of December 2023'
Vastly better than Llama 2 70B on math, code, and multilingual benchmarks
Set the architectural template (router + sparse experts) followed by every later open MoE
best_for: Open-source production inference where Llama 2 70B was too slow
Multilingual workloads (Mixtral was particularly strong outside English at release)
Self-hosted RAG and code-completion stacks under Apache 2.0
Research into MoE routing, distillation, and serving
not_ideal_for: Frontier reasoning by 2025 — superseded by Mixtral 8x22B, Llama 3.x, DeepSeek V3, Qwen 3
Latency-sensitive single-GPU edge deployments (8 experts × 7B still demands meaningful VRAM)
Vision / multimodal tasks — text only at this generation

sources.name: Mistral AI — Mixtral of Experts announcement

public_view.plain_english:
A French startup's open-weight model that activates only 13B of its
47B parameters for any given input — a design that delivers the
quality of a 70B model at the inference cost of a 13B one. Released
via a magnet link with no documentation, it became the first
open-weight Mixture-of-Experts model competitive with proprietary
competitors.
public_view.analogy:
Instead of one expert who studied everything and remembers it all,
you have eight specialists at the door of every question — and the
router decides which two are best suited to answer this particular
question. Same total knowledge, much faster answers.

applications.product: Hosted inference at Together, Anyscale, Fireworks

public_view.investment_angle:
Mistral AI raised $415M at a $2B valuation within 6 months of
incorporation — making the case that European AI labs could
compete on open weights despite trailing OpenAI/Anthropic on
capital. By 2024 the company had reached $6B valuation and
productized Mixtral as Mistral Large API. Mixtral validated MoE as
the dominant frontier architecture (GPT-4, DeepSeek-V3, Claude all
believed to be MoE) and accelerated the inference-platform business
case: serving MoE models well requires specialized infrastructure,
creating moats for inference vendors.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Mixtral's release proved that sparse architectures could ship in
open weights at frontier-comparable quality. Every subsequent MoE
open release (DeepSeek V2/V3, Qwen-MoE, Snowflake Arctic) traces
its strategy to this launch. Marked the transition of open-source
LLM development from "Llama clones" to architectural diversity.

nodes.body_md:
## How Mixtral compares to peers

| Model | Date | Params (active) | Context | MMLU | HumanEval | GSM8K |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Llama 2 70B | 2023-07 | 70B (70B) | 4k | 68.9% | 29.9% | 56.8% |
| Mistral 7B | 2023-09 | 7B (7B) | 8k | 60.1% | 30.5% | 52.1% |
| Claude 2.1 | 2023-11 | undisclosed | 200k | — | — | — |
| GPT-4 Turbo | 2023-11 | undisclosed | 128k | ~86% | ~85% | — |
| **Mixtral 8x7B** | **2023-12** | **47B (13B)** | **32k** | **70.6%** | **40.2%** | **60.4%** |
| Mixtral 8x7B Instruct | 2023-12 | 47B (13B) | 32k | — | — | — |
| Mixtral 8x22B | 2024-04 | 141B (39B) | 64k | 77.8% | 45.1% | 76.5% |

Mixtral's headline result: matches or beats Llama 2 70B on text quality at ~⅙ the inference compute. The architectural lesson — sparse models give better quality-per-FLOP than dense ones at scale — is the basis of every frontier model believed to be MoE today (GPT-4, GPT-4o, Claude 3+, Gemini, DeepSeek-V3).

## The release stunt

Mixtral was released on December 8, 2023 via a magnet link tweeted by Mistral CEO Arthur Mensch with no announcement, no documentation, and no model card. The community reverse-engineered the architecture within hours. Mistral published a formal blog post three days later. The deliberate "release first, document later" approach contrasted sharply with OpenAI's safety-card cadence and earned Mistral a reputation for "ship like the open-source community ships."

nodes.title: BGE-M3 — Open Multilingual Multifunction Embeddings

feels_like: Language model from BAAI — see the linked sources below for benchmark and review coverage
best_for: General-purpose tasks within BAAI's deployment footprint
See the model spec and sources block for benchmarked use cases
not_ideal_for: Tasks far outside the modalities listed in this model's spec
Workflows where a more recent successor in the same family scores higher

public_view.plain_english:
Beijing Academy of AI's January 2024 embedding model — supports
100+ languages and produces dense, sparse, AND multi-vector
outputs from a single forward pass. The de facto open embedding
model for multilingual RAG; widely deployed and forked.
public_view.analogy:
BGE-M3 is the embedding equivalent of "free shipping included" —
most open RAG stacks just default to it because it's the obvious
choice that works.
public_view.investment_angle:
BGE is BAAI's strongest single export to the global open-source
AI ecosystem. The fact that most LangChain-tier deployments
default to it gives BAAI quiet upstream influence on retrieval
architecture worldwide.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Made high-quality multilingual embeddings free and self-hostable.
Combined with BGE-Reranker v2-m3, the BGE stack is the open RAG
backbone for languages other than English.

citations.title: BGE M3-Embedding: Multi-Lingual, Multi-Functionality, Multi-Granularity Text Embeddings Through Self-Knowledge Distillation

nodes.body_md:
## BGE family

- **BGE-large** / BGE-base / BGE-small — English-focused
- **BGE-M3** — multilingual + multi-function (Jan 2024)
- **bge-en-icl** — in-context learning embeddings (71.24 MTEB)
- **BGE-Reranker v2-m3** — companion reranker

nodes.title: Sora — OpenAI's Text-to-Video

model_spec.architecture: DiT (Diffusion Transformer) on spatiotemporal patches; latent video diffusion

benchmarks.name: Output duration
benchmarks.vs_baseline: Runway Gen-2 16s, Pika 4s, prior demos sub-10s

benchmarks.name: Output resolution
benchmarks.vs_baseline: step change vs prior consumer text-to-video

feels_like: Earlier Sora preview — succeeded by Sora 2.
First Sora generation with native synchronized audio — speech, sound effects, ambient soundscape from one model
Real physics: missed basketball rebounds off the backboard; objects respect buoyancy and rigidity
Olympic gymnastics, paddleboard backflips, ice-skating triple axels — motion that prior systems couldn't render
best_for: Short-form social video generation (the Sora app's whole purpose)
Storyboards / previz where physical accuracy matters more than fine-grained creative control
Custom-character video using cameo for personalisation
not_ideal_for: Frame-perfect creative control — no full keyframe editing the way Runway / Kling offer
Long-form (>60s) coherent narrative — best on short clips

public_view.plain_english:
OpenAI's text-to-video model, demonstrated as research previews
in February 2024. The viral demo videos — a fashion-shoot in
Tokyo, a snow leopard in mountains, vintage California
drone footage — convinced the public that AI-generated video
was crossing into "could be mistaken for real footage" territory.
Held back from public release until December 2024 (Sora 1.0)
and meaningfully iterated as Sora 2 in late 2025.
public_view.analogy:
Sora's February 2024 release played the same role for video
that GPT-3's June 2020 release played for text: a step-change
capability demo from a private research lab that compressed
the broader industry's video-AI roadmap by 12-18 months.

applications.product: Various film and ad-agency creative pipelines (limited access)

public_view.investment_angle:
Sora's February 2024 demo was the catalyst for the
text-to-video startup investment wave: Runway, Pika, Genmo,
HeyGen, and Chinese players (Kling from Kuaishou, Hailuo
from MiniMax) all raised significant rounds in the
following 6 months on the thesis that "video is the next
image generation." The Hollywood / advertising / education
industries also began serious AI-video evaluation. By 2025,
text-to-video had crossed into limited production use in
advertising (insurance ads, retail demonstrations, social
content) — though still a tiny fraction of total video
production. The full industry transformation, if it
happens, is a 2026-2030 story.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Sora is the inflection point where video joined image, text,
audio, and code as a domain where AI-generated content was
no longer obviously distinguishable from human-produced
content. The implications for advertising, film, content
moderation, and disinformation are still being absorbed.

citations.title: Video generation models as world simulators

nodes.body_md:
## How Sora compares to text-to-video peers

| System | Date | Org | Max length | Resolution | Released |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Make-A-Video | 2022-09 | Meta | 5s | 768x768 | research |
| Imagen Video | 2022-10 | Google | 5s | 1280x768 | research |
| Phenaki | 2022-09 | Google | 2 min (low quality) | 128x128 | research |
| Pika 1.0 | 2023-12 | Pika | 4s | 1280x720 | product |
| Runway Gen-2 | 2023-06 | Runway | 16s | 1280x768 | product |
| **Sora preview** | **2024-02** | **OpenAI** | **60s** | **1920x1080** | **demo only** |
| Kling 1.0 | 2024-06 | Kuaishou | 2 min | 1080p | product (China-first) |
| Hailuo (MiniMax) | 2024-08 | MiniMax | 6s | 720p | product |
| Veo | 2024-05 | Google | 60s+ | 1080p | demo |
| Sora 1.0 | 2024-12 | OpenAI | 20s (in product) | up to 1080p | product |
| Veo 2 | 2024-12 | Google | 8s (4k) | 4k | API |
| **Sora 2** | **2025-09** | **OpenAI** | **dramatic quality jump** | — | product |
| Veo 3 | 2025-Q3 | Google | improved physics | — | product |

Sora's 60-second 1080p preview was the quality / duration ceiling-breaker. Subsequent product releases (Sora 1.0 Dec 2024, Sora 2 Sep 2025) gradually closed the gap between demo capability and shipping product capability.

## The "world simulator" framing

OpenAI's research write-up emphasized emergent capabilities — Sora apparently learned 3D consistency, object permanence, basic physics — and framed video diffusion as an early step toward a "general world model." This framing had implications for the broader narrative around scaling laws: maybe scaling video pretraining also produces useful world-knowledge representations, not just video output.

The framing was also commercially important: it positioned Sora not just as "OpenAI also has a video generator" but as "OpenAI has a foundational world-modeling capability that happens to ship today as video." The research-preview-then-9-months-of-iteration release pattern was unusual; most labs ship product upon demo.

nodes.title: Claude 3 — Opus, Sonnet, Haiku (Tiered Frontier Family)

feels_like: Three tiers (Opus / Sonnet / Haiku) — first time the same family covered flagship-quality and ultra-fast in one release
Opus Needle-in-Haystack: 99.4% recall average, 98.3% even at 200K tokens — long-context that genuinely works
Opus 90.5% one-shot, 89.2% zero-shot — set new state-of-the-art on multiple expert benchmarks
Haiku is the fastest and cheapest model in its intelligence tier as of 2024 — practical for high-volume tasks
Sonnet 2x faster than Claude 2 / 2.1 with higher intelligence — became the default tier
Opus felt notably more 'careful' than GPT-4 — safer refusals at the cost of occasional over-caution
best_for: Long-document analysis (200K context with reliable retrieval)
Vision + text tasks where Opus / Sonnet outperform GPT-4V
Deployments needing a tier-aware family (Haiku triage → Sonnet workflow → Opus hard cases)
Customer-facing apps where Anthropic's safety tuning is a feature, not friction
not_ideal_for: Bleeding-edge reasoning by 2025 — superseded by Claude 3.5 / 3.7 / 4 family
Self-hosted / open-weights deployments
Tasks where Opus's caution/refusal patterns get in the way

sources.name: Anthropic — Introducing the next generation of Claude

public_view.plain_english:
Anthropic's first three-tier model family — Opus (flagship,
highest-quality), Sonnet (balanced), and Haiku (fast and cheap).
On release, Opus was the first model to publicly outscore GPT-4 on
most benchmarks, ending GPT-4's 12-month run as uncontested SOTA.
Added vision input and pushed context to 200,000 tokens.
public_view.analogy:
Airlines priced this way decades ago — economy, business, first.
Anthropic brought the segmentation to LLM APIs and taught every
competitor the pricing structure. Every major provider now ships a
fast-cheap / balanced / flagship trio.
public_view.investment_angle:
Claude 3 is the model that repositioned Anthropic from "serious
challenger" to "peer of OpenAI." The tiered pricing structure —
Opus at $15/$75 per 1M tokens input/output, Haiku at $0.25/$1.25 —
established the price/quality Pareto frontier that every LLM
vendor now competes on. Amazon's $4B initial and eventual
$13.8B total investment in Anthropic was committed pre-Claude-3
but justified post-launch.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Claude 3 ended the "there's just GPT-4 and then everything else"
narrative that had held for a year. Post-launch, every enterprise
AI procurement discussion started including Claude as a first-tier
option. The tier-based pricing is now industry standard.

citations.title: Introducing the next generation of Claude

nodes.body_md:
## Claude 3 Opus vs frontier peers at launch

| Model | Date | Org | MMLU | GSM8K | HumanEval | Context |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-4 | 2023-03 | OpenAI | 86.4% | 92.0% | 67.0% | 8k/32k |
| GPT-4 Turbo | 2023-11 | OpenAI | — | — | ~85% | 128k |
| Gemini Ultra | 2023-12 | Google | 83.7% | 94.4% | 74.4% | 32k |
| **Claude 3 Opus** | **2024-03** | **Anthropic** | **86.8%** | **95.0%** | **84.9%** | **200k** |
| Claude 3 Sonnet | 2024-03 | Anthropic | 79.0% | 92.3% | 73.0% | 200k |
| Claude 3 Haiku | 2024-03 | Anthropic | 75.2% | 88.9% | 75.9% | 200k |

Opus was the first model to exceed GPT-4 on most benchmarks by non-trivial margins. The HumanEval delta (+17.9) was particularly notable — Claude became the code-generation workhorse in many developer workflows and the default model in Cursor.

## The tiered-pricing innovation

Before Claude 3, LLM providers mostly shipped one flagship per generation with a secondary "turbo" or "instruct" variant. Claude 3's three named tiers — each a differentiated product — forced:

- **OpenAI** to clarify tiering (GPT-4o + GPT-4o-mini, May/July 2024)
- **Google** to relabel Gemini into Ultra / Pro / Nano (Feb 2024, pre-empted)
- **Mistral** to release Large / Medium / Small (Feb 2024, same week)

The economic effect: enterprises can now route traffic across tiers by task, dropping blended cost per query by 3-10× without quality loss. This is the operational foundation of "agentic" deployments (where a single user request spawns dozens of sub-model calls).

nodes.title: Devin — First Autonomous Software Engineer Agent

model_spec.architecture: agent loop with shell, browser, editor, planner

feels_like: Language model from Cognition — see the linked sources below for benchmark and review coverage
Code-leaning workloads are the typical fit per the published model card
Tool-use and agent loops are the typical fit per the published model card
best_for: Coding workflows that match the model's published benchmarks
Agent / tool-use workflows that match the model's published benchmarks
See the model spec and sources block for benchmarked use cases
not_ideal_for: Tasks far outside the modalities listed in this model's spec
Workflows where a more recent successor in the same family scores higher

public_view.plain_english:
Cognition AI's autonomous SWE agent — released as a demo in
March 2024, generally available later. Devin ran in a sandboxed
Linux environment with shell, browser, and code editor, and
completed multi-hour software engineering tasks unsupervised. The
SWE-bench score (13.86%) at launch was 7× the prior best, kicking
off the agentic-coding arms race.
public_view.analogy:
Earlier coding tools were autocomplete on steroids. Devin was
the first attempt at a junior engineer who actually does the
work — opens a ticket, plans, codes, runs tests, fixes errors,
and commits.
public_view.investment_angle:
Cognition raised $200M+ at $4B valuation post-launch. Devin's
demo redefined investor expectations for AI agents — every YC
batch since has had a "Devin for X" pitch. The category created
the need for SWE-bench Verified, Terminal-Bench, and other
long-horizon evals.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Defined the autonomous-coding-agent category. Whether or not
Devin itself dominates long-term, every coding-agent product
(Cursor Composer, Windsurf Cascade, Claude Code, Codex CLI) is
a response to Devin's framing of "agent in a sandbox" as the
right product shape.

citations.title: Introducing Devin, the first AI software engineer

nodes.body_md:
## Why Devin mattered

Three things together for the first time:

1. **Long-horizon execution** — multi-hour tasks without human intervention
2. **Multi-tool environment** — shell + browser + editor inside a sandbox
3. **End-to-end product** — not a demo notebook; a hosted agent product

The combination made "agentic coding" a real product category. Subsequent products (Devin successors plus Claude Code, Codex, Cursor Composer, Replit Agent) all share these shapes.

nodes.title: Suno v3 — Text-to-Music at Consumer Quality

model_spec.architecture: Audio token transformer (likely encodec-style discrete audio tokens + LM)

benchmarks.name: Output duration
benchmarks.vs_baseline: v2 30 seconds; v1 hummable clips only

benchmarks.name: Lyric quality + vocal coherence
benchmarks.score: first version where most outputs sound 'like a real song'

feels_like: First text-to-music model where the output 'sounds like a real song' to most listeners
Generates full 2-minute tracks with vocals, structure, and genre coherence from a one-line prompt
Critics: 'astonishing' (jhave); Tyler Cowen called it a real cultural moment in early 2024
Wide stylistic range — 80s disco, accordion death metal, steampunk theatrical, ballads, post-rock
Weakness: simple, memorable melodies are still hard — 'remarkable but not enough music in the music'
Audio still has tinges of low-res grain and occasional inarticulate vocal yelping (uncanny-valley moments)
best_for: Music ideation, demos, and creative prompt-to-track exploration
Background music for video / podcasts / ads (royalty-free generation by subscription)
Hobbyists who want polished song output without DAW skills
Genre-blending experiments and conceptual / parody tracks
not_ideal_for: Replacing professional songwriters or composers for finished commercial work
Workflows needing fine-grained editing of stems / lyrics / arrangement after generation
Cases where memorable, simple melodic hooks are critical (still a model weakness)

public_view.plain_english:
A Boston-based startup's text-to-music generator that, in
its v3 release (March 2024), crossed the threshold of
"this sounds like a real song." Type a description ("a
melancholy 90s grunge ballad about taxes") and get a 2-minute
track with vocals, instruments, mixing, and mastering. Within
months it became the dominant consumer AI music product.
public_view.analogy:
Where DALL·E 2 / Stable Diffusion were the "type a prompt
and get an image" moment for visual creation in 2022,
Suno v3 was that moment for music in 2024. Same UX
pattern, new modality.
public_view.investment_angle:
Suno raised $125M Series B in May 2024 at ~$500M valuation
on the strength of the v3 release. By early 2025, monthly
paying subscribers had reportedly crossed 500k at $10/mo
tier — making it one of the most successful consumer-AI
standalone products outside of chat. Sparked the inevitable
legal dispute: in June 2024, RIAA-affiliated labels (Sony,
Universal, Warner) filed copyright lawsuits against Suno
and Udio for training on copyrighted music. The lawsuits
are a defining test case for the limits of "fair use" in
AI training data — and could substantially reshape the
economics of generative AI music depending on outcome.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Suno is the proof point that AI consumer products can
reach standalone-business scale outside of chat /
productivity. The pattern (low-friction creative tool +
monthly subscription + viral social distribution) is being
copied across other modalities now (video, 3D, voice,
longform writing).

nodes.body_md:
## Suno vs concurrent text-to-music systems

| System | Year | Org | Quality | Open? | Length |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| MuseNet | 2019 | OpenAI | MIDI / instrumental only | research | up to 4 min |
| Jukebox | 2020 | OpenAI | early raw-audio attempt | research | minutes |
| MusicLM | 2023-01 | Google | research preview | demos | 30 sec |
| MusicGen | 2023-06 | Meta | open weights | open | 30 sec |
| Stable Audio | 2023-09 | Stability | open + API | partial | 90 sec |
| **Suno v3** | **2024-03** | **Suno** | **consumer quality** | **product** | **2 min** |
| Udio v1 | 2024-04 | Udio | competitive | product | 2 min |
| Suno v4 | 2024-11 | Suno | improved | product | 4+ min |

Suno's product velocity through 2024 outpaced research labs significantly. Whether this advantage holds depends on the copyright lawsuits and on whether Big Tech labs (OpenAI, Google, Meta) eventually ship competitive consumer products.

## The copyright case

In June 2024, RIAA-affiliated labels (Sony Music, Universal Music Group, Warner Music) filed lawsuits against Suno and Udio in US federal court alleging willful copyright infringement during training. Suno's response (filed August 2024) acknowledged training on copyrighted music but argued fair-use defense. The case is procedurally pending as of 2026 and is one of the most-watched AI copyright cases globally — the outcome affects training-data strategies across the entire generative AI industry.

nodes.title: DBRX — Databricks' Open MoE

model_spec.architecture: Sparse MoE — 16 experts × 8.25B per expert, top-4 routing

feels_like: Fine-grained MoE: 16 experts × 4 active vs Mixtral/Grok-1's 8×2 — more granular routing
132B total / 36B active per token; beat Llama 2 70B, Mixtral, and Grok-1 on Databricks Gauntlet (30+ benchmarks)
Beat GPT-3.5 on most benchmarks at release — open-source replacement for GPT-3.5 was the headline pitch
Inference up to 2x faster than Llama 2 70B; ~40% the size of Grok-1 by parameter count
Pre-training pipeline became 4x more compute-efficient — DBRX MoE-B beat LLaMA2-13B with 1.7x fewer FLOPs
Aimed at the Databricks customer base — replace proprietary models with self-hosted on Mosaic AI
best_for: Databricks customers running self-hosted LLM workloads on Mosaic AI
Fine-grained MoE research and routing experiments
Replacing GPT-3.5-tier API calls with self-hosted inference
Distillation source for smaller models trained on DBRX outputs
not_ideal_for: Frontier reasoning by 2025 — quickly surpassed by Llama 3.x, DeepSeek V3, Mixtral 8x22B, etc.
Single-GPU edge deployments — the 132B-total MoE needs serious memory
Multimodal tasks (text-only)

sources.name: Analytics Vidhya — DBRX taking on the giants

public_view.plain_english:
Databricks' open-weight MoE model released March 2024 — the
first major US open release after the Mixtral / Llama / Mistral
wave. 132B total parameters, 36B active per query, optimized
for serving on Databricks' own infrastructure. Notable for
being trained on 12T tokens (the largest training-data
disclosure for a US open model at the time).
public_view.analogy:
"Databricks the AI company" announcement — the model was the
visible artifact, but the strategic message was that
Databricks (a $43B-valued data-warehouse company) was now a
serious AI infrastructure provider, not just a customer.
public_view.investment_angle:
DBRX was the model artifact behind Databricks' $1.3B Mosaic AI
acquisition strategy paying off. Operationally, DBRX wasn't
market-leading — by Q3 2024 it was clearly behind Llama 3,
Qwen 2, and DeepSeek V2 — but as a strategic
"we ship frontier models" demonstration it justified
Databricks' AI-platform positioning. The company crossed
$3B ARR by late 2024, with ~25% attributed to AI workloads
that DBRX helped seed.
public_view.why_it_matters:
DBRX was the moment "data company" and "AI model lab" became
overlapping categories. Snowflake (Arctic, April 2024) and
BigQuery's AI investments followed similar logic: own the
data, own the model that runs on the data.

nodes.body_md:
## DBRX vs concurrent open models (Q1-Q2 2024)

| Model | Date | Active params | MMLU | HumanEval | License |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Llama 2 70B | 2023-07 | 70B | 68.9% | 29.9% | LLAMA 2 |
| Mixtral 8x7B | 2023-12 | 13B | 70.6% | 40.2% | Apache 2.0 |
| Mixtral 8x22B | 2024-04 | 39B | 77.8% | 45.1% | Apache 2.0 |
| **DBRX** | **2024-03** | **36B** | **73.7%** | **70.1%** | **DBRX (commercial OK)** |
| Llama 3 70B | 2024-04 | 70B | 82.0% | 81.7% | Llama 3 |
| Qwen 1.5 72B | 2024-02 | 72B | 77.5% | 64.6% | Qwen |
| Snowflake Arctic | 2024-04 | 17B (480B total) | 67.3% | 64.3% | Apache 2.0 |

DBRX held the open-source leaderboard for ~3 weeks before Llama 3 70B's release. Its architecture (fine-grained MoE) influenced subsequent open releases including Qwen 2-MoE and DeepSeek V2.

nodes.title: Stable Audio 2.0 — Open Latent-Diffusion Audio

model_spec.architecture: Latent diffusion on EnCodec audio tokens + DiT (Diffusion Transformer) backbone

benchmarks.name: Output duration
benchmarks.vs_baseline: Stable Audio 1.0 90s; MusicGen 30s; Suno v3 (closed) 2 min

feels_like: Stability's audio diffusion sibling — same lab heritage.
First text-to-image model with DALL·E 2-class quality and permissive open weights
Latent diffusion innovation — denoising in compressed latent space, not pixel space — made consumer-GPU inference viable
Ran on <8GB VRAM at release — first generative model regular people could use locally
best_for: Self-hosted image generation pipelines (privacy / volume / customisation)
Custom-style fine-tuning via LoRA / textual inversion / Dreambooth
ControlNet-style guided generation requiring weight access
not_ideal_for: Out-of-the-box photorealistic aesthetics — Midjourney still the default for that
Reliable in-image text rendering (FLUX.2 and later models leapfrogged here)

sources.name: Stability AI — Stable Diffusion public release
BentoML — Best open-source image generation models

public_view.plain_english:
Stability AI's open text-to-audio generator, releasing 3-minute
44.1kHz stereo tracks from text prompts. Less viral than Suno
(which had launched v3 two weeks earlier as a polished consumer
product), but the only open-weight option in the category — a
role analogous to what Stable Diffusion played in image
generation while DALL-E was closed.
public_view.analogy:
The "Stable Diffusion of audio" — same Stability AI playbook
of "release open weights as the open-source counterweight to
closed competitors." Less polished consumer experience, more
foundational ecosystem value.
public_view.investment_angle:
Stable Audio 2 was Stability AI's attempt to repeat its
image-generation playbook in audio. The execution
underperformed: by 2024 Stability AI was in financial
distress (founder Emad Mostaque had departed in March 2024;
funding crises continued through 2024-25). The open-weight
audio category is now smaller commercially than Suno / Udio's
closed business, illustrating that open weights aren't
automatically the dominant strategy in every modality.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Stable Audio 2 is the open baseline that makes the closed
audio-AI category contestable. Without it, Suno / Udio /
ElevenLabs would have unchallenged pricing power.

nodes.body_md:
## Stable Audio 2 vs concurrent text-to-audio (April 2024)

| Model | Date | Org | Open? | Length | Quality |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Jukebox | 2020 | OpenAI | research | minutes | early-era |
| MusicLM | 2023-01 | Google | demos | 30s | research preview |
| MusicGen | 2023-06 | Meta | open | 30s | open baseline |
| Stable Audio 1.0 | 2023-09 | Stability | open | 90s | open improvement |
| Suno v3 | 2024-03 | Suno | product | 2 min | consumer breakthrough |
| **Stable Audio 2.0** | **2024-04** | **Stability AI** | **open** | **3 min** | **best open at release** |
| Udio v1 | 2024-04 | Udio | product | 2 min | competitive consumer |
| Suno v4 | 2024-11 | Suno | product | 4+ min | continued lead |

nodes.title: Cohere Command R+ — RAG-Optimized Open Frontier

model_spec.architecture: Decoder-only transformer + native tool use + multilingual + RAG-optimized post-training

benchmarks.name: RAG benchmark (HotpotQA, NQ)
benchmarks.score: best open-weight RAG quality at release
benchmarks.vs_baseline: outperforms Llama 2 70B by ~15pts on multi-hop QA

benchmarks.name: Tool use accuracy
benchmarks.score: first open model with strong native tool use

feels_like: Industry-leading RAG with in-line citations baked into the response — Cohere's signature differentiator
104B params, 88.2% MMLU at release — competitive with GPT-4 Turbo on enterprise tasks
Beats Claude 3 Sonnet, Mistral Large, and GPT-4 Turbo on Cohere's internal RAG / tool-use benchmarks
Strong on Berkeley Function Calling and ToolTalk Hard — designed for multi-step agent flows
Multilingual coverage in 10 major business languages (EN, FR, DE, ES, IT, JA, KO, AR, PT, ZH)
Optimised for the enterprise sales motion — RAG citations matter more here than benchmark headlines
best_for: Enterprise RAG where citation accuracy and hallucination reduction matter most
Multi-step tool-use agents in regulated industries
Multilingual customer-facing assistants beyond English
Workflows needing predictable, citation-backed enterprise output
not_ideal_for: Frontier-leaderboard reasoning by 2025 — newer Cohere Command-A and competitors lead
Consumer / chatbot use cases where personality and creativity matter
Coding-agent benchmarks (Claude Sonnet / GPT-5 stronger here)

sources.name: VentureBeat — Command R+ beats GPT-4 Turbo

public_view.plain_english:
Cohere's flagship open-weight model, released April 2024.
Differentiated by being explicitly trained for retrieval-
augmented generation (RAG) workflows — given retrieved
documents in context, it integrates them more accurately and
cites them more reliably than generic LLMs. The default model
behind much enterprise RAG infrastructure in 2024-25.
public_view.analogy:
The "specialist B2B player" of the open-LLM market — where
Llama is the consumer-AI ecosystem star, Command R+ is the
enterprise-RAG workhorse. Less viral, more profitable per
deployment.

applications.product: Embedded in Notion AI / Box AI / Oracle / others (enterprise)
Hosted on Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (deep partnership)

public_view.investment_angle:
Cohere's positioning as the "enterprise-trusted alternative to
OpenAI/Anthropic" was bought partly with Command R+'s release.
The company raised $500M Series D at $5.5B valuation in July
2024 partly on this positioning. Strategic moat: enterprise
relationships (Oracle, Salesforce, Notion) + multilingual
coverage that Western-focused competitors deprioritize +
explicit on-prem deployment options that the OpenAI / Anthropic
APIs don't offer. By 2025-26 the company's revenue trajectory
(reportedly $300M+ ARR) was solid but smaller than the
flagship-lab tier — illustrating the tradeoff between
differentiated B2B positioning and consumer-attention scale.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Command R+ is the proof that "specialized for enterprise"
can be a viable LLM-business positioning distinct from
"race to the frontier." The continued existence of Cohere
as a meaningful enterprise vendor (vs being absorbed by
a hyperscaler) validates the segment.

nodes.body_md:
## Command R+ vs concurrent peers (Q2 2024)

| Model | Date | Params | RAG quality | Tool use | License |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-4 Turbo | 2023-11 | undisclosed | strong | strong | API |
| Claude 3 Opus | 2024-03 | undisclosed | strong | added later | API |
| **Command R+** | **2024-04** | **104B** | **best open** | **native** | **open (CC-BY-NC)** |
| Llama 3 70B | 2024-04 | 70B | RAG via prompt | needs scaffolding | open commercial |
| Mixtral 8x22B | 2024-04 | 141B | OK | needs scaffolding | Apache 2.0 |

Command R+'s niche — best-in-class open RAG + tool use — was the differentiator. Generic-quality benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval) were strong but not leading.

## The Cohere strategic position

Cohere is the only major LLM lab founded by Google Brain alumni (Aidan Gomez, an author of "Attention Is All You Need") that explicitly chose enterprise B2B positioning over consumer-AI. The company has stayed relatively small (~300 employees by 2025) and avoided consumer-product launches, focusing exclusively on the B2B funnel. By 2026, this positioning has produced a stable but smaller business than the flagship labs — which Cohere's leadership has framed as deliberate.

nodes.title: Llama 3 — Meta's Frontier-Comparable Open Model

model_spec.training_tokens: 15T (>5× Chinchilla-optimal — deliberate over-training for inference efficiency)

feels_like: Major leap over Llama 2 — 70B operates in the same 'weight class' as GPT-4 and Claude 3 Sonnet at release
Climbed to 5th place on the LMSYS leaderboard within weeks — first open-weights model to genuinely compete with GPT-4
128K-token vocabulary tokenizer noticeably improved encoding efficiency vs Llama 2
Trained on 15T tokens (7x Llama 2) with 4x more code — coding feel is dramatically better
Strong human-preference rankings vs comparable closed models in real-world scenarios
8B variant sets a new bar for laptop-class quality, especially after instruction tuning
best_for: Open-weights production deployments where Llama 2 was the previous baseline
Self-hosted RAG, code-completion, and chat applications under Llama 3 license
Fine-tuning on private corpora (the open license makes vertical specialisation safe)
Research and academic work that needs an open frontier-tier baseline
not_ideal_for: Frontier reasoning leaderboards by late 2025 — superseded by Llama 3.1/3.3/4 and competitors
Multimodal tasks — text-only at this generation (Llama 3.2 Vision came later)
Workflows blocked by Meta's acceptable-use clauses (>700M MAU restriction, etc.)

public_view.plain_english:
Meta's third-generation open-weight model, trained on 15 trillion
tokens — over 7× more than Llama 2. The 70B variant matched or
approached GPT-4 and Claude 3 Sonnet on most benchmarks while
being downloadable for free with a permissive license. Followed
in July 2024 by Llama 3.1 with a 405B version that explicitly
targeted GPT-4 / Claude 3.5 parity.
public_view.analogy:
The first open-weight model that could plausibly replace a closed
frontier API in production use cases — like the moment Linux
became viable for serious enterprise workloads, but for AI.

applications.product: WhatsApp / Instagram / Facebook Meta AI assistant
Together AI, Anyscale, Fireworks hosted endpoints

public_view.investment_angle:
Llama 3 is the strongest evidence to date that the closed-vs-open
gap is closing, not widening. Meta spent $20B+ in 2024 capex to
ship a model it gave away — a strategy that only makes sense if
open weights are a defensive moat against OpenAI/Microsoft and
Google (denying them pricing power) and an offensive distribution
play (Meta AI is now embedded in WhatsApp / Instagram with 3B+ MAU).
Drove inference-pricing collapse: Llama 3 70B served at <$1 per
1M output tokens by mid-2024, vs Claude Opus at $75. The "open
pulls down closed prices" thesis became operating reality.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Llama 3's release cadence and quality made open weights the
structural baseline for LLM economics. Any closed model now has
to justify its premium against a free open alternative within
one to two quarters of release. This pricing pressure is the
single biggest factor reshaping the LLM business in 2024-26.

nodes.body_md:
## Llama 3 vs concurrent peers (Q2 2024)

| Model | Date | Org | Params | MMLU | HumanEval | GSM8K | License | Cost / 1M out |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-4 Turbo | 2023-11 | OpenAI | undisclosed | ~86% | ~85% | — | API | $30 |
| Claude 3 Opus | 2024-03 | Anthropic | undisclosed | 86.8% | 84.9% | 95.0% | API | $75 |
| Gemini 1.5 Pro | 2024-02 | Google | undisclosed | 83.7% | 71.9% | 91.7% | API | $21 |
| **Llama 3 70B** | **2024-04** | **Meta** | **70B** | **82.0%** | **81.7%** | **93.0%** | **open** | **~$0.90 (Together)** |
| Llama 3.1 405B | 2024-07 | Meta | 405B | 88.6% | 89.0% | 96.8% | open | ~$5 (Together) |
| Claude 3.5 Sonnet | 2024-06 | Anthropic | undisclosed | 88.7% | 92.0% | 96.4% | API | $15 |

The 405B variant approaches Claude 3.5 Sonnet quality at one-third the price (when self-hosted via Together / Fireworks). For high-volume workloads this is the operative comparison.

## What 15T tokens of training data looks like

Meta's data card disclosure:
- **>5% non-English** (vs <2% for prior open models)
- **>4% code** in pretraining (Code Llama-style)
- ~50% deduplicated CommonCrawl
- Quality filtering using Llama 2 itself as a classifier

The data composition + scale is the technical moat. Meta does not disclose the specific quality filters, which are widely considered the highest-leverage piece.

## Llama 3.1 405B — the open frontier

Released July 23, 2024 with Meta's "Open Source AI Is the Path Forward" manifesto from Mark Zuckerberg. Specs:
- 405B params, 16k context (extended from 8k)
- Trained on H100 GPUs, ~16k of them, ~30M GPU-hours total
- First open-weight model whose training compute exceeded GPT-4's estimated training compute
- Cost to host: ~$5/1M output tokens at Together AI launch pricing

405B is the line in the sand: open weights at GPT-4-class scale, free to download, free to commercial-use (subject to the >700M MAU carve-out).

nodes.title: Phi-3 — Microsoft's Small Models, Big Quality

model_spec.architecture: Decoder-only transformer trained on heavily-filtered + synthetic data

benchmarks.name: Inference: tokens / second on iPhone 14
benchmarks.vs_baseline: first GPT-3.5-class model practical on consumer phones

feels_like: Phi-3-mini (3.8B) runs entirely on a phone yet matches GPT-3.5-class quality on common reasoning benchmarks
First widely-deployed open SLM that proved high-quality synthetic + filtered data beats raw scale
Outperforms models same-size and one-tier-up on language, reasoning, coding, and math benchmarks
Available across Azure AI Studio, Hugging Face, and Ollama — meant for laptop / edge from day one
Multilingual coverage is weaker than larger OSS models — primarily English-tuned
Knowledge-heavy queries (history, trivia) clearly limited by parameter count
best_for: On-device / phone / laptop deployments where no GPU is available
Edge inference for IoT, mobile apps, offline assistants
Reasoning-driven workflows where fine-tuned synthetic data beats general-knowledge breadth
Distillation targets and benchmark baselines for SLM research
not_ideal_for: Broad-knowledge chat — limited factual recall by design
Multilingual production beyond English
Tasks where frontier-tier reasoning matters (use Phi-4 or larger models)

public_view.plain_english:
Microsoft's series of small foundation models — 3.8B / 7B / 14B
parameters — that punched far above their weight by training on
aggressively filtered "textbook-quality" data plus synthetic
examples generated by GPT-4. The 3.8B Mini variant reaches
GPT-3.5-class quality while running on a smartphone — making
it the foundation of much on-device AI in 2024-25.
public_view.analogy:
The "data quality is the moat, not parameter count" thesis —
proved at scale. Where DeepSeek's frame is "smarter
architecture," Phi's frame is "smarter data." Both reduce the
importance of raw compute as the determinant of capability.
public_view.investment_angle:
Phi-3 validated the on-device AI investment thesis that
Apple, Qualcomm, and Microsoft were all betting on with
Copilot+ PC, Apple Intelligence, and Snapdragon X. The
model line also gave Microsoft a counter-narrative to the
"OpenAI is doing all the AI work for Microsoft" framing —
Phi was Microsoft Research's own published-paper
contribution, demonstrating internal capability beyond the
OpenAI partnership. Subsequent Phi-3.5 and Phi-4 (2024-25)
extended the methodology.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Phi-3 is the proof point that small models can be useful.
The "small specialized model" segment (Phi, Gemma, Llama
3.2-1B/3B, Qwen 0.5B/1.5B, Apple's on-device models)
is now a real category, and Phi was the trigger.

nodes.body_md:
## Phi-3 vs same-tier peers (Q2 2024)

| Model | Date | Params | MMLU | HumanEval | On-device? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mistral 7B | 2023-09 | 7B | 60.1% | 30.5% | yes (4-bit quant) |
| Llama 2 7B | 2023-07 | 7B | 45.3% | 12.8% | yes |
| Gemma 7B | 2024-02 | 7B | 64.3% | 32.3% | yes |
| **Phi-3 Mini** | **2024-04** | **3.8B** | **68.8%** | **59.1%** | **yes (smartphone)** |
| Llama 3 8B | 2024-04 | 8B | 66.6% | 62.2% | yes |
| Phi-3 Medium | 2024-04 | 14B | 78.0% | 62.2% | yes (desktop) |

Phi-3 Mini's MMLU at 3.8B exceeds Llama 3 8B at half the parameters — the "data quality" thesis at its strongest single-data-point.

## Lineage

| Generation | Date | Innovation |
|---|---|---|
| Phi-1 / 1.5 | 2023-Jun/Sep | initial "textbooks are all you need" experiments |
| Phi-2 | 2023-12 | 2.7B model competitive with 7B Mistral |
| **Phi-3** | **2024-04** | **scale to 3.8B / 7B / 14B with strong results** |
| Phi-3.5 | 2024-08 | refresh; Mini-Instruct, MoE 16x3.8B variants |
| Phi-4 | 2024-12 | 14B; further reasoning emphasis |

nodes.title: DeepSeek-V2 — Open MoE with Multi-Head Latent Attention

model_spec.architecture: MoE + Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA) — KV cache compression via low-rank joint projection

benchmarks.name: API cost / 1M output
benchmarks.vs_baseline: GPT-4 Turbo $30 (107× cheaper), Claude Sonnet $15 (54× cheaper)

feels_like: First DeepSeek model that the wider OSS community took seriously — 236B MoE with only 21B active per token
Saved 42.5% training cost vs DeepSeek 67B, reduced KV cache 93.3%, boosted max throughput 5.76x
DeepSeekMoE architecture (fine-grained experts + shared expert isolation) became the template
128K context window at open-weights pricing — rare in mid-2024
MMLU performance in the top open-source tier despite the small active-parameter footprint
Foreshadowed V3 / R1 — proved the cost-efficient training playbook before it shocked Wall Street
best_for: Self-hosted Chinese / multilingual deployments where API access was politically risky
Cost-sensitive bulk inference via budget providers
MoE-architecture research and KV-cache optimisation experiments
Long-context retrieval workflows (128K) at open-weights price
not_ideal_for: Frontier reasoning by 2025 — superseded by V3 / V3.1 / V4 generations
Edge / single-GPU deployments (236B MoE still demands multi-GPU serving)
Vision / multimodal tasks (DeepSeek-VL came separately)

sources.name: BentoML — Complete guide to DeepSeek models

public_view.plain_english:
A Chinese AI lab's 236B-parameter Mixture-of-Experts model,
released open-weight with a new attention architecture (MLA) that
dramatically reduced inference memory. Its API was priced at
$0.28 per million output tokens — two orders of magnitude cheaper
than GPT-4 Turbo. The pricing itself was the news: DeepSeek had
broken the perceived floor of frontier-quality LLM inference
pricing.
public_view.analogy:
Where Llama made open-weight LLMs practical, DeepSeek V2 made
open-weight frontier-quality LLMs *cheap* — priced at levels that
made every closed-model API vendor recalibrate their margins.
public_view.investment_angle:
DeepSeek V2's API pricing kicked off the Chinese LLM price war of
summer 2024 — Alibaba, Baidu, Tencent, ByteDance all cut prices
by 90%+ in the subsequent 60 days. More structurally, V2 proved
that "efficient MoE + aggressive inference optimization" could be
the moat, not just parameter count. The Multi-Head Latent
Attention innovation alone reduced KV cache by ~93% vs dense
attention, enabling the low pricing. Set up R1's January 2025
market-shaking release on the same architectural foundation.
public_view.why_it_matters:
DeepSeek V2 is the proximal cause of the global LLM inference
price collapse in 2024-25. Every frontier lab now has to explain
why they charge what they do — when a Chinese open model runs at
$0.28/1M tokens and meets 90% of enterprise quality needs.

citations.title: DeepSeek-V2: A Strong, Economical, and Efficient Mixture-of-Experts Language Model

nodes.body_md:
## DeepSeek V2 vs 2024 mid-year peers

| Model | Date | Org | Active params | MMLU | HumanEval | API $ / 1M out |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-4 Turbo | 2023-11 | OpenAI | undisclosed | 86.0% | ~85% | $30 |
| Claude 3 Opus | 2024-03 | Anthropic | undisclosed | 86.8% | 84.9% | $75 |
| Llama 3 70B | 2024-04 | Meta | 70B | 82.0% | 81.7% | ~$0.90 (Together) |
| GPT-4o | 2024-05 | OpenAI | undisclosed | 88.7% | 90.2% | $15 |
| **DeepSeek V2** | **2024-05** | **DeepSeek** | **21B** | **78.5%** | **81.1%** | **$0.28** |
| Qwen 2 72B | 2024-06 | Alibaba | 72B | 77.4% | 86.0% | ~$0.90 |

The economic frame: DeepSeek V2 delivered Llama-3-70B-class quality at ~⅓ the active-parameter inference cost (21B vs 70B), priced at ~⅓ of Together's Llama 3 hosting. End-to-end, ~9× cheaper than hosted Llama; ~100× cheaper than GPT-4 Turbo.

## What came next in the DeepSeek line

| Model | Date | Change |
|---|---|---|
| DeepSeek V2.5 | 2024-09 | improved coding + reasoning, 236B/21B |
| **DeepSeek V3** | **2024-12-26** | **671B/37B MoE, auxiliary-loss-free routing, $5.5M training cost** |
| DeepSeek R1 | 2025-01 | RL-only reasoning on V3 base |
| DeepSeek R1.5 | 2025-Q2 | refresh, tool-use integration |

V2 was the architectural foundation; V3 was the scale-up; R1 was the reasoning payoff. Each built directly on the prior generation's innovations.

nodes.title: AlphaFold 3 — All-Atom Biomolecular Prediction

feels_like: Vision-language model from DeepMind — see the linked sources below for benchmark and review coverage
Vision and multimodal tasks are the typical fit per the published model card
best_for: Vision tasks (charts, documents, images) per the model card
See the model spec and sources block for benchmarked use cases
not_ideal_for: Tasks far outside the modalities listed in this model's spec
Workflows where a more recent successor in the same family scores higher

public_view.plain_english:
DeepMind + Isomorphic Labs' May 2024 release — extends AlphaFold 2
from "predict the shape of one protein" to "predict the shape of
proteins together with DNA, RNA, drugs, and ions." This is what
you actually need for drug discovery, not just academic structural
biology. Released open for academic use through the AlphaFold
Server.
public_view.analogy:
AlphaFold 2 was a sculpture-of-the-protein generator. AlphaFold 3
is a sculpture-of-the-protein-with-drugs-bound-to-it generator —
the one drug discovery actually wanted.
public_view.investment_angle:
AlphaFold 3 is the centerpiece of Isomorphic Labs' drug-discovery
pipeline (DeepMind spinoff valued at ~$3B). Combined with
AlphaProteo (binder design) and AlphaMissense (variant effect),
DeepMind's biology stack is the leading AI-discovery platform.
Multiple pharmas have signed multi-year deals.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Made AI-driven structural biology actually useful for drug
discovery, not just basic science. Extended the reach of
AlphaFold's economic impact from "PDB completion" to "real
therapeutic pipelines." The 2024 chemistry Nobel went to David
Baker + Demis Hassabis + John Jumper for this lineage.

citations.title: Accurate structure prediction of biomolecular interactions with AlphaFold 3

nodes.body_md:
## What's new

| Capability | AlphaFold 2 | AlphaFold 3 |
|---|---|---|
| Single proteins | ✅ | ✅ |
| Protein-protein complexes | ✅ (multimer) | ✅ |
| Protein-DNA / RNA | ❌ | ✅ |
| Protein-ligand | ❌ | ✅ |
| Protein-ion | ❌ | ✅ |
| Diffusion architecture | ❌ (Evoformer) | ✅ |

## The DeepMind biology stack (2024-2026)

- AlphaFold 3 — structure prediction
- AlphaProteo — binder design
- AlphaMissense — variant effect prediction
- AlphaGenome — 1Mb-context DNA variant effects (June 2025)
- AlphaProof — math reasoning (also Nature, Nov 2025)

nodes.title: GPT-4o — Natively Multimodal OpenAI Frontier

feels_like: First end-to-end omni model — text, vision, audio share one neural net (not stitched pipelines)
2x faster, half the price, and 5x higher rate limits than GPT-4 Turbo
Native voice-to-voice latency around 320ms — close to human conversational rhythm (210ms)
Realtime API (Oct 2024) opened up always-on voice assistants for developers
Reasoning is solid but not o1-class — by 2025 it's the 'speed/cost tier', not the 'IQ tier'
GPT-4o mini (Jul 2024) became the GPT-3.5 Turbo replacement at much better quality
best_for: Voice-first applications and real-time multimodal interfaces
Cost-sensitive bulk inference where GPT-4-class quality is enough
Image understanding workflows — strong vision pipeline at API price
Replacing GPT-3.5/Turbo deployments with 4-tier quality at similar cost
not_ideal_for: Hard reasoning, math, or research — o1/o3/GPT-5 are the right tier
Frontier-leaderboard coding (Claude 3.5 Sonnet+ outscored GPT-4o on SWE-bench by mid-2024)
Self-hosted / open-weights workflows

sources.name: TechCrunch — OpenAI debuts GPT-4o omni model

public_view.plain_english:
OpenAI's successor to GPT-4, with a single unified model handling
text, audio, and images natively — instead of separate models
stitched together. The "omni" of the name refers to this
multimodal integration. Launched with a real-time voice mode
designed to mimic natural conversation, complete with interruption
handling and emotional tone. Made flagship-tier capability free
to consumer ChatGPT users for the first time.
public_view.analogy:
Earlier multimodal AI was a translator relay — text goes to a text
model, audio to a speech model, image to a vision model, and a
coordinator passes messages. GPT-4o collapses all three into one
native speaker.

applications.product: Free tier ChatGPT (GPT-4o rolled out to all users)

public_view.investment_angle:
GPT-4o made flagship LLM access free — a strategic move against
Gemini (free on google.com) and Claude (free tier with daily caps).
The free tier drove ChatGPT to ~200M WAU by late 2024 and
reinforced OpenAI's consumer moat. Voice mode seeded the "AI as
ambient interface" category that Apple Intelligence (also built
partly on GPT-4o) then distributed to ~1.5B iOS devices. The
GPT-4o → GPT-4o mini split in July 2024 established the new
tier-pricing floor: flagship capability at Haiku-tier prices
($0.15 per 1M input tokens).
public_view.why_it_matters:
GPT-4o ended the era when "using the best AI" meant paying $20/mo.
Once frontier capability became free with an email address, the
user population expanded by an order of magnitude — reshaping
regulatory scrutiny, labor-market debate, and education policy
everywhere.

nodes.body_md:
## Key spec deltas vs GPT-4 and peers

| Model | Date | Text MMLU | HumanEval | Audio | Vision | Context |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-4 | 2023-03 | 86.4% | 67.0% | cascaded | GPT-4V (bolt-on) | 8k/32k |
| GPT-4 Turbo | 2023-11 | — | ~85% | cascaded | native | 128k |
| Claude 3 Opus | 2024-03 | 86.8% | 84.9% | text-only | native | 200k |
| Gemini 1.5 Pro | 2024-02 | 83.7% | 71.9% | native | native | 1M |
| **GPT-4o** | **2024-05** | **88.7%** | **90.2%** | **native (~320ms)** | **native** | **128k** |
| GPT-4o mini | 2024-07 | 82.0% | 87.2% | native | native | 128k |
| Claude 3.5 Sonnet | 2024-06 | 88.7% | 92.0% | text-only | native | 200k |

GPT-4o was the frontier for ~3 weeks until Claude 3.5 Sonnet (2024-06-20) matched or exceeded it on most text and code benchmarks. The voice and free-tier distribution were the durable competitive moves; the benchmark lead was transient.

## The GPT-4o mini pricing reset

July 18, 2024: OpenAI launched GPT-4o mini at $0.15 / $0.60 per million input/output tokens — ~60% cheaper than Claude Haiku and cheaper than Llama 3 70B hosted at most inference platforms. Performance: MMLU 82.0%, HumanEval 87.2% — i.e., GPT-4 quality from 15 months earlier at 1/200th the price.

This was the single largest LLM price cut of 2024 and forced the entire tier to reprice within weeks (Claude Haiku → $0.25/$1.25, Gemini Flash → $0.075/$0.30). The floor of LLM inference pricing is now limited by inference compute cost, not by margin.

nodes.title: DeepSeek-Coder V2 — Open Coding Frontier

model_spec.parameters: 236B total / 21B active MoE; 16B and 6.7B sibling sizes

feels_like: First open-weights coding model to beat GPT-4 Turbo, Claude 3 Opus, and Gemini 1.5 Pro on code + math
236B MoE / 21B active — derived from DeepSeek-V2 with 6T extra tokens of code/math pre-training
HumanEval 90.2%, MBPP 76.2%, LiveCodeBench 43.4%, MATH 75.7% — frontier coding numbers at OSS price
Programming language support jumped from 86 to 338; context extended from 16K to 128K
16B Lite variant (2.4B active) brings most of the quality to a single-GPU deployment
Set the playbook later refined into DeepSeek V3 / R1 / V4 — coding-specialist via continued pre-training
best_for: Self-hosted code-completion engines and IDE integrations
Repository-level QA and refactor pipelines using open weights
Multilingual code (338 languages) including niche legacy ones
Cost-sensitive bulk inference for code review / lint suggestions
not_ideal_for: Frontier coding by mid-2025 — Claude Opus 4.5 / Sonnet 4.5 / DeepSeek V4 lead by wide margins
Edge / single-consumer-GPU deployments at the 236B scale (use Lite)
Non-coding chat — general dialogue is weaker than non-specialist DeepSeek models

public_view.plain_english:
DeepSeek's June 2024 coding model — 236B mixture-of-experts
activating 21B per token, fine-tuned for code in 338 programming
languages. At release, it beat Codestral 22B and matched closed-
source GPT-4 Turbo on HumanEval. Open under a custom permissive
license. The Coder V3 / V3.2 successors keep DeepSeek at the
open coding frontier.
public_view.analogy:
DeepSeek-V2 is the general model that learned medicine, law,
history. Coder V2 is the same brain that went to coding boot camp
and stayed there.
public_view.investment_angle:
DeepSeek-Coder V2 made open-weight coding competitive with closed
Copilot at the frontier. Combined with V3.2's coding mode and
DeepSeekMath-V2, DeepSeek owns the "open coding leader" position
that GitHub Copilot's pricing model can't easily defend against.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Pushed open-weight coding models past GPT-4 Turbo. By late 2024,
serious enterprise coding deployments started defaulting to
DeepSeek-Coder for self-hosted Copilot replacements.

citations.title: DeepSeek-Coder-V2: Breaking the Barrier of Closed-Source Models in Code Intelligence

nodes.body_md:
## What's distinctive

- **338 languages** — broadest language coverage in any open coding model
- **236B / 21B active MoE** — efficient inference relative to parameter count
- **128K context** — fits most repos

nodes.title: Claude 3.5 Sonnet — Mid-Tier Surpasses Prior Flagships

feels_like: The model that made AI coding actually trustworthy — produces working code nearly every time
49% on SWE-bench Verified at release; ahead of o1-preview and significantly past prior versions (33%)
Outperformed the larger Claude 3 Opus on most benchmarks while costing 80% less
92.0% on HumanEval — edges GPT-4o (90.2%) on Python function tests
Multi-step task completion 40-54% without human help — over half of long tasks still need course-correction
Some users report needing more back-and-forth on long-form outputs
best_for: Front-end and back-end web development — the canonical 2024 coding copilot
Refactors and feature work where reliability beats raw IQ
Tools-and-agents stacks (Cline / Claude Dev / Cursor) — became the default model
GitLab DevSecOps pipelines (10% reasoning gain, no slowdown)
not_ideal_for: Languages or frameworks outside its training distribution
Multi-hour autonomous tasks — prefer Claude 4 family
Hardest reasoning / math benchmarks — better on o1 / o3

sources.name: 16x Engineer — Is new Sonnet 3.5 best for coding?

public_view.plain_english:
A mid-tier Claude 3.5 model that, on release, beat the previous
flagship (Claude 3 Opus) on most benchmarks while charging 1/5
the price. Cemented Anthropic's reputation as the leading model
for code generation — the dominant Claude model in IDEs like Cursor
and Windsurf throughout 2024-25.
public_view.analogy:
The mid-tier sedan that outperforms last year's flagship at half
the price — an industry pattern that reset everyone's expectations
for how often mid-tier was supposed to leap.

applications.product: Claude.ai (default for Pro and free tier)
Windsurf, Cline, Aider, GitHub Copilot Workspace

public_view.investment_angle:
Claude 3.5 Sonnet was the model that captured the developer market
from OpenAI. By late 2024, Anthropic's API revenue split had shifted
notably toward enterprise developer tools — Cursor's $9B valuation
(April 2025) is a direct downstream effect. The introduction of
"artifacts" (interactive code/UI rendered alongside chat) defined
a new product UX that competitors copied within months. The 3.5
nomenclature itself (vs incrementing to "Claude 4") signaled
Anthropic's intent to ship faster cadence than version-major
bumps suggested.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Claude 3.5 Sonnet established that "mid-tier > prior flagship" at
cheaper pricing was the new release cadence — meaning customers
should expect 6-month obsolescence of the model they're building
on. This reframed enterprise procurement: build on the model
abstraction (provider, not version) and assume continuous quality
upgrade.

nodes.body_md:
## How it landed in the competitive matrix (mid-2024)

| Model | Date | MMLU | HumanEval | GPQA | MATH | Price ($/1M out) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-4 Turbo | 2023-11 | — | ~85% | 35.7 | — | $30 |
| Claude 3 Opus | 2024-03 | 86.8% | 84.9% | 50.4 | 60.1 | $75 |
| GPT-4o | 2024-05 | 88.7% | 90.2% | 53.6 | 76.6 | $15 |
| **Claude 3.5 Sonnet** | **2024-06** | **88.7%** | **92.0%** | **59.4** | **71.1** | **$15** |
| Llama 3.1 405B | 2024-07 | 88.6% | 89.0% | 51.1 | 73.8 | ~$5 (Together) |
| GPT-4o (Aug update) | 2024-08 | — | — | — | — | $15 |
| o1-preview | 2024-09 | 92.3% | 92.4% | 78.0 | 94.8 | $60 |
| Claude 3.5 Sonnet (new) | 2024-10 | — | 93.7% | 65.0 | 78.3 | $15 |

Claude 3.5 Sonnet held the "best model overall, in many users' practical experience" position from June 2024 until at least Q1 2025 — substantially longer than the typical SOTA tenure.

## Artifacts — a UX shipping co-product

Released alongside the model: the "artifacts" feature in claude.ai split the conversation into a chat panel (left) and a rendered output panel (right) that automatically displays generated code, UI mockups, SVG diagrams, or documents. This UX pattern was almost immediately copied by ChatGPT (Canvas, October 2024), Gemini (Canvas, late 2024), and several agentic IDEs.

The product lesson: model quality alone wasn't enough to win developer mindshare; the way model output was surfaced to the user mattered just as much. Anthropic's product velocity in 2024-25 — artifacts, projects, computer use, MCP — is a key part of the Claude commercial story not captured by benchmark deltas.

nodes.title: ESM-3 — Generative Protein Language Model

model_spec.architecture: Multi-track protein language model (sequence + structure + function)

feels_like: Language model from Meta AI — see the linked sources below for benchmark and review coverage
best_for: General-purpose tasks within Meta AI's deployment footprint
See the model spec and sources block for benchmarked use cases
not_ideal_for: Tasks far outside the modalities listed in this model's spec
Workflows where a more recent successor in the same family scores higher

public_view.plain_english:
EvolutionaryScale (Meta spinoff) released ESM-3 in June 2024 — a
98-billion-parameter protein language model that handles sequence,
structure, and function in a unified framework. Unlike AlphaFold,
ESM-3 is generative — it can DESIGN new proteins, not just
predict the shape of existing ones.
public_view.analogy:
AlphaFold reads existing proteins. ESM-3 writes new ones.
Different jobs, both essential to AI-driven biology.
public_view.investment_angle:
EvolutionaryScale spun out of Meta in 2024 with $142M+ in
funding from Lux Capital and Nat Friedman. ESM-3 + ESMFold is
the open-source protein backbone that competes with closed
AlphaFold-based pipelines. Open weights matter for biotech
workflows that don't fit Google/DeepMind's licensing.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Made high-quality generative protein design open. Combined with
Baker Lab's RFdiffusion + ProteinMPNN, established the open-
source protein-engineering toolkit that competes with closed
Google offerings.

citations.title: Simulating 500 million years of evolution with a language model

nodes.body_md:
## ESM family

| Version | Year | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| ESM-1 | 2019 | First protein LM at scale |
| ESM-2 | 2022 | 15B params; ESMFold structure prediction |
| **ESM-3** | 2024 | 98B; multi-track sequence + structure + function |

nodes.title: Mistral Large 2 — France's Closed-Frontier Flagship

feels_like: Mistral's most capable model at release — on par with GPT-4o, Claude 3 Opus, Llama 3 405B at 1/3 the parameter count
84.0% MMLU on the pretrained variant — set a new performance/cost Pareto point for open weights
Single-node inference: 123B dense fits on one GPU server — no MoE-routing complexity
Strong at function calling and parallel/sequential tool use — built for business app integration
Particularly good at following precise instructions and multi-turn dialogue
Open weights under Mistral Research License (commercial use needs paid agreement)
best_for: Multilingual production apps — French/German/Spanish/Italian/Arabic/Chinese/Japanese first-class
Function-calling agents and business integrations needing parallel tool calls
80+ programming languages including Python/Java/C++/JavaScript/Bash
Self-hosted deployments where Llama 3.1 405B is too large but smaller models lose quality
not_ideal_for: Frontier reasoning leaderboards — newer Opus / GPT-5 / DeepSeek R1 lead by significant margins
Free-to-use commercial deployments (research license requires payment for production)
Vision / multimodal tasks — text only at this generation

public_view.plain_english:
Mistral's July 2024 flagship — a 123-billion-parameter dense model
for the European market. Released under the Mistral Research
License (free for research, paid for commercial). Followed by
Mistral Medium 3 and Mistral Small 4 as Mistral built a tiered
product line to compete with OpenAI's GPT-4o family.
public_view.analogy:
Mistral's answer to GPT-4 Turbo — same general capability tier,
European-hosted, with weights you can audit if you have a research
license. Less censored, more open about training data origins.
public_view.investment_angle:
Mistral is the European sovereign-AI bet. Mistral Large 2 anchored
the closed-API tier; combined with open Mixtral 8x22B and the 2025
Magistral reasoning models, Mistral covers a Llama + Claude
portfolio under EU jurisdiction. Strategic value to French and EU
digital-sovereignty buyers regardless of pure benchmark position.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Established that a non-US, non-Chinese lab could ship a frontier
model. Mistral Large 2 was Europe's first independent frontier
LLM and the foundation of the EU's AI sovereignty argument.

nodes.body_md:
## Mistral product hierarchy

| Tier | Model | License |
|---|---|---|
| Flagship closed | Mistral Large 2 / Medium 3 / Small 4 | Research / Commercial |
| Open MoE | Mixtral 8x7B / 8x22B | Apache 2.0 |
| Open dense | Mistral 7B, Nemo 12B, Ministral 3B/8B | Apache 2.0 |
| Reasoning | Magistral | Apache 2.0 |
| Audio | Voxtral | Apache 2.0 |
| Multimodal | Pixtral 12B / Pixtral Large 124B | Apache 2.0 / Research |
| Code | Codestral 25.01 / Codestral Mamba / Devstral | Mixed |

nodes.title: AlphaProof — Math Olympiad Reasoning

model_spec.architecture: Gemini-based base model + RL fine-tuning + Lean 4 proof verification

benchmarks.name: International Mathematical Olympiad 2024
benchmarks.vs_baseline: first AI system at IMO medal-level performance

benchmarks.name: Days of self-RL training per problem

feels_like: Language model from DeepMind — see the linked sources below for benchmark and review coverage
Tool-use and agent loops are the typical fit per the published model card
best_for: Agent / tool-use workflows that match the model's published benchmarks
See the model spec and sources block for benchmarked use cases
not_ideal_for: Tasks far outside the modalities listed in this model's spec
Workflows where a more recent successor in the same family scores higher

public_view.plain_english:
DeepMind's mathematical reasoning system that, in July 2024,
achieved silver-medal-level performance on the International
Mathematical Olympiad — solving 4 of 6 problems used in the
actual 2024 IMO competition. AlphaProof works by translating
natural-language problems into the Lean 4 theorem prover, then
using reinforcement learning to search for proofs that the
prover verifies. The verifiable-reward signal is what makes
the RL tractable.
public_view.analogy:
The "AlphaGo for math proofs" — same DeepMind playbook of
"neural network proposes, formal system verifies, RL trains."
The IMO milestone played the same cultural role for math as
AlphaGo's Lee Sedol match did for Go.
public_view.investment_angle:
AlphaProof is not directly monetized but its methodology
transferred into Gemini 2.5 Pro's "thinking mode" and into
the broader frontier-lab consensus that "verifiable rewards
+ RL" is the right recipe for reasoning capability. DeepSeek
R1 (Jan 2025) used essentially the same insight on math /
code domains. The IMO result also triggered a wave of
"automated theorem proving" startup interest — a niche that
had been moribund since the 1990s.
public_view.why_it_matters:
AlphaProof is the proof-of-concept that AI can reach
expert-human performance on rigorous mathematical reasoning
given the right scaffolding (formal verification). The
implications for mathematical research, formal verification
of software, and "AI as scientific collaborator" are still
being explored.

citations.title: AI achieves silver-medal standard solving International Mathematical Olympiad problems

nodes.body_md:
## IMO 2024 results

| Problem | Type | AlphaProof / AlphaGeometry result | Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Algebra | ✓ correct proof | minutes |
| 2 | Algebra | ✓ correct proof | minutes |
| 3 | Combinatorics | ✗ unsolved | exceeded budget |
| 4 | Geometry (AlphaGeometry 2) | ✓ correct proof | seconds |
| 5 | Combinatorics | ✗ unsolved | exceeded budget |
| 6 | Algebra | ✓ correct proof (the hardest problem of the year) | days |

Total: 28/42 points — silver medal threshold (top ~150 of ~600 participating students). Gold medal threshold (top ~50) was 29 points, which AlphaProof missed by 1 point.

Notably: problem 6 was solved by AlphaProof but only ~5 of 600 human students did so. Problem 3 (combinatorics) defeated AlphaProof; combinatorics is typically the hardest area for formal-proof systems because the combinatorial search space is large.

## Lineage and influence

AlphaProof's technique — neural network proposes, formal system verifies, RL on the verified-correct examples — is essentially the same recipe DeepSeek R1 used 6 months later for general math/code reasoning (with code-execution / answer-checking as the verifier instead of Lean). The methodology transferred from "specialized formal-math system" to "general reasoning model" within roughly a year — one of the faster technique-transfer rates in the modern LLM era.

nodes.title: Perplexity Sonar — Search-Native Generation

model_spec.parameters: Sonar Small 8B / Sonar Large 70B / Sonar Huge undisclosed
