benchmarks.name: Citation accuracy (vs ungrounded LLM)
benchmarks.vs_baseline: raw GPT-4 ~50% (hallucination rate on factual queries)

benchmarks.name: Latency
benchmarks.score: <1s to first token incl. web search

feels_like: Language model from Perplexity — see the linked sources below for benchmark and review coverage
Tool-use and agent loops are the typical fit per the published model card
best_for: Agent / tool-use workflows that match the model's published benchmarks
See the model spec and sources block for benchmarked use cases
not_ideal_for: Tasks far outside the modalities listed in this model's spec
Workflows where a more recent successor in the same family scores higher

public_view.plain_english:
Perplexity AI's family of LLM variants explicitly tuned for
"answer this question and cite your sources." Released as
paid API endpoints (Sonar Small, Large, Huge) in 2024. The
underlying technique — Llama 3.1 fine-tuned for citation
quality and integrated tightly with Perplexity's web search
backend — became the default architecture behind any
consumer "AI search" product.
public_view.analogy:
The "AI as Wikipedia of the moment" — same role search
engines played in the 2000s, restructured around generating
natural-language answers instead of retrieving page lists.
Sonar is the LLM optimized for this specific use case.

applications.product: Sonar API (Apple Maps, Snapchat, Carrot Weather, etc.)

public_view.investment_angle:
Perplexity raised $500M Series C at $9B valuation in
November 2024 — a valuation premised on AI search as a
standalone category large enough to coexist with Google
Search. By 2025-26, Perplexity's positioning had evolved
toward "agentic browser" (the Perplexity Comet browser
launch) — extending from "search" to "do tasks for me on
the web." Sonar remains the model layer underneath both
cases. Strategic risk: Google's own AI Overviews + Gemini
integration in Search may close the differentiation.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Perplexity Sonar represents the "search-grounded LLM" as a
distinct model category — a specialization that generic
LLMs (GPT, Claude) approach but don't own as a primary
product surface. Whether AI search remains a separate
category or gets absorbed by general-purpose chat is the
open question.

nodes.body_md:
## Perplexity vs concurrent "AI search" peers

| Product | Date | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| Bing Chat / Copilot | 2023-02 | GPT-4 + Bing search |
| Google AI Overviews | 2024-05 | Gemini + Google Search; default-on |
| **Perplexity (consumer + Sonar API)** | **2022-12 / 2024-09** | **dedicated AI-search product + API** |
| You.com | 2022 | early entrant, struggled to differentiate |
| Phind | 2022 | dev-focused AI search |
| ChatGPT Search | 2024-10 | OpenAI's response — search inside ChatGPT |
| Claude Web Search | 2025-Q2 | Anthropic's response |

Perplexity's bet: search-as-primary-product can sustain a standalone company despite Google's distribution and OpenAI's brand. By 2026 the bet is partly working (revenue scaling, valuation holding) and partly under pressure (consumer AI-search has become table-stakes for every chat product).

nodes.title: OpenAI o1 — Inference-Time Reasoning

model_spec.architecture: Decoder-only transformer + RL on reasoning traces (specifics undisclosed)

feels_like: Preferred to GPT-4o by a large margin in data analysis, coding, and math — but not preferred on natural-language tasks
Genuinely novel: chain-of-thought is internalised, not prompted — model recognises and corrects its own mistakes
Slow — minutes for hard problems vs seconds for GPT-4o; not for chat
Hidden chain of thought is policy-restricted; users can lose access for trying to extract it
Apple's GSM-Symbolic study showed 17.5% perf drop with renamed variables — fragility lurks under the high benchmark scores
Token costs are heavy because of the reasoning trace, even when not shown to the user
best_for: Olympiad-level math, physics, and chemistry problems
Code that needs deep reasoning about correctness rather than fluency
Strategic / multi-step planning where you can wait minutes for an answer
Research workflows that benefit from explicit step-by-step decomposition
not_ideal_for: Real-time chat or low-latency tools — GPT-4o is faster
Open-ended writing, creative copy, or natural-language tasks
Cost-sensitive bulk inference — reasoning tokens are expensive
Use cases where you need to inspect or steer the chain of thought

sources.name: OpenAI — Learning to reason with LLMs
Simon Willison — Notes on OpenAI's new o1 chain-of-thought models

public_view.plain_english:
OpenAI's first model trained explicitly to "think before it answers"
— generating a long chain of internal reasoning before producing
its final response. On math olympiad and competitive-programming
problems, it crossed thresholds (top human percentiles) that prior
models couldn't approach. Trade-off: each query takes 10-60
seconds and costs 5-50× more than GPT-4o.
public_view.analogy:
Earlier models answered like contestants on a quiz show — fast,
confident, sometimes wrong. o1 answered more like a graduate
student writing a problem set — slower, with visible scratch
work, and substantially more accurate on hard problems.
public_view.investment_angle:
o1 opened a new scaling axis — inference-time compute — that doesn't
require larger pretraining runs. Implications: (1) future capability
gains are partly decoupled from $1B+ training spend, shifting
competitive ground from "who can afford the next model" to "who
builds the best reasoning RL pipeline"; (2) inference-time scaling
favors compute-constrained labs (Anthropic, DeepMind) and
challenges hyperscaler advantages (OpenAI/Microsoft); (3) the
economics of "spend more compute for higher-quality answers"
creates new monetization tiers (ChatGPT Pro at $200/mo). Triggered
rapid follow-on releases of reasoning models from every frontier
lab through 2025.
public_view.why_it_matters:
o1 ended the "scaling laws determine everything" era. After o1,
capability could be bought at inference time, not just training
time. This is the most important technical pivot in the LLM
landscape since GPT-3.

citations.title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models

nodes.body_md:
## Capability step-change in math and code

| Benchmark | GPT-4o | o1-preview | o1 | Human reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| AIME 2024 | 13.4% | 56.7% | 83.3% | top 500 in US ~75% |
| Codeforces | 11th %ile | 62nd %ile | 89th %ile | — |
| GPQA Diamond | 53.6% | 73.3% | 78.0% | PhD expert ~70% |
| MATH | 76.6% | 85.5% | 94.8% | — |
| LiveCodeBench | 38.2% | 76.5% | 89.0% | — |
| MMLU | 88.7% | 90.8% | 92.3% | — |

The "uniform" gains on text-only benchmarks (MMLU) are modest. The differential gains on verifiable-reward benchmarks (math, code, science) are extreme. This pattern — reasoning models help where there's a checkable answer, much less where there isn't — has held across all subsequent reasoning-model releases.

## Successor reasoning models (the wave o1 triggered)

| Model | Date | Org | AIME | GPQA | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **o1** | **2024-09** | **OpenAI** | **83.3%** | **78.0%** | first |
| o1-pro | 2024-12 | OpenAI | 86% | 79% | even longer thinking |
| o3 | 2025-01 (preview) | OpenAI | 96.7% | 87.7% | full release April 2025 |
| Claude 3.7 Sonnet | 2025-02 | Anthropic | 80.0% | 84.8% | extended-thinking mode |
| DeepSeek R1 | 2025-01 | DeepSeek | 79.8% | 71.5% | open-weight |
| Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking | 2025-01 | Google | 73.3% | 74.2% | low-latency reasoning |
| Qwen QwQ-32B | 2024-11 | Alibaba | 50.0% | 65.2% | open-weight reasoning |

DeepSeek R1's January 2025 release — open-weight reasoning model approaching o1 quality — was the strategic shock of the year, briefly knocking ~$600B off Nvidia's market cap as investors questioned closed-model moats.

## The hidden reasoning trace controversy

OpenAI showed users only a "summary" of the reasoning trace, not the trace itself. Stated rationale: prevent competitive scraping for distillation training. Researcher critique: the hidden trace makes safety audits, jailbreak research, and capability benchmarking structurally harder. As of mid-2025, no frontier reasoning lab has reverted to fully visible reasoning traces.

nodes.title: Qwen 2.5 — Open Frontier-Comparable from Alibaba

feels_like: First open-source model to top OpenCompass leaderboard — beat Llama 3.1 405B with 1/5 the parameters
72B-Instruct: 74.2 on coding, 77 on math — outscored Claude 3.5 Sonnet (72.1) and GPT-4o (70.6) at release
MMLU 85+, HumanEval 85+, MATH 80+ — frontier-tier across the board for an open-weights model
100+ models across the family (sizes from 0.5B to 72B, plus VL/Audio/Coder variants) — full ecosystem
Pre-training jumped from 7T to 18T tokens with heavy emphasis on knowledge / coding / math
Apache-2.0 license + competitive quality made it the de-facto open-source default in late 2024
best_for: Self-hosted production where Llama 3.1 405B is too big to serve
Multilingual deployments (29 languages with strong coverage)
Fine-tuning on private code or technical corpora
Distillation into smaller production models (the 7B/14B variants are excellent students)
not_ideal_for: Frontier reasoning by mid-2025 — Qwen 3 / DeepSeek R1 / Claude 4 series have moved past it
Vision tasks (use Qwen 2.5-VL) or audio (use Qwen 2.5-Audio)
Workflows where Western-platform compliance is contractual

sources.name: Alibaba Cloud — Qwen 2.5: A Party of Foundation Models

public_view.plain_english:
Alibaba's Qwen 2.5 generation, released September 2024, was the
first Chinese open-weight model family to exceed Llama 3.1 on
most benchmarks at comparable size. The release covered seven
sizes (0.5B → 72B) plus specialized variants for code (Coder)
and math, all under Apache 2.0. By late 2024, Qwen 2.5 had
overtaken Llama as the most-downloaded model family on Hugging
Face globally.
public_view.analogy:
The "Toyota strategy" of Chinese open LLMs — every market segment,
every quality tier, broad license, aggressive shipping cadence.
Where Llama is a sedan family, Qwen 2.5 is sedan + SUV + truck
+ pickup + scooter, all priced lower.

applications.product: Qwen-Coder default in many AI IDEs
Most-downloaded model family on Hugging Face by Q4 2024

public_view.investment_angle:
Qwen 2.5 was the pivot point where Chinese open-weight models
stopped being "good for the price" and became "best-in-class at
any price for the size." Combined with DeepSeek V2's pricing
revolution earlier in 2024, the Chinese open ecosystem effectively
set the global ceiling for what closed-model providers could
charge for non-frontier tasks. By end of 2024, Aliyun's reported
AI revenue was growing 3-4× YoY, much of it Qwen-driven inference.
The strategic pattern: don't try to beat OpenAI / Anthropic at
the absolute frontier; instead, dominate every tier just below
with open weights and aggressive cloud distribution.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Qwen 2.5 made it impossible for any serious enterprise procurement
decision in 2025 to skip the open-Chinese-model option. Even US
enterprises with concerns about Chinese-origin models routinely
benchmark against Qwen 2.5 / 3 to negotiate better pricing from
OpenAI / Anthropic. The market structural effect is large.

nodes.body_md:
## Specialized variants in the Qwen 2.5 family

| Variant | Sizes | Specialization |
|---|---|---|
| Qwen 2.5 (base) | 0.5B-72B | general purpose |
| Qwen 2.5-Instruct | 0.5B-72B | chat / instruction following |
| Qwen 2.5-Coder | 0.5B-32B | code generation; Coder-32B competitive with GPT-4o-mini on coding |
| Qwen 2.5-Math | 1.5B / 7B / 72B | math reasoning |
| Qwen 2.5-VL | 3B / 7B / 72B | multimodal vision |

The Coder and Math variants exemplify a productization pattern Western labs have largely avoided — domain-specific specialized model variants instead of one general flagship.

## Qwen 2.5-72B vs concurrent open peers (Q4 2024)

| Model | Date | Org | Params | MMLU | HumanEval | GSM8K | MATH |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Llama 3 70B | 2024-04 | Meta | 70B | 82.0% | 81.7% | 93.0% | 68.0% |
| Llama 3.1 70B | 2024-07 | Meta | 70B | 83.1% | 80.5% | 95.1% | 68.0% |
| Llama 3.1 405B | 2024-07 | Meta | 405B | 88.6% | 89.0% | 96.8% | 73.8% |
| **Qwen 2.5 72B** | **2024-09** | **Alibaba** | **72B** | **85.3%** | **86.6%** | **95.8%** | **83.1%** |
| Mistral Large 2 | 2024-07 | Mistral | 123B | 84.0% | 92.0% | 93.0% | 75.0% |
| DeepSeek V2.5 | 2024-09 | DeepSeek | 236B/21B | 80.4% | 89.0% | 89.3% | 74.7% |

Qwen 2.5-72B is the strongest dense open model at its size by a substantial margin in math and coding. The MATH benchmark in particular (+15 over Llama 3.1) was driven by Qwen 2.5-Math's training-data investment.

## The "AI tigers" funding context

By Qwen 2.5's release, Alibaba had positioned itself differently from China's "AI tiger" startups (Zhipu, Moonshot, Baichuan, MiniMax). Where the tigers raised hundreds of millions for narrow flagship products, Alibaba treated AI as infrastructure investment — open Qwen as ecosystem fuel, monetized through cloud (Aliyun GPU rental, model API, fine-tuning services) rather than direct model sales. This positioning turned out to be more sustainable as the AI tigers struggled with monetization in 2025.

nodes.title: Molmo — Allen AI's Open Vision-Language Model

model_spec.architecture: Vision encoder + Olmo / Qwen language backbone

feels_like: 72B variant outperforms GPT-4o (estimated 1T+ params) on image / chart / document understanding
7B variant comes close to GPT-4o-class performance — 'tiny model performs as well as powerful big ones' (MIT Tech Review)
Trained on a curated 600K-image dataset (PixMo) — quality-over-quantity proof point
All weights, training data, and inference code released — fully open, not just open-weights
First open VLM that put Allen AI on equal footing with closed-source frontier
Showed open-source vision is on par with proprietary in late 2024 — pivotal for the OSS narrative
best_for: Self-hosted vision tasks at fraction of API cost
Research and academic work needing full data + code access
Distillation experiments and small-VLM training baselines
Document QA, chart analysis, and image understanding without sending data out
not_ideal_for: Frontier visual reasoning by mid-2025 — Gemini 2.5 / 3 Pro and Claude 4 vision lead
Edge / single-laptop deployments at the 72B scale
Audio / video tasks (Molmo is image + text only at this generation)

sources.name: MIT Technology Review — tiny new open-source model performs

public_view.plain_english:
Allen AI's open vision-language family — released September 2024
with sizes from 1B to 72B. Trained with a small (1M-image)
high-quality dataset (PixMo), Molmo demonstrated that you don't
need internet-scale image-text pretraining to get strong VLMs.
Its signature trick: "pointing" — the model outputs (x,y)
coordinates to point at things in images.
public_view.analogy:
A vision model that uses its finger. Most VLMs describe an image
in words; Molmo can also point at the specific pixel where the
answer is.
public_view.investment_angle:
Molmo's "pointing" output reframes VLMs as components of
embodied / interactive systems, not just chat. Used heavily in
open robotics research where the model needs to specify a
grasping point or attention region. The Molmo 2 / MolmoAct
extensions (with Ai2's robotics work) make this a real toolchain.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Showed that high-quality small datasets + open weights beat the
Llava-style "scrape and pretrain" approach for many VLM tasks.
Molmo 72B held its own against Gemini 1.5 Pro and Claude 3 Opus
at release with a fraction of the data.

citations.title: Molmo and PixMo: Open Weights and Open Data for State-of-the-Art Multimodal Models

nodes.body_md:
## Family

| Variant | Backbone | Size |
|---|---|---|
| MolmoE-1B | OLMoE | 1B active |
| Molmo-7B-O | OLMo 7B | 7B |
| Molmo-7B-D | Qwen2 7B | 7B |
| Molmo-72B | Qwen2 72B | 72B |
| **Molmo 2** (Dec 2025) | OLMo / Qwen 3 | Multi-frame video reasoning |

nodes.title: π₀ — Physical Intelligence's Open Robot Foundation

feels_like: First open-weight Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model that genuinely competes with closed Google RT / Tesla / Figure stacks
Flow-matching action head (instead of autoregressive tokens) — generates continuous robot trajectories with physical smoothness
Folds laundry, busses dishes, packs groceries — the demo videos that convinced the field VLAs were real
3B parameters — small enough to run on edge robot compute, not in a datacenter
openpi GitHub repo lets any lab fine-tune for their gripper / arm / workspace
Successors (π₀.₅ open-world; π*0.6 experience-based RL) keep extending the open VLA frontier
best_for: Robotics research and academic VLA work
Startups building physical-AI products who need a foundation to fine-tune from
Long-horizon manipulation tasks (multi-step household / warehouse routines)
Distillation into smaller task-specific robot policies
not_ideal_for: Pure language reasoning, coding, or chat — π₀ is a robot policy, not an LLM
Tasks requiring out-of-distribution objects or scenes far from training data (improved in π₀.₅)
Real-time loops below ~10 Hz on commodity robot CPUs (π₀-FAST helps but still constrained)

public_view.plain_english:
Physical Intelligence's flagship vision-language-action model —
π₀ (October 2024) opened the open-weight VLA category. π₀.₅
(April 2025) added open-world generalization; π*0.6 (Nov 2025)
added experience-based RL. Released through the openpi GitHub
repo, with reference deployments on commodity robot arms.
public_view.analogy:
Pi is the Android of robot brains — open foundation that lab and
startup robots can fine-tune for their specific gripper /
workspace, instead of every team training from scratch.
public_view.investment_angle:
Physical Intelligence raised $400M+ at $2B+ valuation on the
open-weight robotics-foundation thesis. Pi sits one layer above
the hardware (which they also build) and one layer below
application (which their customers / partners build). The open
weights are a wedge to make Pi the default VLA stack.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Made open-weight VLA a real thing — before π₀, every serious VLA
was closed (Google's RT, Tesla's, Figure's). Pi proved you could
open-source the architecture and still build a business on
hardware + integration + commercial fine-tuning.

citations.title: π₀: A Vision-Language-Action Flow Model for General Robot Control

nodes.body_md:
## Pi family timeline

| Model | Date | What's new |
|---|---|---|
| **π₀** | Oct 2024 | First flow-matching VLA |
| **π₀-FAST** | Late 2024 | Latency-optimized variant |
| **π₀.₅** | Apr 2025 | Open-world generalization |
| **π*0.6** | Nov 2025 | Experience-based RL |

nodes.title: Hunyuan-Large — Tencent's First Open Frontier MoE

feels_like: Largest open-source Transformer-based MoE at release: 389B total / 52B active per token
MMLU 88.4% — beat Llama 3.1 70B (79.3%) and matched Llama 3.1 405B at one-tenth the active params
GSM8K 92.8% — surpassed Llama 3.1 405B's 89% on grade-school math
Native 256K context window — long-doc QA at open-weights pricing
1.5T of 7T training tokens were synthetic — first major open-weights model to lean this hard on synthetic
Mixed-expert routing innovations carried into later open MoE work (DeepSeek V3, etc.)
best_for: Self-hosted enterprise deployments where 389B MoE is acceptable
Long-context analysis (256K) on Chinese / English bilingual content
Distillation source — synthetic-data heritage makes outputs friendly for student training
Research on mixed-expert routing and synthetic-data scaling
not_ideal_for: Edge / single-GPU deployments — multi-node serving required
Frontier reasoning by 2025 — DeepSeek V3, Qwen 3, Kimi K2 surpass it
Multimodal tasks (text-only at this generation; vision is in Hunyuan-V or Hunyuan-Video)

public_view.plain_english:
Tencent's first major open-weight large model — a 389-billion-
parameter mixture-of-experts that activates 52B per token. Released
Nov 2024, it brought Tencent into the same league as DeepSeek and
Alibaba on the open frontier.
public_view.analogy:
Mixtral with 50× the parameters. Same idea — only some "experts"
work on each input — at a scale only hyperscalers had matched
until then.
public_view.investment_angle:
Tencent's late but full entry into the open-weight LLM race.
Hunyuan-Large was the proof point that Tencent could match
DeepSeek / Alibaba at scale; the follow-on Hunyuan-T1, A13B, and
Hunyuan 2.0 (Dec 2025, 406B/32B-active) build on this base.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Made it three Chinese hyperscalers (Alibaba, DeepSeek/Tencent)
shipping frontier-scale open MoE — a market structure unique to
China through 2024–2026.

citations.title: Hunyuan-Large: An Open-Source MoE Model with 52 Billion Activated Parameters

nodes.body_md:
## What it shipped

- 389B total / 52B active MoE — at release, the largest open MoE
- Cross-Layer Attention (CLA) for KV-cache compression
- 256K context, training on 7T+ multilingual tokens
- Followed by Hunyuan-Turbo (proprietary), Hunyuan-T1 (reasoning, March 2025), Hunyuan-A13B open MoE (June 2025), and Hunyuan 2.0 (406B/32B active, Dec 2025)

nodes.title: Pixtral Large — Mistral's 124B Multimodal Frontier

feels_like: 124B open-weights multimodal model built on Mistral Large 2 — frontier-level image understanding
Best open-weights model on the LMSys Vision Leaderboard at release — beats nearest competitor by ~50 ELO
Pixtral 12B (precursor) hit 52.5% on MMMU at 12B scale, beating much larger models
Charts, figures, document QA, and instruction following are the noted strengths
Inherits Mistral Large 2's text capabilities — multimodal without compromising text
Inference 30-50% slower than text-only models at the same parameter count — vision encoder overhead
best_for: Open-weights vision tasks where Claude Sonnet / GPT-4o aren't acceptable
Document QA, chart understanding, and figure analysis at frontier quality
Self-hosted multimodal RAG and OCR-heavy workflows
Research and academic work needing an open-weights vision baseline
not_ideal_for: Latency-sensitive vision workflows — encoder overhead is significant
Edge / single-GPU deployments at 124B scale
Tasks needing precise counting or fine-grained visual measurement (a noted weakness)

sources.name: DEV Community — Benchmarking Pixtral Large vs 12B

public_view.plain_english:
Mistral's frontier-tier multimodal model — Mistral Large 2 with a
dedicated 1B vision encoder. At launch (Nov 2024), it led MathVista
(visual math reasoning) and matched closed-source frontier on most
visual tasks. Open-weight under the Mistral Research License.
public_view.analogy:
Pixtral is the visual brain bolted onto Mistral's text reasoner.
A specialized eye that feeds detailed observations into a large
general-purpose mind.
public_view.investment_angle:
Vision capabilities have become table stakes for serious LLM
products. Pixtral Large gave Mistral a credible vision answer to
GPT-4o and Claude 3.5; the smaller Pixtral 12B (Apache 2.0) is a
favorite for self-hosted RAG-with-images workflows.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Showed that visual reasoning (chart understanding, diagram parsing,
document QA) could beat closed frontier models on specific tasks
while shipping open weights — particularly for math-heavy visual
benchmarks.

nodes.body_md:
## What it shipped

- 124B total params (123B Mistral Large 2 base + 1B vision encoder)
- Native variable-resolution image input
- Pixtral 12B sibling under Apache 2.0 — smaller, popular for self-hosted vision RAG
- Followed by Mistral's broader push into multimodal across Voxtral (audio) and Magistral (reasoning)

nodes.title: Model Context Protocol — Open Tool / Context Standard

benchmarks.name: Servers / integrations published (community)
benchmarks.vs_baseline: first protocol with substantial multi-vendor adoption in agentic AI

feels_like: Language model from Anthropic — see the linked sources below for benchmark and review coverage
Tool-use and agent loops are the typical fit per the published model card
best_for: Agent / tool-use workflows that match the model's published benchmarks
See the model spec and sources block for benchmarked use cases
not_ideal_for: Tasks far outside the modalities listed in this model's spec
Workflows where a more recent successor in the same family scores higher

public_view.plain_english:
An open protocol Anthropic released in November 2024 for
standardizing how AI agents talk to external tools, data
sources, and contextual services. Instead of every AI app
integrating every tool individually (M apps × N tools = M×N
integrations), MCP lets any tool publish an MCP server once
and any AI app consume it. Quickly adopted by OpenAI, Google,
cursor / IDE vendors, and thousands of community
integrations.
public_view.analogy:
The "USB for AI" analogy is the one Anthropic used in the
launch post. Before USB, every peripheral had its own
cable / driver / connector; after USB, one standard worked
across the industry. MCP plays the same role for the
AI-to-tool integration layer.

applications.product: Thousands of community MCP servers (Slack, Linear, GitHub, databases, etc.)

public_view.investment_angle:
MCP's adoption trajectory was the surprise of late-2024 / 2025
AI infrastructure. By accepting an Anthropic-originated
standard, OpenAI and Google effectively conceded the
protocol-design layer — choosing interop over dominance.
Strategically: if AI agents become the dominant
productivity-software interface, the protocol layer
underneath is comparable in importance to HTTP for the web.
Anthropic's positioning as the protocol's originator gives
it gravitational pull in the agent-developer ecosystem
even when other models match Claude's capabilities.
Several companies now build "MCP server marketplace"
businesses (similar to npm registries), and MCP-related
consulting / integration is a growing services category.
public_view.why_it_matters:
MCP is the most successful open standard to come out of
the LLM era. Whether it persists as the dominant agent
protocol over the next 5 years (vs being replaced by
something else) is the open question — but its current
momentum makes it the de facto standard for agent
integration in 2025-26.

nodes.title: OLMo 2 — Allen AI's Fully Open Foundation

feels_like: Language model from Allen AI — see the linked sources below for benchmark and review coverage
best_for: General-purpose tasks within Allen AI's deployment footprint
See the model spec and sources block for benchmarked use cases
not_ideal_for: Tasks far outside the modalities listed in this model's spec
Workflows where a more recent successor in the same family scores higher

public_view.plain_english:
The Allen Institute's truly-open language model — not just weights,
but the training data (Dolma), the training code, intermediate
checkpoints, and evaluation suites. OLMo 2 (and the instruction-
tuned Tulu 3 family) is what most academic ML researchers use when
they need a model they can REPRODUCE, not just download.
public_view.analogy:
Llama is "we'll show you the cake." OLMo is "here's the cake, the
recipe, every ingredient with sourcing, and a video of us baking
it." The full reproducibility difference matters for science even
when product capability is similar.
public_view.investment_angle:
OLMo's economic impact is in the academic + government tier where
full reproducibility is required. Doesn't compete head-to-head
with Llama or Qwen for product, but defines the upper bound on
"open enough for science." Used heavily by safety / interpretability
research labs (Anthropic interp team, Apollo Research, etc.).
public_view.why_it_matters:
Pushes the definition of "open source AI" — exposes how partial
Llama, Mistral, and Qwen open releases really are. Ai2's continued
pressure (Tulu 3 405B, Molmo, OLMoE) anchors the open-science end
of the model spectrum.

nodes.body_md:
## What "fully open" means

| Layer | Llama 3 | Qwen 3 | OLMo 2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Final weights | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Training code | partial | partial | ✅ |
| Training data | ❌ | partial | ✅ (Dolma) |
| Intermediate checkpoints | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Evaluation harness | ❌ | partial | ✅ |
| Apache / MIT license | ❌ (custom) | mostly | ✅ Apache |

The OLMo / Tulu / Molmo / OLMoE stack is the only complete open-source pipeline at modern frontier scale.

nodes.title: QwQ-32B Preview — Alibaba's Open Reasoning Model

model_spec.architecture: Qwen 2.5-32B base + RL on reasoning traces

feels_like: Language model from Alibaba — see the linked sources below for benchmark and review coverage
Tool-use and agent loops are the typical fit per the published model card
best_for: Agent / tool-use workflows that match the model's published benchmarks
See the model spec and sources block for benchmarked use cases
not_ideal_for: Tasks far outside the modalities listed in this model's spec
Workflows where a more recent successor in the same family scores higher

public_view.plain_english:
Alibaba's first open-weight reasoning model, released November
2024 as a "preview." Used the same RL-on-reasoning-traces recipe
OpenAI used for o1, but with the model weights downloadable —
making it the first time anyone outside the frontier US labs
could study how reasoning capability is trained in. Its release
8 weeks before DeepSeek R1 partly set the expectation that
"open reasoning" would arrive within months, not years.
public_view.analogy:
The early-access preview that proved the recipe worked outside
OpenAI. Like Linux's early-90s kernel — not the final article,
but the demonstration that ended the "this can only be done
inside one company" framing.
public_view.investment_angle:
QwQ-32B's release signaled that open-weight reasoning was no
longer a research aspiration but a near-term commercial reality.
Combined with DeepSeek R1's January 2025 release, it made clear
that closed reasoning models (o1, o1-pro) would face open
competition within the same generation, not the next. This
structurally limits the pricing power of o-series APIs and
explains why OpenAI accelerated o3 disclosure timing in early
2025.
public_view.why_it_matters:
QwQ proved that the o1 recipe was reproducible from public
research alone — no OpenAI insider knowledge required. That
proof, more than the model's specific capabilities, was the
contribution. By Q1 2025, ten different labs had released
reasoning models; the technique had become a shared methodology.

citations.title: QwQ: Reflect Deeply on the Boundaries of the Unknown

nodes.body_md:
## QwQ-32B-Preview vs o1-preview vs alternatives

| Model | Date | Org | Params | AIME | MATH-500 | GPQA Diamond | Open? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-4o | 2024-05 | OpenAI | undisclosed | 13.4% | 76.6% | 53.6% | API |
| Claude 3.5 Sonnet | 2024-06 | Anthropic | undisclosed | 16.0% | 71.1% | 59.4% | API |
| o1-preview | 2024-09 | OpenAI | undisclosed | 56.7% | 85.5% | 73.3% | API |
| o1 | 2024-09 | OpenAI | undisclosed | 83.3% | 94.8% | 78.0% | API |
| **QwQ-32B-Preview** | **2024-11** | **Alibaba** | **32B** | **50.0%** | **90.6%** | **65.2%** | **open** |
| DeepSeek R1 | 2025-01 | DeepSeek | 671B/37B | 79.8% | 97.3% | 71.5% | open |
| QwQ-32B (final) | 2025-03 | Alibaba | 32B | 79.5% | — | — | open |

QwQ-32B-Preview was the first open model to clearly exceed GPT-4o-class non-reasoning models on math, demonstrating that the reasoning RL recipe added meaningful capability that scaling alone hadn't.

## What QwQ contributed methodologically

While the formal training recipe was not published, the release showed:

- A 32B base model could host the reasoning capability — no need for GPT-4-class compute.
- RL on verifiable-reward problems (math, code) generalized somewhat to non-verifiable reasoning (the GPQA improvement, though smaller, was real).
- The "extended thinking" outputs were highly verbose (often 5,000-15,000 tokens for one response) — the inference-time cost trade-off was real.

These findings preview what DeepSeek R1's much more detailed training disclosure would document in January 2025.

nodes.title: Phi-4 — Microsoft's Synthetic-Data Small Model

model_spec.architecture: Decoder-only transformer; trained heavily on synthetic data

feels_like: 14B parameters that outperforms models 5x larger on math and reasoning — synthetic-data scaling laws in action
MMLU 84.8% (up from Phi-3's 77.9%); MATH 56.1% (up from 42.5%) — large quality jumps without size jump
HumanEval 82.6% — competitive with much larger frontier-tier models on Python code generation
Phi-4-reasoning variants beat o1-mini and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B on most reasoning benchmarks
Phi-4-reasoning-plus surpasses full DeepSeek-R1 on AIME 2025 — 14B beating 671B is the headline
Trained on 9.8T tokens over three weeks, mixing GPT-4o-generated synthetic data with curated web text
best_for: On-device reasoning where larger models can't fit
Math, science, and code benchmarks at SLM cost
Self-hosted production where 14B inference is affordable on a single GPU
Distillation experiments and SLM research baselines
not_ideal_for: Broad-knowledge factual QA — small models still have limited recall
Multimodal tasks (Phi-4 base is text-only; vision lives in Phi-4-multimodal)
Workloads needing 100K+ context (limited window vs frontier models)

sources.name: Microsoft — Phi-4 small models that pack a punch

public_view.plain_english:
Microsoft's December 2024 small model — only 14 billion parameters
but matching GPT-4o on reasoning benchmarks. The trick: Phi-4 was
trained mostly on SYNTHETIC data — examples generated by a larger
model and filtered for quality. It's a proof point that data
quality matters more than raw web-scraped quantity.
public_view.analogy:
Most LLMs read the entire internet (a noisy diet). Phi-4 was raised
on a curated diet of textbook-quality examples generated to cover
exactly the skills it would be tested on.
public_view.investment_angle:
Phi-4 changed the procurement math for SLM (small language model)
deployments — 14B running on a single GPU at GPT-4o-class quality
reshaped on-prem and edge AI. Microsoft uses it as the anchor for
cheap inference in Azure and Copilot-cheap deployments. Followed
by Phi-4-reasoning-15B (March 2026) hitting frontier reasoning on
a single GPU.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Validated synthetic-data training at scale. Showed that closed
labs' web-scraping advantage matters less than once thought —
a small model + great data can beat a huge model + average data.

nodes.body_md:
## What's distinctive

- **14B dense** — small enough to fit on a single A100 in bf16
- **MIT license** — fully permissive for commercial use
- **Synthetic data heavy** — majority of training tokens are LLM-generated and filtered
- **Reasoning focus** — Phi-4-reasoning and Phi-4-reasoning-plus extend with o1-style chain-of-thought training

nodes.title: DeepSeek V3 — 671B Open MoE at $5.5M Training Cost

model_spec.architecture: MoE (256 routed + 1 shared expert, top-8) + MLA + FP8 mixed-precision training + auxiliary-loss-free load balancing

feels_like: 671B-param MoE (37B active per token) — the open-weights model that finally matched GPT-4 / Claude 3.5 Sonnet quality
82.6% HumanEval (coding) and 90.2% MATH-500 — outperformed GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, and Llama 3 on each
Trained for $5.576M on 2.788M H800 GPU hours — 1/100th of comparable proprietary training budgets
Auxiliary-loss-free load balancing was a real architectural innovation, not just scaling
MoE inference needs 8+ H100s; not laptop-friendly even though the weights are open
MMLU score of 88.5 was the highest open-source result at release
best_for: Self-hosted frontier-quality LLM for orgs that can't use proprietary APIs
Math, coding, and general knowledge benchmarks at open-source price
Fine-tuning and distillation into smaller production models
Cost-sensitive bulk inference via budget providers
not_ideal_for: Edge / single-GPU deployments — the MoE size is large despite low active params
Multimodal use cases — text-only
Latency-sensitive interactive chat (better with smaller open models like Qwen 2.5)

sources.name: BentoML — Complete guide to DeepSeek models

public_view.plain_english:
DeepSeek's December 2024 frontier release: a 671-billion-parameter
Mixture-of-Experts model approaching GPT-4o quality, with weights
open and a published training cost of $5.5M — one to two orders
of magnitude below US frontier estimates. Quietly released on
December 26, 2024, it would become the foundation for R1 four
weeks later and the source of the January 2025 Nvidia stock shock.
public_view.analogy:
Where Llama 3 brought open weights to GPT-4 territory at $20B+
of capex, DeepSeek V3 brought open weights to GPT-4o territory
at a publicly-reported price an undergraduate research budget
could (in principle) have funded. Whether the $5.5M number holds
up to scrutiny matters less than the fact that nobody could
refute it conclusively.
public_view.analogy_lite:
The first open frontier-class model trained for less than the
cost of a Manhattan condo.

applications.product: Hosted at every major inference platform within 2 weeks
applications.company: Together, Fireworks, Groq, Hyperbolic, Novita, etc.

public_view.investment_angle:
DeepSeek V3 set up the R1 release that crashed Nvidia's stock by
$600B on January 27, 2025. The investment-thesis attack: if a
Chinese lab can reach GPT-4o quality for $5.5M of training
compute, the assumption that frontier capability requires
$1B+ training runs (and therefore Nvidia GPU sales of similar
magnitude) breaks down. Subsequent independent estimates of
DeepSeek's actual fully-loaded compute spend ranged $50M-$1.6B
(including research, ablations, infrastructure). The fact that
no one could conclusively refute the $5.5M number — and that
even the higher estimates were 10-20× below US frontier
spend — was the structural attack on the AI capex thesis.
public_view.why_it_matters:
DeepSeek V3, not R1, is the model that actually changed the
economics. R1 added the reasoning headlines, but V3 was where
the cost shock originated. Every subsequent debate about US
export controls, AI capex sustainability, and Chinese AI
capability has V3 as a central data point.

nodes.body_md:
## Training cost claim

Per DeepSeek's technical report, full training cost decomposed as:

| Stage | GPU-hours (H800) | Cost @ $2/hr |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-training | 2.664M | $5.328M |
| Context extension | 0.119M | $0.238M |
| Post-training | 0.005M | $0.010M |
| **Total** | **2.788M** | **$5.576M** |

This excludes "the costs associated with prior research and ablation experiments on architectures, algorithms, or data" — a substantial caveat. Independent estimates of the fully-loaded cost (including research compute, infrastructure amortization, salaries) ranged $50M-$1.6B. The reported number is the marginal compute cost of the final training run only.

## DeepSeek V3 vs frontier peers (Q4 2024 / Q1 2025)

| Model | Date | Org | Active params | MMLU | HumanEval | MATH-500 | GPQA | API $ / 1M out |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-4o (Aug update) | 2024-08 | OpenAI | undisclosed | 88.7% | 90.2% | 76.6% | 53.6% | $15 |
| Claude 3.5 Sonnet (new) | 2024-10 | Anthropic | undisclosed | 88.7% | 93.7% | 78.3% | 65.0% | $15 |
| Llama 3.1 405B | 2024-07 | Meta | 405B | 88.6% | 89.0% | 73.8% | 51.1% | ~$5 (Together) |
| Qwen 2.5 72B | 2024-09 | Alibaba | 72B | 85.3% | 86.6% | 83.1% | 49.0% | ~$0.90 |
| **DeepSeek V3** | **2024-12** | **DeepSeek** | **37B** | **88.5%** | **82.6%** | **90.2%** | **59.1%** | **$1.10 / $0.27 cached** |
| o1 | 2024-09 | OpenAI | undisclosed | 92.3% | 92.4% | 94.8% | 78.0% | $60 |

V3 hit GPT-4o-class on MMLU, exceeded GPT-4o on math, was somewhat behind on coding. The pricing — $1.10 per million output tokens, with caching pushing it to $0.27 — was 14-55× below GPT-4o's API.

## What V3 led to

- **DeepSeek R1** (2025-01-20): RL post-training on V3 for reasoning capability; the version that triggered the global AI policy and market response.
- **DeepSeek V3.5 / V4** (2025): incremental refinements following the same playbook.
- **Industry follow-on**: every major open-weight lab (Qwen, Mistral, Llama 4) shifted toward MoE-default architectures in 2025; auxiliary-loss-free routing became standard.

V3 is the architectural ancestor of essentially all serious open-weight 2025 frontier work.

nodes.title: Codestral 25.01 — Mistral's Coding Model

feels_like: Language model from Mistral AI — see the linked sources below for benchmark and review coverage
Code-leaning workloads are the typical fit per the published model card
Tool-use and agent loops are the typical fit per the published model card

Agent / tool-use workflows that match the model's published benchmarks
See the model spec and sources block for benchmarked use cases
not_ideal_for: Tasks far outside the modalities listed in this model's spec
Workflows where a more recent successor in the same family scores higher

public_view.plain_english:
Mistral's January 2025 coding model — 22B parameters, 256K
context, 80+ programming languages. Codestral excels at
fill-in-the-middle (predicting code in the middle of a function,
not just appending). The Codestral Mamba sibling uses
Mamba state-space architecture for very long context, and
Devstral extends to agentic workflows.
public_view.analogy:
The European answer to GitHub Copilot — same use case (autocomplete
+ chat coding), open-weight, hosted in EU.
public_view.investment_angle:
Codestral is Mistral's commercial-coding play targeting
enterprises that need self-hostable Copilot replacements.
Combined with Devstral (agentic) and Codestral Mamba (long
context), Mistral covers the IDE coding / repo-level agent
spectrum without depending on US-hosted weights.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Codestral helped commoditize the "coding-specialized model"
category that previously belonged to GitHub Copilot and DeepSeek-
Coder. Now every major lab ships a coding variant.

public_view.plain_english:
A Shanghai startup's January 2025 open-weight model with a 4
million-token context window — the longest publicly available
at the time, twice Kimi's 2M and four times Gemini 1.5 Pro's 1M.
Achieved this by replacing most layers' standard attention with
"Lightning Attention" — a linear-time variant — and keeping
standard attention only every 8th layer to preserve quality on
short-context tasks.
public_view.analogy:
Where most LLMs are sprinters that get tired by the end of a
marathon, MiniMax-Text-01 changes the running technique itself
so it can keep going for 4× the distance — at some cost to peak
speed in the first kilometer.
public_view.investment_angle:
MiniMax raised >$1B at $2.5B+ valuation by 2024 as one of China's
"AI tigers." The MiniMax-Text-01 release was the company's first
open-weight foundation model — a strategic pivot from purely
closed consumer-product positioning. The Lightning Attention
architecture is a real technical contribution: most labs trying
to push beyond 1M tokens have hit quadratic-attention scaling
walls, and MiniMax's linear-attention hybrid demonstrated a
practical path forward. Anthropic's later 1M-context Claude
Opus 4.6 (and rumors of larger contexts in 2026) likely use
related techniques.
public_view.why_it_matters:
MiniMax-Text-01 is the proof point that >>1M context isn't just
a Google-DeepMind-scale-only feature — it's achievable with
architectural changes that any frontier lab can adopt. The
"context arms race" of 2024-25 has converged on hybrid linear /
standard attention as the default approach, with MiniMax as one
of the early demonstrations.

citations.title: MiniMax-01: Scaling Foundation Models with Lightning Attention

MiniMax's contribution beyond the headline number: by open-sourcing the architecture, they made the technique available for other labs to study and adapt.

public_view.plain_english:
Microsoft's January 2025 generative model for inorganic materials
— generates novel crystal structures with target properties
(band gap, magnetism, ionic conductivity). Published in Nature.
Combined with Microsoft's MatterSim (property simulator),
forms a closed-loop materials design system. Used by partners
in battery, semiconductor, and catalyst R&D.
public_view.analogy:
What RFdiffusion did for proteins, MatterGen does for crystals —
"design me a material with these properties" instead of "find
one in a database."
public_view.investment_angle:
Materials discovery is a 5-10 year R&D problem; AI-acceleration
tools like MatterGen are bets that the next-decade breakthroughs
in batteries / solar / catalysts will originate in AI-designed
candidates rather than human intuition + DFT scans. Microsoft is
positioning Azure as the cloud for AI4Science workloads.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Made generative materials discovery a real workflow rather than
a research demo. Combined with Orbital Materials, NVIDIA BioNeMo,
and academic Boltz / RFdiffusion, AI-for-materials is now an
investment category.

citations.title: A generative model for inorganic materials design

nodes.title: Doubao 1.5 Pro — ByteDance's Sparse MoE Flagship

Deep Thinking mode beats OpenAI o1-preview and o1 on AIME
Outperformed DeepSeek-V3, GPT-4o, and Llama 3-405B on MMLU-Pro and GPQA
Sparse MoE — activates only a subset per token, dense-model quality at fraction of compute
32K to 256K context windows — flexible for both chat and long-doc work
Released the day before DeepSeek R1 stole the cycle — under-marketed but technically competitive
best_for: Cost-sensitive bulk inference at frontier-tier quality (5x cheaper than DeepSeek)
Chinese-language production deployments via the Doubao consumer app

Workflows where regulatory restrictions on Chinese-cloud apply

public_view.plain_english:
ByteDance's flagship API model, released January 22, 2025 — one
day before DeepSeek R1 stole the global news cycle. Doubao 1.5
Pro was the model behind ByteDance's domestic chat product
(Doubao app) which had ~75M MAU by end of 2024, making it the
most-used AI assistant in China by user count.
public_view.analogy:
The "TikTok of Chinese AI" — distribution-first product strategy.
ByteDance built model capability after dominating user
attention, the inverse of the OpenAI / Anthropic playbook.

applications.product: Doubao consumer chat (most-used AI app in China)

public_view.investment_angle:
Doubao represents ByteDance's bet that consumer-AI distribution
moat (TikTok / Douyin user-graph + recommendation infrastructure)
transfers to AI assistant market share. By Q1 2025, Doubao MAU
rivalled ChatGPT's China-equivalent share. ByteDance's reported
AI capex in 2024 (~$11B) was the largest of any Chinese tech
company. The strategic message: model capability is necessary
but not sufficient — the distribution moat is now the wider
competitive moat in the consumer AI market.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Doubao is the consumer Chinese AI product the West underestimates
most. By distribution count, it's larger than any Western AI
consumer product outside ChatGPT. ByteDance's quiet success here
shapes the long-term competitive landscape more than the more
publicized DeepSeek / Qwen battles.

nodes.title: DeepSeek R1 — Open-Weight Reasoning Model

model_spec.architecture: Mixture-of-experts decoder-only + GRPO RL on reasoning traces

feels_like: First open-weights reasoning model that genuinely competes with o1 — broke the closed-source moat
~90% on advanced math benchmarks vs ~83% for GPT-4o; the chain-of-thought is fully visible
Trained for ~$5.5M on 2,048 H800s — proof you don't need $100M training runs to reach the frontier
Excellent on math/logic/coding; weaker on broad creative writing and multi-turn personality
Open weights mean you can self-host, fine-tune, distil — no API rate limits
January 2025 release sparked the 'DeepSeek moment' that hit Nvidia stock and reset cost expectations
best_for: Math proofs, logic puzzles, and step-by-step derivations where explicit reasoning helps
Coding and engineering tasks that benefit from chain-of-thought
On-prem / air-gapped deployments where API models can't go
Cost-sensitive bulk inference (≈10x cheaper than ChatGPT-class via providers)
Distillation into smaller open models for production
not_ideal_for: Casual chat, tone, or creative writing — ChatGPT/Claude feel more polished
Multimodal tasks (image / vision) — text-only model
Latency-sensitive UX — reasoning trace adds significant time

sources.name: MIT Technology Review — DeepSeek's top AI reasoning model despite sanctions
BentoML — Complete guide to DeepSeek models V3, R1, V4

public_view.plain_english:
A Chinese AI lab released an open-weight reasoning model that
approached OpenAI's o1 in quality, with training compute reportedly
a fraction of frontier US labs'. Released free with a permissive
license. Within a week of release, it triggered a $600B drop in
Nvidia's market cap as investors questioned whether closed-model
moats and US capex assumptions still held.
public_view.analogy:
The Sputnik moment for the closed-model US AI thesis. Within days,
every assumption about how much you needed to spend to reach the
frontier was being re-litigated in earnings calls.

applications.product: Self-hosted by every major inference platform

public_view.investment_angle:
R1's release was the largest single-day market shock in AI history.
Nvidia lost ~$600B in market cap on January 27, 2025 (the Monday
after R1's Friday release). The investment thesis under attack:
"frontier capability requires $1B+ training runs only US/EU
hyperscalers can afford." DeepSeek's reported $5M training cost
(contested — independent estimates suggest $50M-$500M when including
research compute and infrastructure) made closed-model moats look
fragile. Reshaped narratives about US export controls (R1 was
trained on H800s, the export-controlled-but-still-allowed Nvidia
variant), Chinese AI capability, and the long-run economics of
LLM provider concentration.
public_view.why_it_matters:
R1 is the model that broke the "scale is the moat" investment
thesis in public. Whether the technical claims hold up to longer
scrutiny or not, the perception shift was permanent — every
frontier US lab now has to defend why their training spend is
justified given the existence of open competitors at much lower
reported cost.

citations.title: DeepSeek-R1: Incentivizing Reasoning Capability in LLMs via Reinforcement Learning

nodes.body_md:
## R1 vs o1 — the comparison that moved markets

| Dimension | OpenAI o1 (2024-09) | DeepSeek R1 (2025-01) |
|---|---|---|
| Org | OpenAI (US, closed) | DeepSeek (China, open) |
| Architecture | undisclosed | 671B MoE / 37B active |
| Weights | not released | open (MIT license) |
| Training data | undisclosed | partial disclosure |
| Training compute (reported) | undisclosed (likely >100M GPU-hr) | 5.6M GPU-hr |
| AIME 2024 | 83.3% | 79.8% |
| MATH-500 | 96.4% | 97.3% |
| GPQA Diamond | 78.0% | 71.5% |
| API cost / 1M output | $60 | $2.19 |

The 27× cost difference at near-comparable benchmarks is the headline number that drove the market reaction.

## The Sputnik framing — and the pushback

Within 72 hours of release, R1 was the most-discussed AI release since GPT-4. The "Sputnik moment" framing dominated US tech press. Pushback emerged within weeks:

- The reported $5M training cost excluded research compute, ablations, and infrastructure — full-cost estimates landed in the $50M-$500M range.
- DeepSeek had likely distilled outputs from o1 / Claude / GPT-4 in pretraining (denied by DeepSeek; widely suspected based on sample analysis).
- The benchmark performance had gaps vs o1 on several axes (GPQA, real-world coding) that the headline "matches o1" didn't capture.
- The H800 GPUs used were a downgraded export-compliant variant; H100 access would have changed the picture.

By Q2 2025 the consensus had moderated: R1 was a real and significant achievement, but the framing as "China matched US frontier at 1% the cost" had been substantially walked back.

## Permanent effects

Regardless of the contested specifics, R1 made permanent the following:

- **Open-weight reasoning is a category** — Qwen QwQ, Sky-T1, Mistral Magistral, all followed within months.
- **US export-control debate intensified** — R1 became Exhibit A in arguments both for tighter chip export restrictions and against them (depending on whether you read it as "controls failed" or "controls slowed but didn't stop").
- **Closed-model pricing pressure** — OpenAI, Anthropic, Google all repriced reasoning tiers downward in February-April 2025.

nodes.title: Grok 3 — xAI's Frontier Bet

model_spec.architecture: Decoder-only transformer + extended-thinking mode + native tool use
model_spec.training_compute: ~200k H100 GPUs (Colossus cluster, the largest single-cluster training run publicly disclosed at release)

feels_like: First xAI model trained on the Colossus supercluster — 10x compute of Grok-2
AIME 2025 93.3% and GPQA 84.6% — credibly frontier-tier on math and graduate science
Chatbot Arena Elo 1402 at release — not a benchmark stunt, real human-preference signal
TechCrunch flagged that the released graphs omitted o3-mini-high's cons@64 score, sparking benchmark trust debate
Trained with reinforcement learning at unprecedented scale to refine chain-of-thought
Most useful inside the X (Twitter) product — outside that surface the alternatives feel more polished
best_for: Research and scientific Q&A where chain-of-thought matters
Real-time information retrieval inside the X platform with full timeline access
Math and competition-style coding (LiveCodeBench 79.4%)
Users who want a less-restricted model than ChatGPT/Claude defaults
not_ideal_for: Coding-agent leaderboards — Claude Opus 4.5 and DeepSeek V4 score higher on SWE-bench
Workflows where benchmark transparency matters — release controversy is a real concern
Multimodal-first applications — Grok 3 lags on image tasks vs Gemini 2.5 Pro / GPT-5

sources.name: TechCrunch — Did xAI lie about Grok 3 benchmarks?

public_view.plain_english:
xAI's third-generation flagship model, released February 2025
after a sub-12-month build of the largest known single-site
GPU training cluster (Colossus, 200,000 H100s in Memphis).
Approached or matched GPT-4o / Claude 3.7 / Gemini 2.5 Pro
on benchmarks at launch and was integrated into X (Twitter)
as the default conversational AI for X Premium subscribers.
public_view.analogy:
The "Tesla strategy" of AI — sheer compute scale + vertical
integration (own chips eventually, own data center, own
distribution platform via X). Whether it scales to a
durable competitive position is the open question; the
strategic willingness to deploy capital is not in doubt.
public_view.investment_angle:
xAI's $10B March 2025 fundraise (at >$50B valuation)
was substantially underwritten by the Grok 3 release
demonstrating that xAI's compute-heavy strategy could
produce frontier-comparable models. The Colossus cluster
itself — built in ~122 days from the first ground broken
— became a case study in AI infrastructure deployment
speed and a recruitment tool. xAI's distribution moat
via X (~600M MAU) is the most direct consumer
distribution any frontier-model lab has access to,
though monetization conversion remains contested. The
"Memphis cluster" also sparked political controversy
around environmental impact (powered by ~30 mobile gas
turbines, drawing local-government scrutiny).
public_view.why_it_matters:
Grok 3 demonstrated that a startup founded in 2023 could
reach frontier capability in under 24 months given
sufficient capital and infrastructure willingness. The
competitive implications: the frontier-model market
likely sustains 4-6 well-funded labs in 2026-2030
rather than consolidating to 2-3. Whether xAI's
specific positioning as the "most pro-free-speech AI"
converts to durable customer preference remains to be
seen.

citations.title: Grok 3 Beta — The Age of Reasoning Agents

nodes.body_md:
## Grok 3 vs early-2025 frontier

| Model | Date | Org | Reasoning | Cluster scale | Distribution |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-4o | 2024-05 | OpenAI | none (then) | undisclosed | ChatGPT, Apple |
| Claude 3.7 Sonnet | 2025-02 | Anthropic | extended-thinking | undisclosed | claude.ai, AWS |
| Gemini 2.5 Pro | 2025-03 | Google | integrated | TPU v5/v6 fleet | Google products |
| **Grok 3** | **2025-02** | **xAI** | **Think mode** | **200k H100 (Colossus)** | **X / Twitter** |
| DeepSeek R1 | 2025-01 | DeepSeek | dedicated | undisclosed (smaller) | API + open |
| o3 | 2025-04 | OpenAI | dedicated | undisclosed | API |

Grok 3's benchmark performance was competitive but not best-in-class on most measures. The strategic story was the speed-of-build and the distribution-via-X integration, not the per-benchmark numbers.

## The Colossus cluster as a competitive primitive

xAI's choice to build (rather than rent) the world's largest known training cluster was an unusual strategic bet:

- **Capital intensity**: ~$3-5B in GPU + power + facility capital
- **Speed advantage**: avoided multi-year cloud-vendor procurement queues
- **Optionality**: cluster usable for many future training runs without competing with other tenants
- **Risk**: hardware obsolescence over the depreciation cycle

The bet has implications beyond xAI. Anthropic, Google, OpenAI, and Meta have all expanded their owned-or-controlled compute substantially in 2024-26 partly in response. The "compute moat" thesis that DeepSeek R1's January 2025 release had partially attacked is not dead — it's just moved up a tier.

public_view.plain_english:
Arc Institute's Evo 2 — a 40-billion-parameter foundation model
trained on 9.3 trillion nucleotides of DNA across all domains of
life, with a million-base-pair context window. Predicts variant
effects, generates novel functional sequences, and is the first
DNA model at the scale of language frontier models. Published in
Nature (March 2026).
public_view.analogy:
GPT for DNA. Like a language model that learned every biological
sequence ever published — and can write new ones that fold,
function, and survive selection.
public_view.investment_angle:
Arc Institute is the new model for non-academic / non-corporate
biology research — billionaire-funded (Patrick Collison, Stripe;
Silvio Micali; Vitalik Buterin), open-output. Evo 2 + State +
Stack is their bet that biological foundation models will
rewrite drug discovery economics in 5-10 years.
public_view.why_it_matters:
First million-base-pair DNA model — long enough to capture
full bacterial genomes in context. Combined with AlphaFold 3,
ESM-3, and Boltz, defines the AI-for-biology foundation layer
that all downstream therapeutic / agricultural / synbio work
builds on.

citations.title: Genome modeling and design across all domains of life with Evo 2

nodes.body_md:
## What "9.3T nucleotides" means

For comparison: a single human genome is ~3 billion base pairs. Evo 2's training corpus is ~3,000 human-genome equivalents drawn from all branches of life — bacteria, archaea, viruses, eukaryotes. The 40B-parameter capacity at 1M context lets the model reason about whole bacterial genomes (typically under 1Mb) end-to-end.

Figure's vision-language-action model for the Figure 02 and 03
humanoid robots. Helix uses a two-system architecture — System 1
handles fast motor control at 200Hz, System 2 reasons about
longer-horizon goals. Released February 2025, with Helix 02

public_view.analogy:
Daniel Kahneman's "Thinking Fast and Slow" implemented in a
robot — one fast reflexive layer, one slow deliberate layer,
working together.
public_view.investment_angle:
Figure raised $675M at $2.6B valuation in early 2024, then
$1.5B+ at $39.5B valuation early 2025 on the Helix + Figure 03
home-humanoid roadmap. The bet: if humanoids work, the VLA model
+ hardware integration moat is enormous. Closed-stack approach
contrasts with Physical Intelligence's open one.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Demonstrated fast-loop (200Hz) VLA control — a regime that
cloud-hosted models like Gemini Robotics can't match for
latency. Established the two-system control pattern that other
humanoid teams have begun adopting.

nodes.title: Claude 3.7 Sonnet — Hybrid Reasoning Model

feels_like: First hybrid-reasoning model — same checkpoint switches between fast standard mode and extended thinking
70.3% on SWE-bench Verified with custom scaffold — industry-leading at release; jumped from 62.3% baseline
Output token limit jumped 8x to 64K (128K beta) — made long-form code/docs viable in one response
Anthropic showed the chain-of-thought to users — competitor o1 hides it
Same price as 3.5 Sonnet ($3 in / $15 out, includes thinking tokens) — extended thinking is essentially free
Bug-find-and-fix loops finally feel reliable: read report → navigate codebase → root cause → working patch
best_for: Agentic coding with Claude Code CLI — the model designed for this surface
Math proofs, multi-step logic, and scientific analysis where extended thinking pays off
Long-form code generation, technical writing, or analysis (up to 128K output)
Teams that want one model toggling between fast and slow modes via prompt
not_ideal_for: Casual chat or copy that doesn't need reasoning — extended thinking adds latency without benefit
Hard reasoning leaderboards by late 2025 — Claude 4 / Opus 4.5 have moved well past it
Workloads needing 1M context (3.7 Sonnet ships 200K)

sources.name: Anthropic — Claude 3.7 Sonnet and Claude Code

public_view.plain_english:
The first Claude model that can switch between fast standard
responses and slow "extended thinking" mode in a single product —
rather than offering two separate models like OpenAI's GPT-4o vs
o-series split. Achieved state-of-the-art on real software
engineering benchmarks (SWE-bench Verified) and powered Anthropic's
first agentic IDE feature, Claude Code, released the same week.
public_view.analogy:
A car with both city and highway modes built into the same
vehicle — drivers don't have to switch cars to get the right
behavior for the road. OpenAI's approach was a sedan and a
sports car; Anthropic's was a hybrid you toggle.
public_view.investment_angle:
Claude 3.7 Sonnet's SWE-bench Verified score (70.3%) was a 21-point
leap over Claude 3.5 Sonnet on real-world software engineering
tasks — the first model where "have it write the patch and merge"
became a credible production workflow. Drove a wave of agentic
developer tools to product-market fit (Cursor's $9B valuation in
April 2025, Devin's $4B valuation, Cognition's growth).
Anthropic's Q1 2025 ARR reportedly crossed $1B annualized largely
on developer-tool API revenue tied to this model.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Claude 3.7 Sonnet was the first model where "AI writes meaningful
portions of production codebases autonomously" stopped being a
demo and started being a measurable share of real engineering
work. The SWE-bench Verified leaderboard from Q1 2025 onward
reads like an industry transition.

nodes.body_md:
## Hybrid vs separate-model approach

| Approach | OpenAI | Anthropic |
|---|---|---|
| Standard model | GPT-4o | Claude 3.7 Sonnet (default) |
| Reasoning model | o1, o1-pro, o3 | Claude 3.7 Sonnet (extended-thinking mode) |
| Switch granularity | per-API-endpoint | per-request flag |
| Reasoning trace | hidden (summary only) | fully visible |
| Pricing | reasoning ~5-10× more | reasoning ~3-5× more (token-based) |

The hybrid approach has UX advantages (one model abstraction for developers) but architectural costs (the same weights have to handle both modes well). The empirical evidence as of mid-2025 is that hybrid can compete on quality but with somewhat higher inference cost in standard mode.

## SWE-bench Verified — the headline benchmark

SWE-bench Verified is a curated subset of real GitHub issues from popular Python repos; the model must produce a code patch that passes the project's test suite.

| Model | Date | SWE-bench Verified |
|---|---|---|
| GPT-4 (Mar 2023) | 2023-03 | 1.7% |
| Claude 3 Opus | 2024-03 | 11.7% |
| GPT-4o | 2024-05 | 33.2% (with agent scaffolding) |
| Claude 3.5 Sonnet | 2024-06 | 49.0% |
| Claude 3.5 Sonnet (Oct update) | 2024-10 | 53.1% |
| o3-mini | 2025-01 | 49.3% |
| **Claude 3.7 Sonnet (extended)** | **2025-02** | **70.3%** |
| Claude 4 Sonnet | 2025-05 | 73.6% |
| Claude 4 Opus | 2025-05 | 79.4% |

The SWE-bench trajectory is the cleanest single-benchmark story of LLM progress 2023-25 — a 41× improvement in two years on a task that maps to real economic value.

## Claude Code release

Anthropic shipped Claude Code (a CLI agentic coding tool, eponymous with this codebase's likely tooling reader) the same week as Claude 3.7 Sonnet. It used the model in extended-thinking mode by default and demonstrated multi-step plan→edit→test loops that would consume 50,000+ tokens per task. The economic message: at $0.003/1k input, a Claude Code session might cost $5-50 depending on task scope — vs the engineering hour it replaces.

Audio synthesis / transcription tasks per the model card

public_view.plain_english:
DeepMind's vision-language-action (VLA) family — Gemini Robotics,
Robotics-ER, On-Device, and Robotics 1.5/ER 1.5 (Sept 2025).
Same Gemini multimodal brain, fine-tuned to output robot motor
commands instead of text. Deployed on Boston Dynamics Atlas,
Apptronik Apollo, and partner humanoid platforms with multi-
embodiment Motion Transfer.
public_view.analogy:
Gemini learned to talk and see. Gemini Robotics learned to
move — same brain, different output channel (motor commands
instead of words).
public_view.investment_angle:
DeepMind's bet that the AI moat extends from chat → code →
physical action. Gemini Robotics is the platform play — Google
provides the brain, hardware partners (Apptronik, Boston Dynamics,
Agility) provide the body. Mirrors Android's OEM model. Apptronik
raised $5B with this thesis.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Established VLA as a real product category, not just research.
Combined with Physical Intelligence π series, NVIDIA GR00T, and
Figure Helix, embodied AI is at the same "early commercialization"
point that LLMs were in 2022.

citations.title: Gemini Robotics: Bringing AI into the Physical World

nodes.body_md:
## VLA frontier (2025-2026)

| Model | Org | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Gemini Robotics 1.5 / ER 1.5 | Google DeepMind | Multi-embodiment Motion Transfer |
| π₀ / π₀.₅ / π*0.6 | Physical Intelligence | Open via openpi |
| Figure Helix / Helix 02 | Figure AI | On Figure 02/03 humanoids |
| NVIDIA GR00T N1 / N1.5 / N1.7 | NVIDIA | Open Apache-2.0 ecosystem |
| OpenVLA / OFT | Stanford / Berkeley | MIT-licensed academic |

nodes.title: Gemma 3 — Google's Open-Weight Family

feels_like: Four sizes (1B / 4B / 12B / 27B) — covers laptop-class up to single-GPU server inference
Gemma-3-4B-IT beats Gemma-2-27B-IT; Gemma-3-27B-IT beats Gemini 1.5 Pro on shared benchmarks
Vision input from 4B up; 1B is text-only — first Gemma family with multimodal at small sizes
128K context window across all sizes; 140+ language coverage
Interleaves 5 local-attention layers per global layer — saves memory at long context
Distillation + RL + model merging in post-training delivered noticeable math/coding/instruction gains
best_for: Single-GPU production inference (27B fits H100 / A100 80GB at FP precision)
Laptop / phone deployment via the 1B / 4B variants
Multilingual apps in 140 languages at open-weights pricing
Vision + text workflows that don't justify a frontier API call
not_ideal_for: Frontier reasoning leaderboards — Gemini 2.5 / 3 Pro and competitors lead
Workloads needing 1M+ context (Gemma 3 caps at 128K)
Audio or video input (Gemma 3 base is text + image)

public_view.plain_english:
Google DeepMind's March 2025 open-weight family, sized 1B / 4B /
12B / 27B. Gemma 3 added vision input (from 4B up), 128K context,
and 140-language coverage — making it the most capable single-GPU
open model from a major US lab at release.
public_view.analogy:
Gemini's open-source little sibling. Same architectural DNA,
runnable on consumer GPUs.
public_view.investment_angle:
Gemma is Google's defensive open-weight play against Llama and
Qwen — important for Vertex AI customers who want hosted Google
weights without being locked into closed Gemini. The 27B variant
became the de facto default for "Google-aligned open LLM" in
enterprise procurement through 2025.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Made Google a real player in the open-weight tier — until Gemma 3,
Llama and Qwen had owned that space outright. The vision support
and 128K context closed key feature gaps with Llama 3.2 Vision and
Qwen 2.5-VL.

nodes.body_md:
## Family

| Size | Vision | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 1B | text only | Edge / mobile |
| 4B | yes | Workstation |
| 12B | yes | Single-GPU prod |
| 27B | yes | Frontier-of-open |

All under the custom Gemma license — restricts certain uses but permits commercial deployment.

See the model spec and sources block for benchmarked use cases
